description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return an index of a maximal element. Assume the array length is positive. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array, and the element at that index must be greater than or equal to every element in the array. If multiple elements share the maximal value, returning any one of their indices is acceptable. The algorithm must terminate for all valid inputs.
fn max(a: &Vec<i32>) -> usize

Given an array of mathematical integers with length at least 2, return an integer representing an upper bound on the difference between any two elements of the array. Formally, let the result be diff. For every pair of valid indices i and j (0 <= i, j < length), the value at index i minus the value at index j must be less than or equal to diff. There is no ordering constraint between i and j. Intuitively, this corresponds to the maximum possible difference between any two elements in the array (i.e., the difference between the maximum and minimum elements).
fn MaxDifference(a: Vec<int>) -> (diff: int)

Given an array of integers and a non-negative rotation offset, produce a new array that is the result of rotating the input array to the left by the given offset. The output must have the same length as the input. For each index i in the range [0, n), where n is the input array length, the element at position i in the output must equal the element at index (i + offset) modulo n in the input. Assume the array length and the offset are strictly less than 1,000,000 and also strictly less than half of usize::MAX to avoid arithmetic overflow during index computations. The function must correctly handle empty arrays (the result should also be empty).
fn rotate(a: Vec<int>, offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)
function_signature: fn rotate(a: Vec<int>, offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)

description:
Given two arrays of 64-bit unsigned integers, determine whether they share at least one common value. Assume the following:
- The second array is sorted in non-decreasing (ascending) order; duplicates are allowed.
- All elements in both arrays are non-negative (this is inherent for unsigned integers).
The function must return true if and only if there exists an index pair (i, j) such that the i-th element of the first array equals the j-th element of the second array. If the function returns false, it must be guaranteed that no such pair exists. Arrays may be empty.
fn tangent(r: Vec<u64>, x: Vec<u64>) -> (b: bool)
function_signature: fn tangent(r: Vec<u64>, x: Vec<u64>) -> (b: bool)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return an index of a maximum element. Assume the input size is positive. The output index must be within the bounds of the array. For every valid position in the array, the value at the returned index is greater than or equal to the value at that position. If multiple elements share the maximum value, returning any one of their indices is acceptable.
fn find_max(a: &[i32]) -> (max_idx: usize)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether they share at least one common element. Arrays may be empty. Return true if and only if there exists an index within the bounds of the first array and an index within the bounds of the second array such that the elements at those indices are equal. Otherwise, return false, which implies that for all index pairs within bounds across both arrays, the elements are unequal.
fn has_common_element(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)
function_signature: fn has_common_element(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given an array of integers and a positive integer n, compute the maximum among the first n elements of the array. Assume that n is strictly greater than 0, n does not exceed the length of the array, and n is strictly less than 1000. The result must be an element taken from within the first n positions, and every element in that prefix must be less than or equal to the returned value. Elements beyond the first n positions are irrelevant to the computation.
fn max(a: &Vec<int>, n: usize) -> (max: int)

Given an array of mathematical integers with even length, return a new array formed by summing each consecutive pair of elements in the input. Assume the input length is even. The output array must have length exactly half of the input length, and for every valid index i in the output, the element at position i equals the sum of the two input elements at positions 2·i and 2·i+1.
fn pairwise_addition(a: Vec<int>) -> (result: Vec<int>)

Implement a function that, given an array of 32-bit signed integers, returns the maximum value. The array may be empty. The function must satisfy the following properties:
- If the array is empty, return 0.
- For any array (empty or not), the returned value is greater than or equal to every element in the array.
- If the array is non-empty, the returned value must be equal to one of the elements of the array (i.e., it is the maximum element that appears in the array).
Assume standard 0-based indexing and that the input array is a valid vector.
fn max(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (max: i32)

Implement a function that returns the square of an unsigned 64-bit integer. Assume the input is a natural number less than 1,000,000,000. Additionally, assume that the square of the input is strictly less than the maximum value representable by a 64-bit unsigned integer. The function must return a value equal to the mathematical square of the input.
fn carre(a: u64) -> (c: u64)

Given an array of 32-bit integers, return the index of the first element that is equal to zero. If the array contains no zero, return the array’s length. Use zero-based indexing, and allow the array length to be zero. The returned index must satisfy the following properties: it lies within [0, n] where n is the array length; every position strictly before the returned index contains a non-zero value; and if the returned index is strictly less than n, then the element at that index is zero.
fn mfirst_cero(v: &[i32]) -> (i: usize)

Given a vector of mathematical integers, return a new vector such that: (1) the output vector has the same length as the input, and (2) for every valid index i, the element at index i in the output equals the square of the element at index i in the input. There are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn square_elements(a: Vec<int>) -> (squared: Vec<int>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, construct and return a new array that is the concatenation of the first array followed by the second array. The inputs may be empty and there are no additional constraints. The returned array must satisfy all of the following properties: its length equals the sum of the lengths of the two inputs; for every valid index i in the first array, the element at index i in the result equals the element at index i of the first array; and for every valid index i in the second array, the element at index len(first) + i in the result equals the element at index i of the second array. The implementation must be proven to maintain these properties for all inputs and to terminate.
fn append_array(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (c: Vec<i32>)
function_signature: fn append_array(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (c: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers, modify it in place so that every negative element is replaced with 0 while all non-negative elements remain unchanged. No preconditions are imposed on the input values or the array length. The operation must not change the array length. Formally, for every valid index, the element in the post-state equals 0 if the corresponding element in the pre-state was negative; otherwise it equals the corresponding pre-state element.
fn zap_negatives(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the array contains only one distinct value. Return true if and only if either the array is empty or every element in the array is equal to every other element. The result must satisfy the following guarantees: if the function returns true, then for any pair of valid indices within the array bounds, the elements at those indices are equal; if the function returns false, then there exist two valid indices within the array bounds such that the elements at those indices are not equal.
fn has_only_one_distinct_element(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

function_signature:
spec fn max(x: u64, y: u64) -> u64
fn slow_max(a: u64, b: u64) -> (z: u64)

description:
Implement a function that takes two 32-bit signed integers: a positive iteration count and a starting value that is strictly greater than the iteration count. The function performs exactly that many decrements by one on the starting value and returns the final result. Assume the iteration count is positive and the starting value is strictly larger than the iteration count. The returned value must be non-negative and must equal the starting value minus the iteration count. Additionally, at any intermediate step t where 0 <= t <= the iteration count, the current value equals the starting value minus t, and the step counter remains within the range [0, iteration count]. The process must be provably terminating, with the number of remaining steps strictly decreasing on each iteration.
fn main_func(n: i32, k: i32) -> (k_out: i32)
function_signature: fn main_func(n: i32, k: i32) -> (k_out: i32)

description:
Given a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers and a 32-bit signed integer threshold, modify the array in place by replacing every element that is strictly greater than the threshold with -1. Elements that are less than or equal to the threshold must remain unchanged. Assume the array may be empty. After the function returns, for every valid index in the array, if the original value at that index was greater than the threshold, then the new value at that index must be -1; otherwise, the value at that index must be identical to its original value. The length and relative order of positions are preserved; only values strictly greater than the threshold are altered to -1.
fn replace(arr: &mut Vec<i32>, k: i32)

Given a non-negative integer input representing a desired size, construct and return a dynamic array of unsigned integers of that length containing exactly the sequence 0, 1, 2, ..., size−1. The result must satisfy the following conditions: its length equals the input size; the array is non-decreasing, meaning for any two indices j and k with 0 ≤ j ≤ k < size, the element at position j is less than or equal to the element at position k; and for every index j with 1 ≤ j < size, the element at position j is non-negative. The function should also handle the boundary case when the input size is 0 by returning an empty array.
fn array_up_to_n(n: usize) -> Vec<usize>

Given an integer input value and an array of integers, determine whether the input value is strictly greater than every element in the array. The function should return true if the input value is greater than all elements; otherwise, it should return false. There are no preconditions on the array (it may be empty and need not be sorted) or on the integer value. The function must satisfy the following postconditions: (1) If it returns true, then for all valid indices of the array, the input value is strictly greater than the corresponding element. (2) If it returns false, then there exists at least one valid index in the array whose element is greater than or equal to the input value. Empty arrays are allowed, and in that case the function should return true.
fn is_greater(n: i32, a: Vec<i32>) -> bool
function_signature: fn is_greater(n: i32, a: Vec<i32>) -> bool

description:
Given an array of 32-bit integers, implement a function that reverses the array in place. After the function returns, for every valid index i in the range [0, n), the element at position i must equal the element that was originally at position n − 1 − i, where n is the length of the array. The function must work for any array length, including zero and one.
fn invert_array(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Implement a function that takes a mutable array (vector) of 32-bit integers and reverses its contents in place. After the function returns, for every index i such that 0 <= i < n (where n is the length of the array at the time of the call), the element at position i must equal the element that was originally at position n - 1 - i. There are no preconditions on the input: the array may be empty or have any length. The function must terminate for any finite-length input.
fn reverse(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Given two integer arrays of equal length, return a new array where each element is the remainder of dividing the corresponding element of the first array by the corresponding element of the second array. Assume both input arrays have the same length. Assume every element in the second array is non-zero. The output array must have the same length as the inputs. For all valid indices i (0 <= i < length), the output at index i equals the modulo operation of the first array’s element by the second array’s element at that index. The function must handle the empty array case correctly, as the constraints still hold when the length is zero.
fn element_wise_modulo(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (result: Vec<int>)
function_signature: fn element_wise_modulo(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (result: Vec<int>)

description:
Given two arrays of integers of equal length, return a new array containing the element-wise sums. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. The output array must have the same length as the inputs, and for every valid index i (0 <= i < length), the output’s i-th element equals the sum of the i-th elements of the two inputs.
fn array_sum(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)
function_signature: fn array_sum(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)

description:
Given a byte slice representing an ASCII string, determine whether there exists a '.' character such that it is followed by exactly two characters and those two characters are the final two characters of the string (i.e., the '.' appears at position length − 3). Return true if and only if such a position exists; otherwise, return false. There are no assumptions about the content of the two trailing characters—they need not be digits. The input may be any finite byte slice, including empty.
fn is_decimal_with_two_precision(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that takes a 64-bit unsigned integer as input and returns that same value. Assume the input is non-negative (which holds for unsigned integers). The function should conceptually count from 0 up to the input value without exceeding it. The implementation must guarantee that the result equals the input. If iteration is used, the intermediate counter must always remain within the inclusive range from 0 to the input, and progress toward completion must strictly decrease to ensure termination.
fn count_to_and_return_n(n: u64) -> (r: u64)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum element in the array. Assume the input array length is strictly positive. The returned value must be at least as large as every element in the array, and it must be equal to one of the elements in the array (i.e., the maximum is attained at some index).
fn max_array(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (max: i32)

Compute and return the minimum element of a given non-empty array of integers. Assume the input array has at least one element. The returned value must be equal to one of the elements of the array and must be less than or equal to every element in the array.
fn minimum(a: Vec<int>) -> (m: int)

Given a non-empty array of integers, return the maximum element of the array. Assume the input size is at least 1. The returned value must be greater than or equal to every element in the array, and it must equal at least one element of the array.
fn max_array(a: Vec<int>) -> (m: int)

Given a non-empty array of integers, return the smallest element in the array. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The returned value must satisfy both of the following: it is less than or equal to every element of the array, and it is equal to at least one element of the array. If an iterative approach is used, the iteration must progress through the array and terminate, and at any point during the traversal over a prefix of the array, the current candidate must be less than or equal to all elements in that processed prefix and must be equal to one of the processed elements.
fn find_smallest(s: Vec<int>) -> (min: int)

Given a non-empty array of characters, return a new array that is the exact reverse of the input. Assume the input array length is strictly positive. The output must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the range [0, n), where n is the input length, the character at position i in the output must be equal to the character at position (n − i − 1) in the input.
fn reverse(a: Vec<char>) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return an index of a maximum element. Assume the input length is at least 1. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array, and the value at that index must be greater than or equal to every other element in the array. If multiple positions hold the maximum value, returning any one of those indices is acceptable.
fn find_max(a: &[i32]) -> (i: usize)

Given a read-only array of bytes representing a string, determine whether all characters in the array are identical.

Return true if and only if every pair of positions within the array holds the same byte value. Edge cases are defined as follows: an empty array and a single-element array are considered to have all identical characters and should return true.

Correctness requirements:
- If the function returns true, then for any two indices i and j within bounds (0 <= i < length and 0 <= j < length), the elements at those indices are equal.
- If the function returns false, then the length of the array is at least 2, and there exist two distinct indices i and j within bounds such that the elements at those indices are different.

There are no additional preconditions on the input; any finite byte array is allowed.
fn all_characters_same(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers, return a new array that contains all elements of the input except the first one, preserving their original order. Assume the input length is strictly greater than zero. The result must be exactly the subsequence of the input consisting of elements at indices from 1 up to the last index (i.e., the input without its first element). Equivalently, the returned array must have length equal to the input length minus one, and for every valid position k in the result, its element must equal the input element at position k+1.
fn remove_front(a: Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Given a non-empty array of characters, return a new array that contains the characters of the input in reverse order. Assume the input size is at least 1. The output must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the range [0, length of input), the element at position i of the output must equal the element at position (length of input − i − 1) of the input. The input is not modified; a new array is produced that represents the reversal.
fn reverse(a: Vec<char>) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Given an array of 64-bit unsigned integers, return a 64-bit signed integer defined as follows:
- If the array is empty, return -1.
- Otherwise, consider each element converted to a 64-bit signed integer; return the maximum value under signed 64-bit ordering. The returned value must be at least as large (in signed comparison) as every converted element and must be equal to the signed 64-bit conversion of some element in the array.
No additional assumptions are required; the array length may be zero.
fn max(a: Vec<u64>) -> i64

Given an array of 32-bit integers and a target value, return the index of the first occurrence of the target in the array. If the target does not appear, return the array’s length. Assume the array length is strictly positive. The returned index must be within the inclusive range from 0 to the array length. If the returned index is strictly less than the array length, then the element at that position equals the target, and all positions before it do not equal the target (i.e., it is the first occurrence). The search must be well-founded and terminate.
fn find(a: Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (index: usize)
function_signature: fn find(a: Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (index: usize)

description:
Given a non-empty slice of characters, return a new vector containing the same characters in reverse order. Assume the input length n is strictly positive. The output must have the same length as the input. For every valid index k with 0 <= k < n, the element at position k in the result must be equal to the element at position n - 1 - k in the input.
fn reverse(a: &[char]) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the index of a maximum element. Assume the input size is positive. The returned index must be within the valid range of the array (0-based), and the element at that index must be greater than or equal to every other element in the array. If multiple elements share the maximum value, returning the index of any one of them is acceptable.
fn find_max(a: &[i32]) -> (i: usize)

Given a non-empty array of mathematical integers, return the minimum element of the array. Assume the input size is positive (the array contains at least one element). The returned value must be less than or equal to every element in the array and must be equal to one of the elements of the array.
fn min_array(a: Vec<int>) -> (r: int)

Implement a function that takes two arrays of integers and returns a new array that is the concatenation of the first input array followed by the second input array. The function must work for any input arrays, including empty arrays. The output array must satisfy the following properties: its length equals the sum of the lengths of the two input arrays; for every index i within the bounds of the first input array, the element at position i in the output equals the element at position i of the first input; for every index j within the bounds of the second input array, the element at position j plus the length of the first input in the output equals the element at position j of the second input. Indices are 0-based and all referenced indices are guaranteed to be in range as specified.
fn concat(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)
function_signature: fn concat(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)

description:
You are given an array of integers and a single integer value. Implement a function that returns a new array consisting of all elements of the input array followed by the given value appended at the end. The input array length may be zero or greater.

The function must satisfy the following properties:
- The resulting array has length exactly one greater than the input array length.
- For every index within the bounds of the original array, the element at that index in the result equals the corresponding element in the input.
- The last element of the result equals the given integer value.
- Conceptually, the result is the concatenation of the original array with a one-element array containing the given value (i.e., the result represents the original sequence followed by that value).
fn append(a: Vec<int>, b: int) -> (c: Vec<int>)
function_signature: fn append(a: Vec<int>, b: int) -> (c: Vec<int>)

description:
Given an array of 32-bit integers and a positive integer n such that n is at most the length of the array, compute the minimum value among the first n elements of the array. The returned value must be equal to one of the elements within the first n positions, and it must be less than or equal to every element within those n positions.

Assumptions:
- The array length is at least n.
- The value n is strictly positive.

Output requirements:
- The result equals some element among the first n elements of the array.
- No element within the first n positions is strictly less than the result.
fn min(a: &Vec<i32>, n: usize) -> i32

Given a read-only array of characters, return the index of the first occurrence of the character 'e'. If 'e' does not appear in the array, return -1. Assume the array length is strictly less than the maximum value of a 32-bit signed integer. If the result is not -1, it must be a valid index within the array bounds, the character at that index must be 'e', and all positions before that index must not contain 'e'.
fn first_e(a: &[char]) -> i32

Implement a function that takes a vector of mathematical integers and returns a new vector of the same length where each output element is the cube of the corresponding input element. There are no preconditions on the input; the function must be total and terminate for all inputs. The result must preserve the input length, and for every valid index i in the range [0, n), the ith element of the output equals the cube of the ith element of the input.
fn cube_elements(a: Vec<int>) -> (cubed: Vec<int>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers, return the index of the first occurrence of the minimum value in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array. The element at the returned index must be less than or equal to every element in the array. Additionally, for any position strictly before the returned index, the element there must be strictly greater than the element at the returned index. This guarantees that the index corresponds to the first occurrence of the global minimum.
fn getmini(a: &Vec<i32>) -> usize

function_signature:
spec fn abs(x: int) -> int
fn absx(x: Vec<i32>) -> (y: Vec<i32>)

spec fn abs(a: int) -> int
fn aba(a: Vec<i32>) -> (b: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a target integer, implement a function that performs a linear search from the beginning of the array to locate the first occurrence of the target. The function must return the index of the first occurrence if it exists; otherwise, it must return the length of the array. There are no preconditions on the input array (it may be empty, contain duplicates, or be unsorted).

The result must satisfy the following properties:
- The returned index is within the range [0, array length].
- If the returned index equals the array length, then the target does not appear anywhere in the array.
- If the returned index is strictly less than the array length, then the element at that index equals the target, and the target does not appear at any earlier index. In other words, the returned index is the first position of the target in the array.
fn linear_search(a: &Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> (n: usize)

Given a collection of integer sequences, return a new sequence containing the first element from each inner sequence. Assume the outer collection may be empty, but every inner sequence in the collection is non-empty. The returned sequence must have the same length as the outer collection, and for every valid index, the element at that index in the output equals the first element of the corresponding inner sequence in the input.
fn get_first_elements(lst: Vec<Vec<int>>) -> (result: Vec<int>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers, return the maximum value present in the array. Assume the input array length is strictly positive. The returned value must be equal to some element of the array, and it must be greater than or equal to every element in the array. Indices are considered over the valid range from 0 (inclusive) to the array length (exclusive).
fn find_max(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (max: i32)

Given an array of 32-bit integers and a target value, return the index of the first occurrence of the target in the array. If the target does not appear in the array, return -1. Assume the array length is strictly less than 2^31 so that any valid index fits in a 32-bit signed integer. The result must satisfy the following: if a nonnegative index i is returned, then 0 <= i < n and the element at position i equals the target; if a negative value is returned, then the target does not occur at any position in the array.
fn find(blood: Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (index: i32)
function_signature: fn find(blood: Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (index: i32)

description:
Given an array of 64-bit signed integers and an integer threshold, determine whether there exist two distinct indices i and j (with i < j) such that the absolute difference between the corresponding elements is strictly less than the threshold. The array may be empty or of any length. The threshold may be any 64-bit signed integer. If the threshold is non-positive, the answer must be false because absolute differences are non-negative. The comparison of differences should be understood over mathematical integers (i.e., the result must be correct even for extreme input values). Return true if such a pair exists; otherwise, return false.
fn has_close_elements(numbers: &[i64], threshold: i64) -> bool

Given an array of unsigned 64-bit integers and an unsigned 64-bit delimiter value, construct a new array by inserting the delimiter between every pair of consecutive elements of the input. Assume the input array length can be zero or positive. The resulting array must satisfy the following properties: (1) If the input length is 0, the output length is 0. (2) If the input length is 1, the output is exactly the input. (3) If the input length is n ≥ 2, then the output length is 2n − 1, and for all indices i in [0, n), the element at even position 2i of the output equals the i-th element of the input; for all indices i in [0, n − 1), the element at odd position 2i + 1 of the output equals the delimiter. The output must be functionally equivalent to the recursive specification that, for inputs of length at most 1, returns the input unchanged; otherwise, it returns the result of applying the same operation to the input without its last element, followed by the delimiter and the last element.
pub open spec fn intersperse_spec(numbers: Seq<u64>, delimiter: u64) -> Seq<u64>
pub fn intersperse(numbers: Vec<u64>, delimiter: u64) -> (result: Vec<u64>)
function_signature:
pub open spec fn intersperse_spec(numbers: Seq<u64>, delimiter: u64) -> Seq<u64>
pub fn intersperse(numbers: Vec<u64>, delimiter: u64) -> (result: Vec<u64>)

description:
Implement a function that processes an array of 32-bit unsigned integers and returns two results:
- The exact sum of all elements as a 64-bit unsigned integer.
- The product of all elements as an optional 32-bit unsigned integer.

Assume the array length is strictly less than 2^32 (i.e., less than u32::MAX). The returned sum must equal the mathematical sum of the array elements. The product is computed using standard 32-bit unsigned multiplication semantics: if the exact mathematical product of all elements fits within the range [0, u32::MAX], return it as Some(value); otherwise, return None to indicate that overflow occurs during sequential multiplication.

The overflow condition must be evidenced by the existence of some position i in the array (0 <= i < length) such that the product of the elements strictly before position i, multiplied by the element at position i, would exceed u32::MAX. When overflow does not occur, the returned product must equal the exact mathematical product of all elements. For the empty array, the sum is 0 and the product is Some(1).
fn sum_product(numbers: Vec<u32>) -> (u64, Option<u32>)

You are given a sequence of characters that may include any characters, including parentheses. Define the "parentheses-only" subsequence of the input as the subsequence obtained by removing all characters except '(' and ')'. Assume the parentheses-only subsequence forms a valid sequence of balanced groups, meaning: its overall net nesting level (where '(' increments and ')' decrements) is zero, and the net nesting level of every prefix is never negative.

Partition the parentheses-only subsequence into contiguous groups such that:
- Each group is non-empty.
- Each group consists only of '(' and ')'.
- Each group is balanced: its net nesting level is zero, and every non-empty strict prefix of the group has a positive net nesting level.

Return these groups in order as a list of character vectors. The concatenation of all groups in the result must equal the parentheses-only subsequence of the original input. Non-parenthesis characters in the input are ignored and do not appear in the output.

Assumptions:
- The parentheses-only subsequence of the input has an overall net nesting level of zero.
- The net nesting level of every non-empty prefix of the parentheses-only subsequence is non-negative.
fn separate_paren_groups(input: &Vec<char>) -> (groups: Vec<Vec<char>>)

function_signature:
spec fn seq_max(a: Seq<i32>) -> i32
fn rolling_max(numbers: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given two arrays of characters of equal length, where every element in both arrays is either the character '0' or the character '1', produce a new array of the same length representing the element-wise XOR of the two inputs using character semantics. For each index, the output character must be '0' if the two input characters at that index are equal, and '1' if they are different. Assume the input arrays have exactly the same length and that each element in both arrays is either '0' or '1'. The result must contain only '0' and '1' characters and have the same length as the inputs.
fn string_xor(a: &[char], b: &[char]) -> Vec<char>

Implement three functions that operate on non-negative integers and character sequences:

1) A function that converts a single decimal digit to its corresponding character. Assume the input is a single digit in the range 0 through 9. The output must be the character '0' through '9' matching the input value.

2) A function that converts a non-negative integer to the sequence of its decimal digits as characters, in left-to-right order, without leading zeros. For the value zero, return an empty sequence (i.e., do not return the character '0'). The input is a non-negative integer that fits within an 8-bit unsigned integer.

3) A function that constructs the character sequence representing the numbers from 0 up to a given non-negative integer, separated by single spaces. The resulting character sequence must be exactly: the character '0', followed by, for each positive integer i from 1 to the input value, one space ' ' and then the decimal digit characters of i as produced by the second function. For an input of zero, the result is just the single character '0'. The input is a non-negative integer that fits within an 8-bit unsigned integer. There must be no trailing space at the end of the sequence.

Correctness requirements:
- The single-digit conversion must only accept values 0 through 9.
- The integer-to-digit-sequence conversion must produce no characters for zero and otherwise produce the canonical decimal representation (no leading zeros) in left-to-right order.
- The 0-to-n sequence must begin with '0' and, for each subsequent number, contain a single leading space followed by its decimal digits. The sequence must be contiguous in increasing order from 0 to the input value.
fn single_digit_number_to_char_impl(n: u8) -> (output: char)
pub fn number_to_char_impl(n: u8) -> (char_vec: Vec<char>)
fn string_sequence_impl(n: u8) -> (string_seq: Vec<char>)

You are given two arrays of characters: a text and a pattern. Assume the pattern is non-empty. Define that the pattern occurs in the text at index i if the pattern matches the consecutive characters of the text starting at i (i.e., the pattern is a prefix of the suffix of the text beginning at i). Overlaps are allowed; for example, in the text "aaaa" the pattern "aa" occurs at indices 0, 1, and 2, for a total of 3 occurrences.

Task: Compute the total number of indices i such that the pattern occurs at i in the text. If the total number of occurrences fits within an unsigned 32-bit integer, return Some(count). If the count is larger than u32::MAX, return None.

Additional details and assumptions:
- The pattern length is at least 1. The text length may be zero.
- If the text length is smaller than the pattern length, the result is 0.
- The result counts overlapping occurrences. Formally, the count is the number of i with 0 <= i <= text_len - pattern_len such that for all j in 0..pattern_len, text[i + j] == pattern[j].
- Return Some(k) exactly when the true count equals k and k <= u32::MAX; return None exactly when the true count is greater than u32::MAX.
fn how_many_times_impl(string: Vec<char>, substring: Vec<char>) -> Option<u32>
fn is_prefix(substring: Vec<char>, string: Vec<char>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn how_many_times_impl(string: Vec<char>, substring: Vec<char>) -> Option<u32>
fn is_prefix(substring: Vec<char>, string: Vec<char>) -> bool

description:
Given a 32-bit unsigned integer greater than 1, compute its largest proper divisor. A proper divisor of a number n is a positive integer d such that 1 ≤ d < n and n mod d = 0 (equivalently, there exists a natural number k with d × k = n). The result must strictly divide the input, be strictly less than the input, and be at least as large as every other proper divisor of the input. If the input is prime (i.e., it has no proper divisor greater than 1), return 1. Assume the input satisfies n > 1.
fn largest_divisor(n: u32) -> (ret: u32)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether any prefix of the array has a strictly negative cumulative sum when starting from zero. Assume the following input constraint: for every prefix length i in the range 0 ≤ i ≤ n (where n is the array length), the cumulative sum of the first i elements, computed using mathematical integers (i.e., without machine overflow), is at most INT32_MAX. Return true if and only if there exists an index i with 0 ≤ i ≤ n such that the sum of the first i elements is less than 0; otherwise, return false. The empty prefix has sum 0 and therefore does not satisfy the condition.
fn below_zero(operations: Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

You are given two tasks on arrays of 64-bit integers.

Task 1: Implement a function that returns the number of occurrences of a given 64-bit integer in an input array. The input array may be empty. The result must equal the mathematical frequency of the target value in the array (the number of indices whose element equals the target). The returned count must be a non-negative integer and cannot exceed the length of the array.

Task 2: Implement a function that removes all elements that occur more than once in the input array. The input array may be empty. The function must return a new array consisting exactly of those elements whose overall frequency in the original array is 1, preserving their original relative order. Elements appearing more than once in the input must not appear in the output. No additional assumptions (e.g., sortedness) are made about the input.

Correctness requirements:
- For Task 1, the output must equal the frequency defined over the entire input array; equivalently, for any prefix of the array, the number of matches in that prefix is the count for that prefix, and the final count is the count for the full array.
- For Task 2, the output must be the result of filtering the input by the predicate “frequency equals 1” with respect to the original array, maintaining stability (original order) and never introducing or reordering elements.
pub open spec fn count_frequency_spec(seq: Seq<i64>, key: i64) -> int
fn count_frequency(elements: &Vec<i64>, key: i64) -> (frequency: usize)
fn remove_duplicates(numbers: &Vec<i64>) -> (unique_numbers: Vec<i64>)

Given a read-only array of characters, return a new array of the same length where each character’s ASCII letter case is flipped. Specifically:
- If an element is a lowercase ASCII letter ('a' to 'z'), the corresponding element in the result must be its uppercase counterpart.
- If an element is an uppercase ASCII letter ('A' to 'Z'), the corresponding element in the result must be its lowercase counterpart.
- Any character that is not an ASCII letter must remain unchanged in the result.

Assume the input may be empty. The output must have exactly the same length as the input. For every valid index i, the result at position i must equal the case-flipped version of the input character at position i according to the rules above.
fn flip_case(str: &[char]) -> Vec<char>

Given a finite list of character arrays, return a single character array formed by concatenating all inner arrays in order from left to right. If the input list is empty, the result must be an empty array. Empty inner arrays contribute no characters to the result. The result must be exactly the mathematical concatenation of the input, preserving the order of characters and the order of inner arrays. Formally, the returned sequence is equal to the concatenation of the sequences at indices 0, 1, ..., up to the last index of the input list. The procedure must terminate after processing all inner arrays.
fn concatenate_impl(strings: Vec<Vec<char>>) -> Vec<char>

Given a vector of 32-bit integers, produce a new vector that reorders only the elements at indices divisible by 3 (using zero-based indexing) so that those elements are in nondecreasing order, while all other positions remain unchanged. The output vector must have the same length as the input and must be a permutation of the input; that is, for every integer value, the number of occurrences in the output equals the number of occurrences in the input.

More precisely:
- Assume zero-based indexing. Indices divisible by 3 are 0, 3, 6, and so on.
- The output length equals the input length.
- For every index i where i mod 3 ≠ 0, the output value at position i is equal to the input value at position i (these positions are fixed).
- For all index pairs i and j with 0 ≤ i < j < n, both i and j divisible by 3, the output satisfies output[i] ≤ output[j]. Equivalently, the subsequence formed by the values at indices divisible by 3 is sorted in nondecreasing order.
- The output is a permutation of the input: for every integer x, count(output, x) = count(input, x).

No additional preconditions are required; empty and non-empty inputs are both allowed.
fn sort_third(l: Vec<i32>) -> (l_prime: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array obtained by incrementing each element by exactly 1. Assume the input may be empty. Assume further that for every valid index in the input, adding 1 to that element does not overflow a 32-bit signed integer (i.e., each element is at most i32::MAX - 1). The output must have the same length as the input, preserve the original order, and satisfy: for every index i within bounds, result[i] equals input[i] + 1.
fn incr_list(l: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Implement a function that computes the area of a triangle from two unsigned 64-bit integers representing the base length and the height. The result must be the integer value of base × height ÷ 2 (using truncating integer division). Assume both inputs are strictly positive. Additionally, assume that the mathematically computed value base × height ÷ 2 fits within the range of a 64-bit unsigned integer, so no overflow occurs. The function must return exactly base × height ÷ 2 under integer arithmetic.
fn triangle_area(a: u64, h: u64) -> (area: u64)
function_signature: fn triangle_area(a: u64, h: u64) -> (area: u64)

description:
Given an input string, determine whether it is a palindrome when interpreted as a sequence of Unicode characters (not bytes). Return true if and only if, for every integer index i with 0 <= i < n, the character at position i is equal to the character at position n - 1 - i, where n is the number of Unicode characters in the string. No normalization or filtering is applied: comparisons are exact and include spaces, punctuation, and any Unicode scalar values. The empty string and any single-character string are considered palindromes.
fn is_palindrome(text: &str) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether there exists a prefix of the array whose arithmetic sum is strictly negative. Formally, return true if and only if there exists an index i with 0 <= i <= n (where n is the length of the array) such that the sum of the first i elements, interpreted in unbounded mathematical integers (i.e., without 32-bit overflow), is less than 0; otherwise, return false. The array may be empty, in which case the answer is false.
fn below_zero(operations: Vec<i32>) -> bool

Given an array of characters, construct and return a new array containing exactly those characters from the input that are not vowels. Consider as vowels the five letters 'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u' and their uppercase forms 'A', 'E', 'I', 'O', 'U'. The relative order of the non-vowel characters must be preserved. The input may be empty. Formally, the result must equal the input sequence filtered by the predicate "character is not one of the specified vowels".
fn remove_vowels(input: &[char]) -> Vec<char>

Given a sequence of 32-bit signed integers and an integer threshold, determine whether every element of the sequence is strictly less than the threshold. Return true if and only if for all valid indices within the sequence, the element at that index is strictly less than the threshold. The sequence may be empty; in that case, the result is true.
fn below_threshold(l: &[i32], t: i32) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that returns the nth Fibonacci number using 32-bit unsigned integers, with precise overflow handling. The mathematical Fibonacci sequence is defined as F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and for n ≥ 2, F(n) = F(n − 1) + F(n − 2). The input is a non-negative integer n provided as a 32-bit unsigned value. The function must return Some(v) exactly when F(n) fits within a u32; in that case, v must equal the mathematical Fibonacci value F(n). Otherwise, the function must return None, and this must occur precisely when F(n) strictly exceeds u32::MAX. Assume the monotonicity of the Fibonacci sequence (if i ≤ j then F(i) ≤ F(j)), and use the known bounds that F(47) ≤ u32::MAX while F(48) > u32::MAX. Consequently, for any input n ≥ 48, the function must return None, and for 0 ≤ n ≤ 47, it must return Some(F(n)). The function must handle the base cases n = 0 and n = 1 by returning 0 and 1 respectively.
fn fib(n: u32) -> Option<u32>

Implement a verified Fibonacci computation with overflow detection. Define a mathematical specification of the Fibonacci sequence over natural numbers: the value at index 0 is 0, at index 1 is 1, and at any index k ≥ 2 it is the sum of the two preceding values. Prove that this specification is monotonic: for any natural numbers i and j where i ≤ j, the Fibonacci value at i is less than or equal to the value at j. Then implement an executable function that takes a non-negative 32-bit unsigned integer as input and returns either the exact Fibonacci value as a 32-bit unsigned integer or indicates that the value cannot be represented in 32 bits. The function must satisfy the following: it returns Some(v) if and only if the mathematical Fibonacci value at the input index equals v and v fits within the range of u32; it returns None if and only if the mathematical Fibonacci value at the input index exceeds u32::MAX. The function must terminate (using a decreasing measure on the input), handle the base cases for indices 0 and 1 directly, and for indices ≥ 2 compute the result as the sum of the results for the two preceding indices. Additionally, ensure that for inputs greater than or equal to a threshold beyond which the Fibonacci value exceeds u32::MAX, the function returns None, and for inputs below that threshold, summation does not overflow.
spec fn spec_fib(n: nat) -> nat
proof fn lemma_fib_monotonic(i: nat, j: nat)
fn fib(n: u32) -> Option<u32>
function_signature:
spec fn spec_fib(n: nat) -> nat
proof fn lemma_fib_monotonic(i: nat, j: nat)
fn fib(n: u32) -> Option<u32>

description:
Given a string, determine whether the sequence of angle brackets within it is correctly balanced. Treat only the characters '<' and '>' as bracket tokens; all other characters are ignored. The sequence is considered correct if and only if (1) the total number of '<' equals the total number of '>', and (2) for every prefix of the string, the number of '>' never exceeds the number of '<' (i.e., the running difference between '<' and '>' never becomes negative). Assume the input length can be represented as a 32-bit signed integer.
fn correct_bracketing(brackets: &str) -> bool

Given a finite array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the array is monotonic. Return true if and only if the array is either nondecreasing or nonincreasing across its entire length. Formally, the output must be true exactly when one of the following holds: for every pair of indices i and j with 0 <= i < j < length, the value at index i is less than or equal to the value at index j (nondecreasing), or for every such pair, the value at index i is greater than or equal to the value at index j (nonincreasing). Arrays of length 0 or 1 are considered monotonic.
fn monotonic(l: Vec<i32>) -> (ret: bool)

Implement two functions related to primality and prime factors under the following specifications.

1) Primality predicate:
- A number x is considered prime if it has no integer divisor in the range [2, x - 1]. Under this definition, 1 is treated as prime (it vacuously satisfies the condition).

2) Function A: Determine primality
- Input: a non-negative integer at least 2.
- Output: a boolean indicating whether the input is prime according to the predicate above.
- Correctness requirement: The result is true if and only if for all integers j with 2 <= j < input, input % j != 0.

3) Function B: Largest prime factor
- Input: a non-negative integer n at least 2.
- Output: an integer largest satisfying all of the following:
  - 1 <= largest <= n.
  - largest is prime according to the predicate defined above.
  - n is divisible by largest (n % largest == 0).
  - For every integer p with 0 <= p < n, if p is prime according to the predicate and p divides n, then p <= largest. In other words, largest dominates all prime divisors of n that are strictly less than n; when n itself is prime, returning n is permitted.

Assume all inputs satisfy the stated preconditions. The returned values must satisfy all postconditions listed.
fn is_prime(num: u32) -> (result: bool)
fn largest_prime_factor(n: u32) -> (largest: u32)
function_signature:
fn is_prime(num: u32) -> (result: bool)
fn largest_prime_factor(n: u32) -> (largest: u32)

description:
Implement a function that computes the sum of the first n positive integers for a given non-negative 32-bit unsigned input. Let S(n) denote this mathematical sum, defined by S(0) = 0 and, for n > 0, S(n) = n + S(n − 1). The function must return Some(S(n)) if S(n) fits within a 32-bit unsigned integer; otherwise, it must return None. The result must be consistent with the closed form S(n) = n(n + 1)/2 and with the monotonicity property: for any i ≤ j, S(i) ≤ S(j). No preconditions are imposed on the input; the function must handle all 32-bit unsigned values. It is known that the largest input for which S(n) fits in a 32-bit unsigned integer is 92681; consequently, for any input n ≥ 92682, the function must return None.
fn sum_to_n(n: u32) -> Option<u32>

Given an input string consisting of arbitrary Unicode characters, determine whether the parentheses in the string are correctly balanced. Treat the string as a sequence of Unicode scalar values. Only the characters '(' and ')' affect the check; all other characters are ignored. Scan the string from left to right, maintaining a count where '(' increases the count by one and ')' decreases it by one. The string is considered correctly bracketed if, during the scan, the count never becomes negative (i.e., there is never a closing parenthesis without a preceding unmatched opening parenthesis) and the final count is zero (i.e., all opening parentheses are matched). Assume the input length is at most i32::MAX and the negation of the length is at least i32::MIN. Return true if the parentheses are correctly balanced under these rules; otherwise, return false.
fn correct_bracketing(brackets: &str) -> (ret: bool)

You are given a vector of 32-bit unsigned integers representing the coefficients of a polynomial P(x), where the element at index i is the coefficient of x^i. Compute the coefficients of the derivative P'(x). For an input of length n:
- If n = 0, return an empty vector.
- Otherwise, return a vector of length n − 1 whose j-th element (0-based) is (j + 1) × input[j + 1].
Assume the input length is at most 2^32 − 1. Perform the multiplication using 64-bit arithmetic and return 64-bit unsigned integers to avoid overflow. The result must correspond exactly to the index-weighted sequence of the input starting from index 1, i.e., the sequence [1 × input[1], 2 × input[2], …, (n − 1) × input[n − 1]].
fn derivative(xs: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u64>

Compute the n-th term of a three-step Fibonacci (Tribonacci) sequence defined by the following recurrence: T(0) = 0, T(1) = 0, T(2) = 1, and for n >= 3, T(n) = T(n - 1) + T(n - 2) + T(n - 3). The input is a non-negative integer n (assume n fits in a 32-bit unsigned integer). The function must return the exact value of T(n) if it fits within a 32-bit unsigned integer; otherwise, it must indicate overflow by returning None. The sequence is monotone non-decreasing: for any i and j with 0 <= i <= j, T(i) <= T(j). The solution must terminate for all valid inputs.
spec fn spec_fibfib(n: nat) -> nat
fn fibfib(x: u32) -> Option<u32>
function_signature:
spec fn spec_fibfib(n: nat) -> nat
fn fibfib(x: u32) -> Option<u32>

description:
Given a string, return the count of standard vowels present in the string, where vowels are the ASCII letters 'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u' and their uppercase forms. The count is case-insensitive but limited strictly to these five letters; any other characters, including non-ASCII letters, are not considered vowels.

Additionally, if the string is non-empty and its last character is 'y' or 'Y', add 1 to the result.

Assume the input length, measured in Unicode scalar values, does not exceed u32::MAX. If the string is empty, return 0 (there is no trailing 'y' to add).
fn vowels_count(s: &str) -> (ret: u32)

Given a slice of characters, compute the sum of the numeric code points of the uppercase English letters only. Consider uppercase letters to be exactly the ASCII range 'A' through 'Z'. Characters outside this range, including lowercase letters, digits, punctuation, and non-ASCII Unicode characters, contribute zero to the sum. The input may be empty. The function must return a 128-bit unsigned integer equal to the mathematically defined sum obtained by traversing the input sequence and adding the code point of each character that is within 'A'..'Z'.
fn digit_sum(text: &[char]) -> (sum: u128)

function_signature:
spec fn sum(s: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn below_zero(operation: &[i64]) -> (r: bool)

description:
Given a read-only sequence of characters, determine whether the length of the sequence is a prime number. Use the following definition of primality: an integer n is prime if and only if n >= 2 and, for all integers k with 2 <= k < n, n % k != 0. If the length is less than 2, return false. The input length may be any non-negative integer.
pub open spec fn is_divisible(n: int, divisor: int) -> bool
pub open spec fn is_prime(n: int) -> bool
fn prime_length(str: &[char]) -> (result: bool)

Compute the Brazilian factorial (also known as the product of factorials or superfactorial) of a given non-negative integer. The input is provided as a 64-bit unsigned integer. Define the mathematical factorial for natural numbers by 0! = 1, 1! = 1, and for n ≥ 2, n! = n × (n − 1)!. Define the Brazilian factorial BF(n) as the product of factorials from 1 through n: BF(0) = 1, BF(1) = 1, and for n ≥ 2, BF(n) = n! × BF(n − 1) (equivalently, BF(n) = ∏_{k=1}^n k!). The function must return an Option<u64> where:
- Some(v) is returned if BF(n) fits exactly within a 64-bit unsigned integer, with v equal to BF(n).
- None is returned if BF(n) exceeds the maximum value representable by a 64-bit unsigned integer.
You may assume all computations are performed over mathematical integers and then checked against 64-bit bounds. The Brazilian factorial is known to be positive for all n and monotonically non-decreasing with respect to n. In particular, BF(9) exceeds u64::MAX, and for all n ≥ 9, BF(n) > u64::MAX, so the function must return None for such inputs.
pub fn brazilian_factorial_impl(n: u64) -> (ret: Option<u64>)

Given a non-empty slice of 32-bit signed integers, compute the mean absolute deviation (MAD) using Euclidean integer division. Let n be the length of the slice (assume n > 0). Define the mean as the integer q such that sum = n * q + r with 0 ≤ r < n (i.e., q is the Euclidean quotient of the sum divided by n). For each element x in the slice, compute the absolute deviation |x − mean|. Let S be the sum of these absolute deviations. The required output is the Euclidean quotient of S divided by n, returned as a 32-bit unsigned integer. All division operations are mathematical Euclidean divisions with non-negative remainders, and the absolute difference between any two 32-bit signed integers fits within a 32-bit unsigned integer.
pub fn mean_absolute_deviation(numbers: &[i32]) -> u32

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a 32-bit signed integer value, determine whether the value is strictly greater than every element in the array. Return true if and only if for all valid indices in the array, the value is greater than the element at that index. There are no preconditions on the array or the value; the array may be empty. For an empty array, the property holds vacuously and the result must be true.
fn is_greater(arr: &Vec<i32>, number: i32) -> bool

Given a non-empty array of integer arrays, return one of the arrays whose length is maximal among all arrays in the input. Assume the outer array has a strictly positive length. The returned array must be exactly equal to one of the arrays in the input (i.e., it is not a newly constructed array), and its length must be at least as large as the length of every array in the input. If several arrays tie for the maximal length, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn max_length_list(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (max_list: &Vec<i32>)

Given a read-only array of 64-bit unsigned integers, return a new array that is an exact deep copy of the input. The output must have the same length as the input, and for every valid index, the element at that index in the output must equal the corresponding element in the input. During construction, the partial output must always be prefix-correct: after processing t elements (for some t with 0 <= t <= input length), the first t elements of the output must match the first t elements of the input and the partial output length must equal t. The method must only access elements within the bounds of the input array and must complete after processing all elements, making strictly decreasing progress on the number of remaining elements.
fn list_deep_clone(arr: &Vec<u64>) -> Vec<u64>

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array where each element is the square of the corresponding element in the input. The output must preserve the input order and have the same length as the input. Assume the following precondition: for every valid index, the square of the element is non-negative and strictly less than i32::MAX, ensuring that squaring any input element does not overflow a 32-bit signed integer.
fn square_nums(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> (squared: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty list of integer arrays, determine whether all arrays have the same length. Assume the outer list contains at least one inner array. Return a boolean value that is true if and only if, for every pair of indices within the bounds of the outer list, the lengths of the corresponding inner arrays are equal; otherwise return false.
fn all_sequence_equal_length(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (result: bool)

Given a non-empty collection of integer sublists, return a reference to one sublist that has the smallest length among all sublists in the collection. Assume the input contains at least one sublist. The returned sublist must be exactly equal to some sublist present in the input (i.e., it is not a modified copy), and its length must be less than or equal to the length of every other sublist in the collection. If multiple sublists share the minimal length, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn min_sublist(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (min_list: &Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array obtained by subtracting the second array from the first array element by element. Assume both input arrays have the same length (including the possibility of length zero). Additionally, assume that for every index within the valid range, the mathematical difference between the corresponding elements fits within the 32-bit signed integer range, so no overflow or underflow occurs. The resulting array must have the same length as the inputs, and at each index it must equal the first array’s element minus the second array’s element.
fn element_wise_subtract(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a target integer value, determine whether every element of the array is equal to the target. Return true if and only if, for all valid indices i with 0 ≤ i < length of the array, the element at index i equals the target; otherwise, return false. The function must handle arrays of any length, including empty arrays. For an empty array, the result should be true (the universal condition holds vacuously). There are no additional input preconditions.
fn all_elements_equals(arr: &Vec<i32>, element: i32) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that takes two arrays of 32-bit signed integers and returns a new array where each element is the sum of the corresponding elements from the two inputs. Assume both input arrays have the same length. Additionally, assume that for every index, the mathematical sum of the two elements at that index lies within the 32-bit signed integer range [i32::MIN, i32::MAX]; this guarantees that no overflow occurs. The function must return an array of the same length as the inputs, and for every valid index, the returned value equals the sum of the two input elements at that index. The inputs may be empty.
fn add_list(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array that contains exactly those elements of the input that are odd (i.e., have a remainder of 1 when divided by 2). Preserve the original relative order of the retained elements. There are no preconditions on the input: the array may be empty or of any length, and its elements may be any valid 32-bit unsigned values. The output must be exactly the subsequence of the input obtained by filtering with the predicate “is odd,” with no additional elements and no reordering.
fn filter_odd_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (odd_list: Vec<u32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether they share at least one common value. Return true if and only if there exist valid indices in both arrays such that the elements at those indices are equal. The arrays may be empty; there are no additional constraints on their contents or sizes.
fn has_common_element(list1: &Vec<i32>, list2: &Vec<i32>) -> bool

Implement two functions over arrays of 32-bit signed integers. The first function returns whether a given value appears in an array. Its result must be true if and only if there exists an index within the array bounds whose element equals the queried value. The second function computes the intersection of two arrays and returns an array containing all distinct values that appear in both inputs. The intersection must satisfy: every returned element is present in both input arrays, and no element appears more than once in the output. The input arrays may be empty and may contain duplicates. There is no requirement on the ordering of elements in the returned intersection.
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn intersection(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn intersection(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given a vector of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the vector contains only one distinct value. Return true if and only if every element is equal to the first element. For an empty vector or a vector with a single element, return true. The returned boolean must be exactly equivalent to the universal property: for all indices i with 1 <= i < length of the vector, the element at position i equals the element at position 0. No additional preconditions are imposed on the input.
fn has_only_one_distinct_element(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Implement two functions over a vector of characters.

1) Frequency counting: Given a vector of characters and a target character, return the number of times the target appears in the vector. Assume all inputs are valid, including an empty vector. The returned frequency must equal the total count of the target character in the entire vector.

2) First repeated character: Given a vector of characters, return the earliest index i (0-based) and the character at that position such that the character at position i appears more than once in the entire vector. If no character is repeated, return None. Additionally, when a result is returned, every character at positions strictly before i must appear at most once in the entire vector. When None is returned, every character in the vector must appear at most once.

There are no preconditions on input size or content. An empty vector is valid and should yield a frequency of 0 for any target, and None for the first repeated character query.
pub open spec fn count_frequency_rcr(seq: Seq<char>, key: char) -> int
fn count_frequency(arr: &Vec<char>, key: char) -> (frequency: usize)
fn first_repeated_char(str1: &Vec<char>) -> (repeated_char: Option<(usize, char)>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, compute the maximum possible difference between any two elements, defined as element_a minus element_b. Assume the array length is positive. Additionally, assume every element is strictly greater than i32::MIN / 2 and strictly less than i32::MAX / 2. The returned value must be an upper bound for all pairwise differences in the array: for any two valid indices, element_at_i − element_at_j is less than or equal to the returned value. The intended result equals the difference between the largest and smallest elements in the array. The integer bounds guarantee that tracking the minimum and maximum values and computing the final difference does not overflow.
fn max_difference(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (diff: i32)

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array containing exactly those elements of the input that are even (i.e., divisible by 2), preserving their original order. The output must match the result of taking the input sequence and filtering it by the predicate “value % 2 == 0”. Assume no additional constraints on the input length. In the intended verified solution, while iterating through the input from left to right, the partial result after processing any prefix of length i must equal the even elements from that prefix, and the loop index must always remain within the bounds 0 <= index <= input length.
fn find_even_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (even_numbers: Vec<u32>)

Given three arrays of 32-bit signed integers with equal length n, construct and return a new array formed by interleaving the elements index-wise: for each index i from 0 to n − 1, append the i-th element of the first array, then the i-th element of the second array, then the i-th element of the third array. Assume n is such that 0 ≤ 3 · n ≤ the maximum value of a 32-bit signed integer. The returned array must have length exactly 3 · n, and for every valid index i, the elements at positions 3 · i, 3 · i + 1, and 3 · i + 2 in the output must match the i-th elements of the first, second, and third input arrays respectively. The input arrays may be empty.
fn interleave(s1: &Vec<i32>, s2: &Vec<i32>, s3: &Vec<i32>) -> (res: Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers of equal length, return a new array where each element is the product of the corresponding elements from the two input arrays. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. Also assume that for every index within bounds, the product of the two corresponding elements fits within the 32-bit signed integer range [i32::MIN, i32::MAX]. The output array must have the same length as the inputs, and for each valid index i, the output at position i equals the product of the inputs at position i. If the inputs are empty, the result is an empty array.
fn element_wise_multiplication(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of characters, return the number of characters that are ASCII decimal digits. A character is considered a digit if and only if its ASCII code lies in the inclusive range from 48 to 57 (corresponding to '0' through '9'). There are no preconditions on the input. The return value must satisfy 0 <= result <= input_length. The result must be extensionally equal to a mathematically specified count: the number of positions in the sequence whose character is a digit. Equivalently, this can be defined by a recursive specification that processes the sequence from the end, adding 1 when the last character is a digit and 0 otherwise, with a well-founded measure that decreases by the sequence length. If the algorithm processes the sequence left-to-right, it should maintain the property that after k elements have been processed, the running count equals the number of digit characters in the first k elements.
fn count_digits(text: &Vec<char>) -> (count: usize)

Given a vector of characters, construct and return a new vector of the same length where the case of each ASCII alphabetic character is toggled:
- Each lowercase letter in the range 'a'..='z' is replaced by its corresponding uppercase letter in 'A'..='Z'.
- Each uppercase letter in the range 'A'..='Z' is replaced by its corresponding lowercase letter in 'a'..='z'.
- All other characters (including digits, punctuation, whitespace, and any non-ASCII Unicode characters) remain unchanged.

Output requirements:
- The output vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector.
- For every valid index i (0 <= i < length of the input), the output at position i equals the toggle-case transformation of the input character at position i as defined above.

There are no additional preconditions on the input; the function must handle empty inputs and arbitrary Unicode characters by leaving non-ASCII characters unchanged.
fn to_toggle_case(str1: &Vec<char>) -> (toggle_case: Vec<char>)

Given a non-empty list of integer arrays, return the length of the shortest inner array. Assume the outer list contains at least one inner array. The result must be a non-negative integer, must be less than or equal to the length of every inner array, and must equal the length of at least one inner array in the list.
fn smallest_list_length(list: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (min: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the smallest value contained in the array. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The result must satisfy both of the following correctness conditions: it is less than or equal to every element of the array, and it is equal to at least one element in the array.
fn smallest_num(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> (min: i32)

Given an array of characters, determine whether it contains the letter 'Z' in either uppercase ('Z') or lowercase ('z'). The array may be empty. Return true if and only if there exists an index within the bounds of the array such that the character at that index is 'Z' or 'z'; otherwise, return false.
fn contains_z(text: &Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Given two arrays of unsigned 32-bit integers of equal length, return a new array containing the element-wise unsigned integer division of the first array by the second array. Assume the input arrays have the same length. Assume every element of the second array is non-zero so that division is well-defined. Also assume that for every index, the quotient of the corresponding elements is representable as a 32-bit unsigned integer. The output array must have the same length as the inputs, and for each valid index i, the result at position i equals the unsigned integer division of the first array's element at i by the second array's element at i. The function must handle empty arrays as valid input.
fn element_wise_division(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)

You are given a non-empty array of integer arrays. Assume that every inner array has length at least 2 (it may be longer). Consider the second element (index 1) of each inner array. Among all inner arrays, find the minimum value of these second elements. Return the first element (index 0) of one inner array whose second element attains this minimum. If multiple inner arrays have the same minimum second element, returning the first element from any one of them is acceptable.
fn min_second_value_first(arr: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (first_of_min_second: i32)

Implement two functions on arrays of 32-bit signed integers. The first function determines whether a given value is present in an input array. Its return value must be true if and only if there exists an index within the array bounds whose element equals the queried value; otherwise, it must return false. The second function takes two arrays and returns an array that includes all values that appear in exactly one of the two inputs. The output array may be in any order and the inputs may be empty and may contain duplicates. Every value included in the output must appear at least once in one input and not appear in the other input, and the output must not contain duplicates. Formally, for every valid index of the first input, if that element does not occur in the second input, it must occur in the output; and for every valid index of the second input, if that element does not occur in the first input, it must occur in the output. Additionally, every pair of distinct indices of the output must hold different values (i.e., all output elements are pairwise distinct).
proof fn lemma_vec_push<T>(vec: Seq<T>, i: T, l: usize)
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn find_dissimilar(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:
proof fn lemma_vec_push<T>(vec: Seq<T>, i: T, l: usize)
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn find_dissimilar(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers and an index k (1-based), construct and return a new array equal to the original array with the k-th element removed. Assume the following constraints hold: the array length n is greater than 0, and k satisfies 1 <= k < n. The relative order of the remaining elements must be preserved. Equivalently, the result is the concatenation of the first k-1 elements of the input and the elements from position k+1 to the end. The input array must not be modified; produce a new array as output.
fn remove_kth_element(list: &Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> Vec<i32>

function_signature:
spec fn is_divisible(n: int, divisor: int) -> bool
fn prime_num(n: u64) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given an array of non-negative integers (0-based indexing), determine whether the parity of each index matches the parity of the element stored at that index. Return true if and only if, for every valid index i in [0, n), i mod 2 equals the element at index i mod 2. The array length may be zero, in which case the property holds vacuously.
fn is_even_at_even_index(arr: &Vec<usize>) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that, given a dynamic array of 32-bit signed integers, returns a new array containing exactly the elements that are strictly less than zero. The relative order of elements in the output must be the same as their order in the input. The returned sequence must be equal to the result of filtering the input by the predicate x < 0. There are no preconditions on the input array; it may be empty or contain any combination of negative, zero, or positive values. The input must not be modified.
fn find_negative_numbers(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (negative_list: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array containing exactly the elements of the input that are odd (i.e., not divisible by 2), preserving their original relative order. The input may be empty. The result must be logically equivalent to applying a filter with the predicate "value % 2 != 0" to the input sequence.
fn find_odd_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (odd_numbers: Vec<u32>)

Given three integer arrays of equal length, compute the number of indices where all three arrays have the same value at that index. Assume the arrays have identical lengths (including the possibility of zero length). The result must be an integer between 0 and the common length of the arrays, inclusive.
spec fn count_identical(s1: Seq<i32>, s2: Seq<i32>, s3: Seq<i32>) -> int
fn count_identical_position(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>, arr3: &Vec<i32>) -> usize
function_signature:
spec fn count_identical(s1: Seq<i32>, s2: Seq<i32>, s3: Seq<i32>) -> int
fn count_identical_position(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>, arr3: &Vec<i32>) -> usize

description:
Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array where each element is the cube of the corresponding input element. Assume the following input constraints hold for every index within the array bounds: the square of each element is within the 32-bit signed integer range, and the cube of each element is within the 32-bit signed integer range. The output must preserve the order and length of the input, and for every valid index i, output[i] equals input[i] × input[i] × input[i].
fn cube_element(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> (cubed: Vec<i32>)

Given two integer arrays of equal length, determine whether the first array is strictly greater than the second array at every corresponding index. Assume both arrays have the same length. Return true if and only if for all indices i in the range [0, n), the element at index i in the first array is greater than the element at index i in the second array. If the arrays are empty, the result should be true.
fn is_smaller(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

function_signature:
spec fn sum_negative_to(seq: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn sum_negatives(arr: &Vec<i64>) -> (sum_neg: i128)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers and a split index, partition the array into two new arrays. Assume the split index is a positive integer strictly less than the length of the array. Return a tuple of two arrays: the first contains exactly the first split-index elements of the input in the same order, and the second contains exactly the remaining elements from the split index to the end, also preserving order. The output must match these exact subranges of the input; no reordering or modification of values is allowed.
