fn split_array(list: &Vec<i32>, l: usize) -> (Vec<i32>, Vec<i32>)

You are given a finite array of 64-bit signed integers. Implement a function that returns the total sum of all elements as a 128-bit signed integer.

Correctness must be stated against the mathematical sum of the sequence: define s([]) = 0 and for any non-empty sequence s(prefix ⧺ [last]) = s(prefix) + last. The function must ensure its return value equals this mathematical sum applied to the input array.

Assume the input length is non-negative (as for any array). The elements may be any 64-bit signed values, including boundary values. For any prefix length k with 0 ≤ k ≤ length, the sum of the first k elements is bounded between k · i64::MIN and k · i64::MAX (a property implied by each element being within the 64-bit range). The algorithm must terminate.
spec fn sum_to(arr: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn sum(arr: &Vec<i64>) -> (sum: i128)
function_signature:
spec fn sum_to(arr: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn sum(arr: &Vec<i64>) -> (sum: i128)

description:
Given an array of characters, compute how many elements are uppercase English letters. A character is considered uppercase if its Unicode scalar value lies in the inclusive range 65 through 90 (i.e., 'A' to 'Z' in ASCII). The input may be empty. Return the count as an unsigned 64-bit integer.
The result must satisfy the following properties:
- The count is within bounds: 0 ≤ count ≤ the length of the input array.
- The count equals the value defined by an idealized, purely functional recursive specification that processes the sequence from right to left: if the sequence is empty, the count is 0; otherwise, the count is the result for the sequence without its last element plus 1 if the last element is an uppercase letter and plus 0 otherwise. The recursive specification must decrease structurally with the sequence length to ensure termination.
In addition, any iterative solution must maintain the following invariants while scanning the array from left to right:
- At any point during processing a prefix of length k, both the position k and the accumulated count are within bounds: 0 ≤ k ≤ array length and 0 ≤ accumulated count ≤ k.
- The accumulated count for the processed prefix equals the count specified by the recursive definition for that same prefix.
The task is to implement a function that returns the described count and fulfills the stated correctness properties.
spec fn is_lower_case(c: char) -> bool
spec fn is_upper_case(c: char) -> bool
spec fn count_uppercase_recursively(seq: Seq<char>) -> int
fn count_uppercase(text: &Vec<char>) -> (count: u64)
function_signature:
spec fn is_lower_case(c: char) -> bool
spec fn is_upper_case(c: char) -> bool
spec fn count_uppercase_recursively(seq: Seq<char>) -> int
fn count_uppercase(text: &Vec<char>) -> (count: u64)

description:
Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the second array appears as a contiguous subarray of the first array.

You must provide two functions:
1) An index-based checker that, given a start index, determines whether the contiguous slice of the first array starting at that index and having the same length as the second array is exactly equal to the second array. Assume the second array’s length is less than or equal to the first array’s length. Also assume the start index is valid for such a comparison, i.e., it is an integer k with 0 ≤ k ≤ n − m, where n is the length of the first array and m is the length of the second array. The function returns true if and only if the slice of the first array from k (inclusive) to k + m (exclusive) matches the second array element-by-element; otherwise it returns false.

2) A search function that checks whether there exists some start index k satisfying 0 ≤ k ≤ n − m such that the contiguous slice of the first array from k (inclusive) to k + m (exclusive) matches the second array element-by-element. Assume the second array’s length is less than or equal to the first array’s length. The function returns true if and only if such an index exists; otherwise it returns false.

Indexes are 0-based. If the second array is empty (m = 0), it is considered to occur at every valid start index, and the search function must return true. For the index-based checker with an empty second array, any k with 0 ≤ k ≤ n is valid and should yield true.
fn is_sub_list_at_index(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>, start: usize) -> bool
fn is_sub_list(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn is_sub_list_at_index(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>, start: usize) -> bool
fn is_sub_list(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> bool

spec fn is_even(n: u32) -> bool
fn is_product_even(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the sum of its minimum and maximum elements. Assume every element in the array is strictly greater than i32::MIN / 2 and strictly less than i32::MAX / 2; this guarantees the sum fits within the 32-bit signed range. The result must equal the mathematical minimum of the array plus the mathematical maximum of the array, viewed over the entire input. The array can be unsorted and may contain duplicate values. For an array of length 1, the output is twice that single element.
fn sum_min_max(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (sum: i32)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, compute an element-wise bitwise XOR of the arrays.
Assume the two input arrays have the same length. Return a new array of the same length where, for every valid index i, the value at position i is equal to the bitwise XOR of the corresponding elements from the two input arrays.
If the arrays are empty, return an empty array.
fn bit_wise_xor(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of characters, construct a new array of the same length where each position is processed independently as follows: if the character at that position is an uppercase ASCII letter ('A' through 'Z'), the output at that position is the corresponding lowercase ASCII letter; otherwise, the output at that position is exactly the input character. Assume the input length can be any non-negative integer (including zero). The output length must equal the input length. Formally, for every valid index i, output[i] equals input[i] if input[i] is not an uppercase ASCII letter; otherwise, output[i] is the lowercase counterpart of input[i].
fn to_lowercase(str1: &Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

Given a finite list of character sequences, produce a new list consisting of the last character from each input sequence, in the same order as the inputs. Assume every input sequence is non-empty. The output list must have the same length as the number of input sequences, and for each valid index, the element at that index in the output is equal to the last character of the corresponding input sequence. If the input list is empty, the output is empty.
fn extract_rear_chars(s: &Vec<Vec<char>>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers and a positive index n that is strictly less than the array length, return a new array formed by reversing the first n elements and then appending the remaining elements in their original order. The input array is not modified.

Assumptions:
- The array length is greater than 0.
- The index n satisfies 0 < n < length of the array.

Output requirements:
- The returned array has the same length as the input array.
- Its first n elements are the reverse of the input array’s first n elements.
- The elements from position n to the end are identical to the corresponding elements of the input array and remain in the same order.
fn reverse_to_k(list: &Vec<i32>, n: usize) -> Vec<i32>

You are given an array of arrays of 32-bit signed integers. Assume that every inner array is non-empty. Build and return a new array that contains, for each inner array in order, its first element. The outer array may be empty; in that case, return an empty array.
The returned array must satisfy the following properties:
- Its length is exactly equal to the number of inner arrays in the input.
- For every valid index i, the element at position i in the result is equal to the first element of the i-th inner array in the input.
fn get_first_elements(arr: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Implement two functions operating on arrays of characters:

1) Membership check: Given an array of characters and a key character, return true if and only if the key occurs at some valid index in the array; otherwise return false. There are no preconditions on the input array.

2) Character removal by filter: Given two arrays of characters, return a new array consisting of exactly those characters from the first array whose value does not appear anywhere in the second array. The relative order of the characters retained from the first array must be preserved. The result must satisfy both of the following properties:
- Every character in the result is an element of the first array and does not occur in the second array.
- For every character in the first array, that character either occurs in the second array or occurs somewhere in the result.

Assume the input arrays may be empty. Duplicates are supported: every occurrence in the first array whose value is absent from the second array is included in the result.
fn contains(str: &Vec<char>, key: char) -> bool
fn remove_chars(str1: &Vec<char>, str2: &Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>
function_signature:
fn contains(str: &Vec<char>, key: char) -> bool
fn remove_chars(str1: &Vec<char>, str2: &Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

description:
Given an array of characters, determine whether all elements in the array are identical. Return true if and only if every character at index i (for all i with 1 <= i < array length) is equal to the character at index 0. If the array has length 0 or 1, return true. There are no additional preconditions on the input; the array may be empty.
fn all_characters_same(char_arr: &Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Implement two functions over an array of 32-bit integers.

1) Frequency of a target value:
Given an immutable array and a target integer, return the number of times the target appears in the array. The result must equal the frequency defined by the following abstract specification over the sequence view of the array: the frequency of a value in an empty sequence is 0; otherwise it is the frequency in the sequence without its last element plus 1 if the last element equals the target, and plus 0 otherwise. Assume the array length is finite. The returned count must be non-negative and no greater than the array length.

2) Remove duplicates by global frequency:
Given an immutable array, construct and return a new array that contains exactly those elements from the input whose total frequency in the input (as defined by the specification above) is 1. The elements in the returned array must appear in the same relative order as they appear in the input. Elements that appear more than once in the input must not appear in the output at all. The behavior on an empty input array should produce an empty output.

No assumptions about sorting or distinctness are made; any 32-bit integer values are allowed. Both routines must be total and terminate, and their results must agree with the abstract specification described above.
pub open spec fn count_frequency_rcr(seq: Seq<i32>, key: i32) -> int
fn count_frequency(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (frequency: usize)
fn remove_duplicates(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (unique_arr: Vec<i32>)

Given a sorted array of 32-bit signed integers in nondecreasing order and a target integer, return the index of the first occurrence of the target. Assume the input array is sorted such that for all indices i and j with 0 <= i < j < length, the element at i is less than or equal to the element at j. If the target appears, the returned index must be within bounds, the element at that index must equal the target, and every index before it must not equal the target. If the target does not appear anywhere in the array, return None.
fn find_first_occurrence(arr: &Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> Option<usize>

Implement a function that, given a vector of unsigned 32-bit integers, returns a new vector containing exactly the even elements of the input, in their original relative order. The input may be empty. The output must be observationally equivalent to filtering the input sequence by the predicate “value modulo 2 equals 0,” preserving duplicates and order. The function must not modify the input. There are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn remove_odds(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (even_list: Vec<u32>)

function_signature:
spec fn rotation_split(len: usize, n: usize) -> int
fn rotate_right(list: &Vec<u32>, n: usize) -> (new_list: Vec<u32>)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers, implement a function that determines whether the array is sorted in non-decreasing order. Assume the input length is strictly positive. The function should return true if and only if, for all indices i and j with 0 <= i < j < length, the element at position i is less than or equal to the element at position j. Otherwise, return false. You may assume the input is read-only and that the function always terminates. Inputs that do not satisfy the non-empty requirement will not be provided.
fn is_sorted(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (is_sorted: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return the 0-based index of the first element that is odd. If the array contains no odd elements, return None. The function has no preconditions. The following postconditions must hold:
- If the result is Some(idx), then every element strictly before idx is even, and the element at idx is odd.
- If the result is None, then every element in the array is even.
Assume standard 0-based indexing and that returning None is permitted when there is no odd element.
fn find_first_odd(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> Option<usize>

Given an array of non-negative integers (0-based indexing), determine whether the parity of each element matches the parity of its index. Specifically, for every index i in the range [0, length of the array), the element at position i must be even if i is even and odd if i is odd. Return true if and only if this property holds for all indices; otherwise, return false. The array may be empty.
fn is_odd_at_odd_index(arr: &Vec<usize>) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of 64-bit signed integers and two indices, compute the exact sum of the elements in the inclusive index range from the start index to the end index. Assume the indices satisfy 0 <= start <= end and end is strictly less than the length of the array (i.e., they designate a valid, non-empty contiguous segment of the array). Return the sum as a 128-bit signed integer.

Correctness requirements:
- The returned value must equal the mathematical sum of the array elements in the inclusive range [start, end].
- Conceptually, for any prefix of the selected range (from the start index up to any intermediate position within the range), the partial sum is bounded by the element-wise extremal bounds: it lies between i64::MIN multiplied by the number of elements in that prefix and i64::MAX multiplied by the number of elements in that prefix. This reflects that each element is within the i64 range and ensures arithmetic safety of intermediate sums.
- The computation must terminate after processing exactly end - start + 1 elements.
spec fn sum_to(arr: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn sum_range_list(arr: &Vec<i64>, start: usize, end: usize) -> (sum: i128)

description:
Given a sequence of characters, a target character, and a replacement character, construct and return a new sequence where every occurrence of the target character in the input is replaced by the replacement character. The input may be empty. The output must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the range [0, length), the character at position i in the result must be equal to the replacement character if and only if the character at position i in the input equals the target character; otherwise it must be equal to the original character at that position. The original input sequence must not be modified.
fn replace_chars(str1: &Vec<char>, old_char: char, new_char: char) -> (result: Vec<char>)
function_signature: fn replace_chars(str1: &Vec<char>, old_char: char, new_char: char) -> (result: Vec<char>)

description:
Given two arrays of 32-bit unsigned integers of equal length, construct a new array where each element is the integer quotient of the corresponding elements of the first array divided by the second array. Assume the following conditions hold:
- The two input arrays have the same length.
- Every element in the second array is non-zero (no division by zero).
- For every index, the quotient produced by dividing the element from the first array by the corresponding element from the second array lies within the 32-bit signed integer range [i32::MIN, i32::MAX].
Return an array whose length equals the length of the inputs, and such that for every valid index i, the element at position i in the output equals the integer division result of the input elements at position i in the first and second arrays. Indexing is 0-based. The function is required to satisfy the specification only for inputs meeting the stated assumptions.
fn element_wise_divide(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers with length at least 2, return two indices (i_min, i_second) that satisfy the following properties:
- Consider the global minimum value of the entire array. If the value at i_min equals this global minimum, then for every other index k (where k is within bounds and k != i_min), the value at k is greater than or equal to the value at i_second.
- There exists at least one index k (within bounds and k != i_min) whose value is equal to the value at i_second.
Duplicates are allowed. The second index may point to an element with the same value as the minimum if the minimum occurs multiple times. Assume the input length is at least 2 (so the global minimum is well-defined on the array).
fn second_smallest(numbers: &Vec<i32>) -> (indices: (usize, usize))

Given an array of 32-bit integers and a single 32-bit integer value, construct a new array by inserting the given value immediately before each element of the original array. Assume the input array may be empty or have any finite length. The output array must have exactly twice the length of the input array. For every valid index k in the range [0, input_length), the element at position 2k of the output must be the given value, and the element at position 2k + 1 must be equal to the k-th element of the input array. The construction must preserve the original order of elements. A correct solution is expected to be provably terminating by processing each input element exactly once, and to maintain the following partial-correctness property during construction: after processing the first t elements (for any t in [0, input_length]), the partial output has length 2t and, for all indices k in [0, t), positions 2k and 2k + 1 are the inserted value and the k-th input element, respectively.
fn insert_before_each(arr: &Vec<i32>, elem: i32) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of integers and a positive split index, construct a new array by splitting the original array at the given index and appending the first part to the end of the second part. Formally, assume the array length is greater than 0 and the split index satisfies 1 <= split index < array length. The result must be exactly the concatenation of the subarray from the split index to the end followed by the subarray from the beginning up to (but not including) the split index. The output array must preserve the order within each subarray and have the same length as the input.
fn split_and_append(list: &Vec<i32>, n: usize) -> (new_list: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of characters, return a new array of the same length where every occurrence of a space (' '), a comma (','), or a dot ('.') in the input is replaced by a colon (':') in the output. All other characters must remain unchanged and in their original positions. There are no preconditions on the input; the function must handle empty arrays and any valid Unicode character. Formally, the output length must equal the input length, and for every index k in the range [0, output_length), the output character at position k must be ':' if the input character at position k is a space, comma, or dot; otherwise, it must be identical to the input character at position k.
fn replace_with_colon(str1: &Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn difference(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given an array of characters, determine whether every element is a decimal digit from '0' to '9'. Return true if and only if all characters in the array have Unicode code points between 48 and 57 inclusive (i.e., the ASCII digits '0' through '9'). If the array is empty, return true.

Correctness requirement: The result must be true exactly when, for all valid indices in the array, the character at that index is a digit ('0'–'9'). Otherwise, the result must be false.
fn is_digit(c: char) -> bool
fn is_integer(text: &Vec<char>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn is_digit(c: char) -> bool
fn is_integer(text: &Vec<char>) -> bool

description:
Given a vector of characters, produce a new vector of the same length where each lowercase English letter ('a' through 'z') is converted to its uppercase counterpart, and every other character remains unchanged. The conversion for lowercase letters must follow the standard ASCII mapping: for any lowercase letter c in 'a'..'z', the resulting character is the one obtained by subtracting 32 from c’s ASCII code (i.e., 'a' -> 'A', ..., 'z' -> 'Z'). The input can be any finite vector of characters, including non-ASCII characters; only lowercase ASCII letters are transformed. The output vector must have exactly the same length as the input, and for each index i within bounds, the output at position i reflects the described mapping applied to the input at position i. The original input must not be modified.
fn to_uppercase(str1: &Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Implement a function that takes two arrays of unsigned 32-bit integers of equal length and returns a new array containing the element-wise remainder of dividing the first array by the second. For each index, the divisor in the second array must be non-zero. Assume that for every index, the computed remainder fits within the signed 32-bit integer range. The returned array must have the same length as the input arrays, and at every index it must equal the remainder of the corresponding elements.
fn element_wise_module(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)

fn count_true(arr: &Vec<bool>) -> (count: u64)

description:
Implement two functions over arrays of 32-bit integers.
1) A membership check: Given an array and an integer value, return true if and only if there exists some index within the array where the element equals the given value. The array may be empty.
2) A cross-array existence check: Given two arrays, return true if and only if there exists some index in the first array such that the corresponding value appears somewhere in the second array. Either array may be empty.
There are no additional input preconditions. The returned results must exactly match these existence properties:
- For the membership check, the result is true if and only if there exists an index in the valid range of the array where the element equals the target.
- For the cross-array check, the result is true if and only if there exists an index in the valid range of the first array whose value is contained in the second array.
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn any_value_exists(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn any_value_exists(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

fn sub_array_at_index(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>, idx: usize) -> (result: bool)
fn is_sub_array(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

spec fn is_divisible(n: int, divisor: int) -> bool
fn is_non_prime(n: u64) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given an array of characters and a replacement character, construct and return a new array of the same length where each element is transformed as follows: at every index from 0 to n−1, if the input element is the ASCII space character (U+0020), the corresponding output element must be the given replacement character; otherwise, the output element must be equal to the input element at that position. The input array may be empty. The output length must be exactly equal to the input length. Only the standard ASCII space character is considered a blank for replacement; other whitespace or Unicode characters must remain unchanged.
fn replace_blanks_with_chars(str1: &Vec<char>, ch: char) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Implement two functions operating on integer arrays:

1) Membership check: Given an array and an integer value, return true if and only if there exists an index within the array’s bounds whose element equals the given value. The result must exactly reflect logical membership: it is true precisely when the value occurs in the array and false otherwise. No assumptions are made about ordering or duplicates in the array; any finite-length array is allowed.

2) Filter by exclusion: Given two arrays, referred to as the first array and the second array, return a new array consisting only of elements taken from the first array that do not occur anywhere in the second array. The following properties must hold for the returned array:
- Every element in the returned array appears in the first array and does not appear in the second array.
- For every position in the first array, the element at that position either appears in the second array or appears somewhere in the returned array.

There are no preconditions on the contents or lengths of the input arrays. The specification does not impose any requirement on ordering or uniqueness in the returned array; duplicates may be present. The algorithm must terminate for all valid inputs.
fn contains(str: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn remove_elements(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
function_signature:
fn contains(str: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn remove_elements(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
Implement two functions over arrays of 32-bit signed integers.

1) Boolean membership check: Given an array and a key value, return true if and only if there exists an index within the array bounds such that the element at that index equals the key; otherwise return false. There are no preconditions on the array (it may be empty).

2) Distinct intersection: Given two arrays, return a new array containing values that are present in both input arrays. The returned array must satisfy the following properties:
- Every element in the returned array appears in both input arrays.
- The returned array contains no duplicate values (all elements are pairwise distinct).
There are no preconditions on the inputs (they may be empty and may contain duplicates). No particular ordering of the returned elements is required.
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn shared_elements(list1: &Vec<i32>, list2: &Vec<i32>) -> (shared: Vec<i32>)
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn shared_elements(list1: &Vec<i32>, list2: &Vec<i32>) -> (shared: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, construct and return a new array defined as follows: take all elements of the first array except its last element (i.e., the prefix up to but not including the final element), and then append all elements of the second array in their original order. Assume the first array is non-empty. The output must preserve the relative order of the retained elements from the first array and of all elements from the second array. The resulting array’s length equals (length of the first array minus one) plus (length of the second array). The inputs are not modified.
fn replace_last_element(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> (replaced_list: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether every adjacent pair of elements is consecutive, increasing by exactly 1. Assume for every element x in the array that x + 1 is a valid 32-bit signed integer and non-negative, i.e., -1 <= x < i32::MAX - 1. Return true if and only if, for all indices i where an adjacent pair exists, the next element equals the current element plus one. Otherwise, return false.
fn contains_consecutive_numbers(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (is_consecutive: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a target integer, determine whether the target value appears in the array. Return true if and only if there exists an index i such that 0 <= i < the array's length and the element at that index equals the target; otherwise return false. There are no preconditions: the array may be empty, and any integer value may be provided as the target. The correctness requirement is that the Boolean result must exactly correspond to the truth of the existential statement described above.
fn contains_k(arr: &Vec<i32>, k: i32) -> bool

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the difference between the maximum element and the minimum element in the array. Assume the input size is positive. Additionally, assume every element lies within the inclusive range [i32::MIN / 2, i32::MAX / 2]. Under these assumptions, the result must be nonnegative and must upper-bound every pairwise difference in the array: for any two valid indices i and j, the value at index i minus the value at index j is less than or equal to the returned result. If the array contains a single element, the result is 0.
fn difference_max_min(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (diff: i32)

Given a positive integer representing the length of a contiguous period measured in days in a standard 7‑day week that contains exactly five working days and two days off per week, compute the minimum and maximum possible number of days off contained in any such contiguous period of that length. The period may start on any day of the week. Return the results as a pair of non‑negative integers [min, max] where min <= max and both values are at most the input length.
Assume the input is at least 1. For every complete group of seven days in the period, there are exactly two days off. For the remaining r = n mod 7 days beyond the complete weeks, the minimum additional days off contributed by these remaining days is max(0, r − 5) and the maximum additional days off is min(2, r). Therefore, the overall minimum equals 2 × floor(n / 7) + max(0, r − 5), and the overall maximum equals 2 × floor(n / 7) + min(2, r).
fn solve(n: i8) -> Vec<i8>

You are given two integers: a month number (1 through 12) and a weekday index (1 through 7) indicating the day of the week on which the first day of that month falls. Assume a non-leap year with the following fixed month lengths: January 31, February 28, March 31, April 30, May 31, June 30, July 31, August 31, September 30, October 31, November 30, December 31. Determine how many weeks the month spans when laid out on a standard calendar grid of seven days per week, taking into account the leading empty days before the first day (based on the given weekday index). Formally, the answer is 1 + floor((weekday_index − 1 + days_in_month − 1) / 7), which is equivalent to ceil((weekday_index − 1 + days_in_month) / 7). The inputs always satisfy 1 ≤ month ≤ 12 and 1 ≤ weekday_index ≤ 7. The output is guaranteed to be an integer between 4 and 6 inclusive.
fn solve(m: i8, d: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn solve(m: i8, d: i8) -> i8

description:
You are given a non-empty sequence of characters representing a user-entered password line. First, remove a trailing newline character if the last character is '
'; if there is no trailing newline, leave the sequence unchanged. Next, repeatedly remove leading or trailing whitespace characters (where whitespace is any of: space ' ', tab '	', newline '
', or carriage return '') until neither end of the sequence is a whitespace character; internal whitespace must remain unchanged. After this preprocessing, determine whether the resulting sequence is a valid password. A valid password is defined as having length at least 5 and containing at least one lowercase letter (ASCII 'a' to 'z'), at least one uppercase letter (ASCII 'A' to 'Z'), and at least one digit (ASCII '0' to '9'). If the password is valid, return the character sequence for "Correct
"; otherwise, return the character sequence for "Too weak
".
Assume the input sequence length is strictly positive.
fn solve(input: Vec<char>) -> (output: Vec<char>)

You are given two integer sizes and two arrays of characters describing the directions of arrows along the borders of a rectangular grid. The first size is the number of horizontal positions (n), and the second size is the number of vertical positions (m). Assume n and m are integers satisfying 2 <= n <= 20 and 2 <= m <= 20. The horizontal array has length exactly n and each element is either '<' or '>'. The vertical array has length exactly m and each element is either '^' or 'v'.

Determine whether the configuration is "disconnected" based solely on the arrows at the four corners, defined by the first and last elements of each array. The configuration is disconnected if and only if at least one of the following holds:
- The first horizontal element is '>' and the first vertical element is 'v'.
- The first horizontal element is '<' and the last vertical element is 'v'.
- The last horizontal element is '>' and the first vertical element is '^'.
- The last horizontal element is '<' and the last vertical element is '^'.

Return the result as a vector of characters. Return ['N','O','\n'] if the configuration is disconnected; otherwise return ['Y','E','S','\n'].
fn solve(n: i8, m: i8, horizontal: Vec<char>, vertical: Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>
fn check_disconnected(n: i8, m: i8, horizontal: &Vec<char>, vertical: &Vec<char>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn solve(n: i8, m: i8, horizontal: Vec<char>, vertical: Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>
fn check_disconnected(n: i8, m: i8, horizontal: &Vec<char>, vertical: &Vec<char>) -> bool

description:
You are given an integer angle in degrees as a signed 8-bit value. Angles are interpreted modulo 360, so any input is normalized into the range [0, 360) before reasoning about rotations. You may rotate the image by quarter-turns, i.e., by 0, 1, 2, or 3 steps of 90 degrees.

For a chosen number of quarter-turns r in {0, 1, 2, 3}, define the image’s resulting orientation relative to the vertical as the angle A obtained by normalizing (-input_angle + 90 × r) modulo 360 into [0, 360). The deviation from vertical for this r is the shortest angular distance to vertical, computed as min(A, 360 − A), which lies in [0, 180].

Return the rotation count r (an unsigned 8-bit value) that minimizes the deviation from vertical. The result must satisfy 0 ≤ r ≤ 3. If multiple rotation counts yield the same minimal deviation, return the smallest such r.

Formally, for all k in {0, 1, 2, 3}, the deviation after k rotations is at least the deviation after the returned r, and in case of equality, r ≤ k.
fn solve(x: i8) -> (result: u8)

You are given a positive integer n representing the perimeter of a rectangle with integer side lengths. Count how many distinct non-square rectangles with positive integer side lengths have perimeter exactly n. Two rectangles that differ only by swapping the two side lengths are considered the same (i.e., count unordered pairs of side lengths). Equivalently, count the number of unordered pairs of positive integers (a, b) with a < b such that 2a + 2b = n. Assume n > 0. The returned value must satisfy the following properties: (1) If n is odd, the result is 0. (2) If n is even and n modulo 4 equals 2, the result is n / 4. (3) If n is divisible by 4, the result is n / 4 minus 1.
fn solve(n: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given four positive integers: the total distance between two endpoints, a vehicle’s fuel capacity, the position of a single refueling station measured from the starting endpoint, and the number of full journeys to perform. Assume the station position is strictly within the segment (i.e., the station lies between the two endpoints). The vehicle starts at the starting endpoint with a full tank. Fuel consumption is one unit per unit distance. The vehicle may refuel to full capacity only when passing the station; refueling at endpoints is not allowed.

Determine whether the itinerary of the specified number of end-to-end journeys (alternating direction each time) is possible. If it is impossible, return −1. If it is possible, return 0 if no refueling at the station is needed at all, or return 1 if at least one refueling at the station is needed at any point.

Input assumptions:
- All four inputs are positive.
- The station’s position is strictly less than the total distance (i.e., it lies strictly inside the route).

Impossibility conditions (if any holds, the answer must be −1):
- The fuel capacity is less than the distance from the start to the station.
- The fuel capacity is less than the distance from the station to the opposite endpoint.
- When the number of journeys is greater than one, the fuel capacity is less than the quantity (2 × total distance − station position).
- When the number of journeys is exactly one and the capacity is less than the total distance as well as less than the station position, the trip is impossible.

Feasibility conditions for any non-negative answer:
- The capacity must be at least the distance to the station and at least the distance from the station to the far endpoint.
- If the number of journeys is greater than one, the capacity must also be at least (2 × total distance − station position).
- If there is exactly one journey, feasibility implies either the capacity is at least the total distance or at least the distance to the station.

Exact behavior on a single journey (if feasible):
- Return 0 if the capacity is at least the total distance (no refuel needed).
- Return 1 if the capacity is less than the total distance but at least the distance to the station (one refuel at the station is needed).

For multiple journeys (if feasible), the capacity necessarily meets the station reachability, the far-end reachability from the station, and the (2 × total distance − station position) bound. The returned value is guaranteed to be in {−1, 0, 1} and does not exceed the number of journeys.
fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, f: i8, k: i8) -> (result: i8)
function_signature: fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, f: i8, k: i8) -> (result: i8)

description:
You are given a shell game with three positions labeled 0, 1, and 2. A token starts at an initial position x. Assume 0 ≤ x ≤ 2. You then perform n moves, where 1 ≤ n ≤ 2,000,000,000. The moves are defined as follows for each step i from 1 to n inclusive:
- If i is odd, the token swaps between positions 0 and 1: a token at 0 moves to 1; at 1 moves to 0; at 2 stays at 2.
- If i is even, the token swaps between positions 1 and 2: a token at 1 moves to 2; at 2 moves to 1; at 0 stays at 0.
Return the final position of the token after all n moves. The result must be one of {0, 1, 2}.
fn shell_game(n: i32, x: i8) -> (result: i8)
function_signature: fn shell_game(n: i32, x: i8) -> (result: i8)

description:
You are given exactly three integers. Assume all three values are strictly positive and each is at most 1500. Return an array of characters that spells either "YES" or "NO" according to the following rule.

First, consider the three numbers sorted in nondecreasing order (call them a ≤ b ≤ c). Return "YES" if and only if at least one of the following conditions holds:
- a = 1 (i.e., at least one of the numbers is 1), or
- a = 2 and b = 2 (i.e., at least two of the numbers are 2), or
- (a, b, c) = (2, 4, 4), or
- (a, b, c) = (3, 3, 3).

Return "NO" in all other cases. The output must be exactly either the character sequence ['Y', 'E', 'S'] or ['N', 'O'].
fn solve(nums: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<char>

You are given two integers representing a total count and a selected count. Assume the total count is at least 1 and the selected count is between 0 and the total count (inclusive). Return a pair of two integers [x_min, x_max] that summarizes the possible range for the number of "good" unselected items under the following requirements:
- The pair has exactly two elements.
- Both elements are non-negative.
- The first element is less than or equal to the second element.
- Both elements are less than or equal to the number of unselected items (total minus selected).

The values must be computed as:
- If the selected count is 0 or equals the total count, then x_min = 0 and x_max = 0.
- Otherwise, x_min = 1 and x_max = min(total − selected, 2 × selected).

The function must return the pair [x_min, x_max] that satisfies the above constraints and ensures x_min ≤ x_max. Inputs and outputs are 8-bit signed integers.
fn solve(n: i8, k: i8) -> Vec<i8>
function_signature: fn solve(n: i8, k: i8) -> Vec<i8>

description:
You are given a positive signed 8-bit integer as input. Consider the integer-valued function h(x, y) = x^2 + 2xy + x + 1, where x and y are positive integers. Return an array representing a solution to the equation h(x, y) = r under the following specifications:
- Assume the input r is strictly positive.
- The return value must be either an empty array or a 2-element array of positive integers.
- If a 2-element array is returned, it must satisfy h(x, y) = r.
- If r <= 4, return an empty array.
- If r > 4 and r is even, return an empty array.
- If r > 4 and r is odd, return exactly the 2-element array [1, (r - 3) / 2].
A returned empty array is considered valid by definition. Any returned non-empty array must contain two positive integers that satisfy the equation.
fn solve(r: i8) -> Vec<i8>

Given a positive signed 8-bit integer representing an amount of money, return the minimum number of banknotes/coins needed to obtain exactly that amount using denominations 100, 20, 10, 5, and 1. Assume the input is at least 1. The returned value must be non-negative and must equal the sum defined by the canonical decomposition using integer division and modulo: floor(amount / 100) + floor((amount mod 100) / 20) + floor((((amount mod 100) mod 20) / 10)) + floor(((((amount mod 100) mod 20) mod 10) / 5)) + (((((amount mod 100) mod 20) mod 10) mod 5)).
fn solve(n: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given three distinct integer positions on a one-dimensional number line. Each position is guaranteed to be within the inclusive range [1, 100]. The task is to compute the minimum possible total distance required to move all three positions to a single meeting point (the meeting point can be any real number on the number line). Return this minimal total distance as an integer. Assume the inputs satisfy the constraints: all three values are within [1, 100], and no two values are equal. The returned value is guaranteed to be within [1, 99].
fn find_minimum_total_distance(x1: i8, x2: i8, x3: i8) -> (result: i8)
function_signature: fn find_minimum_total_distance(x1: i8, x2: i8, x3: i8) -> (result: i8)

description:
You are given three nonnegative counts representing the available quantities of three distinct colors. A "table" (or group) consists of exactly three items. No table may be monochromatic; that is, a table cannot be formed using three items of the same color. Assume each input count is provided as a signed 8-bit integer and is nonnegative (so each count is in the range [0, 127]). Compute the maximum number of such tables that can be formed from the given counts.

Formally, let the three counts be a, b, and c. The output must equal min(floor((a + b + c) / 3), a + b, a + c, b + c), using integer division for floor((a + b + c) / 3). The output must be a nonnegative integer.
fn solve(r: i8, g: i8, b: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn solve(r: i8, g: i8, b: i8) -> i8

description:
Implement a function that, given an 8-bit signed integer representing a 1-based index, returns the name of the U.S. President at that position in a fixed chronological list of the first 40 presidencies. Assume the input is between 1 and 40 inclusive. The mapping is 1-based (1 corresponds to the first element of the list). The list of 40 names is fixed and exactly:

The function must return exactly the name at the position indicated by the input. Inputs outside the range [1, 40] are not required to be handled.
fn solve(a: i8) -> (result: &'static str)

You are given two base point values and two time values for a contest with two participants. The first participant has a base point value P1 and a time T1, and the second participant has a base point value P2 and a time T2. Assume the following constraints hold:
- Each base point value is an integer in the range [250, 3500], and is divisible by 250.
- Each time value is an integer in the range [0, 180].
For each participant, the final score is computed as S = max(3 * P / 10, P - P * T / 250), using integer arithmetic. Your task is to determine the winner by comparing the two final scores:
- Return "Misha" if the first participant’s score is strictly greater than the second’s.
- Return "Vasya" if the second participant’s score is strictly greater than the first’s.
- Return "Tie" if both scores are equal.
The returned string must be exactly one of: "Misha", "Vasya", or "Tie", and it must correctly reflect the comparison of the two computed scores.
fn determine_winner(a: i8, b: i8, c: i8, d: i8) -> (result: String)

description:
Implement a function that takes two signed 8-bit integers as inputs. Assume both inputs are at least 1. The function must return a pair of signed 8-bit integers (first, second) satisfying the following properties: the second component equals the smaller of the two inputs; the first component equals the total n + m − 1 minus the second component (equivalently, it equals the larger input minus 1). Consequently, the sum of the returned components must equal n + m − 1. The returned values must fit within the signed 8-bit range.
fn solve(n: i8, m: i8) -> (i8, i8)
function_signature: fn solve(n: i8, m: i8) -> (i8, i8)

description:
Given a positive 8-bit signed integer input, compute the alternating sum of the integers from 1 up to the input, where odd terms are subtracted and even terms are added. Formally, the sum is: −1 + 2 − 3 + 4 − 5 + … up to the input value. Assume the input is strictly greater than zero. The returned value must be an 8-bit signed integer equal to this alternating sum. Additionally, the result must satisfy the following closed-form characterization for positive inputs: if the input is even, the result equals the input divided by 2; if the input is odd, the result equals (input divided by 2) minus the input. Here, division refers to integer division on positive integers.
fn solve(n: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given a word composed of uppercase English letters. Assume the input length is between 1 and 10 inclusive, and every character is between 'A' and 'Z'. There are two fixed sets of letters:
- Set A: A, E, F, H, I, K, L, M, N, T, V, W, X, Y, Z
- Set B: B, C, D, G, J, O, P, Q, R, S, U
Determine whether all characters of the input belong to the same set (either all in Set A or all in Set B). Return the string "YES" if this condition holds; otherwise, return "NO". The return value must be exactly one of these two strings, represented as a vector of characters. "YES" must be returned if and only if all characters are contained in a single set as specified above.
fn solve(word: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

You are given the coordinates of two squares on a standard 8×8 chessboard using 1-based indexing. Each square is identified by a row and a column, both in the range 1 through 8 inclusive. Assume both the starting square and the destination square are valid (their row and column are within 1..=8).

Return a length-3 array where:
- The first element is the minimum number of moves a rook needs to move from the starting square to the destination on an empty board.
- The second element is the minimum number of moves a bishop needs to move from the starting square to the destination on an empty board. If the destination is unreachable by a bishop (the squares are of different colors), return 0 for the bishop. Note that if the starting square equals the destination, the bishop also requires 0 moves.
- The third element is the minimum number of moves a king needs to move from the starting square to the destination on an empty board.

Details:
- Rook: Returns 0 if the starting square equals the destination; 1 if the two squares share the same row or the same column; otherwise 2.
- Bishop: Returns 0 if the starting square equals the destination; 1 if the two squares lie on the same diagonal; 2 if the squares are of the same color but not on the same diagonal; otherwise 0 (unreachable because the squares are of different colors).
- King: Returns the Chebyshev distance between the squares, i.e., max(|row difference|, |column difference|).

All returned values are integers between 0 and 8 inclusive.
fn solve(r1: i8, c1: i8, r2: i8, c2: i8) -> (result: Vec<i8>)
function_signature: fn solve(r1: i8, c1: i8, r2: i8, c2: i8) -> (result: Vec<i8>)

description:
You are given an integer n and a sequence of length n consisting only of the characters 'S' and 'F'. Assume n is at least 2 and the sequence length equals n. Define two counts over all adjacent pairs in the sequence: count_SF is the number of indices i (1 <= i < n) such that the (i-1)-th character is 'S' and the i-th character is 'F'; count_FS is the number of indices i (1 <= i < n) such that the (i-1)-th character is 'F' and the i-th character is 'S'. Return the word "YES" (as a vector of characters ['Y','E','S']) if count_SF is strictly greater than count_FS; otherwise return "NO" (as ['N','O']).

description:

Assume n >= 1 and the array length equals n. The returned value must be an integer between 0 and n - 1 (inclusive), reflecting the count of adjacent equal-character pairs within the array.

description:
Given a signed 8-bit integer input that is at least 1, return the minimal number of fixed-size steps of 5 needed to reach or exceed the input. Formally, the output s must satisfy: s >= 1, 5 * s >= x, and (s - 1) * 5 < x. The input is guaranteed to fit in the signed 8-bit range, and the result also fits in the signed 8-bit range. This is equivalent to computing ceil(x / 5) for x >= 1.
fn min_steps(x: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given a positive signed 8-bit integer. Assume the input value is at least 1. Return an integer result defined as follows: if the input is odd, the result equals (input − 1) / 2. If the input is even, let z be the largest power of two that does not exceed the input. Formally, z is a power of two satisfying 1 ≤ z ≤ input and 2z > input; then the result equals (input − z) / 2. The auxiliary computation that determines z must only consider candidates that are powers of two, remain within the inclusive range [1, input] throughout the search, and terminate.

description:
You are given two positive integers representing the counts of two different types (e.g., two colors of socks). Each day you first form a pair using one item from each type until one type is exhausted. The number of such days equals the minimum of the two counts. After these different-type pair days, you may form same-type pairs using only the remaining items from the type that still has items left. Each same-type day consumes two items from the remaining type, so the number of same-type days equals half of the remaining difference between the two counts, using integer division. Assume both input counts are at least 1. Return two integers: the number of different-type pair days and the number of same-type pair days. The outputs must be non-negative, the number of different-type days must not exceed either input count, and the returned values must match min(first_count, second_count) for the different-type days and floor(|first_count − second_count| / 2) for the same-type days.

description:
You are given a character array that represents an input. Assume the input length is at least 2 and that the first two characters are ASCII decimal digits ('0' through '9'). The input may optionally end with a newline character; otherwise, any trailing characters beyond the first two are irrelevant and should be ignored. Only the first two characters are used for the computation.

Define the following mapping from a digit to its "good count":
- '0' -> 2
- '1' -> 7
- '2' -> 2
- '3' -> 3
- '4' -> 3
- '5' -> 4
- '6' -> 2
- '7' -> 5
- '8' -> 1
- '9' -> 2

Compute the product of the good counts of the first and second digits. This product is guaranteed to be between 1 and 49 inclusive. Return a new character array that is the standard decimal representation of this product (without leading zeros) followed by a newline character. The output must contain only digit characters and the trailing newline, and its last character must be a newline.

description:

Return the minimum number of operations needed so that the three values can form a non-degenerate triangle, i.e., after applying the operations, each pair of sides must have a sum strictly greater than the remaining side. The result must be a non-negative integer. If the input already satisfies the strict triangle inequality, return 0.

description:
You are given a single integer input. Assume the input value is an 8-bit signed integer and is at least 2. Determine the winning side based on the parity of the input: if the input is odd, the winning side is "black"; if the input is even, the winning side is "white". Return a string that encodes this outcome exactly as follows:
- If the input is odd, return the string "black\n".
- If the input is even, return the string "white\n1 2\n" (i.e., the word "white", followed by a newline, then the two integers "1 2" separated by a space, followed by a newline).
The returned string must match exactly, including spaces and newline characters. No other outputs are allowed.

You are given a single signed 8-bit integer input that is guaranteed to be at least 1. Compute an output value defined by the following rule: if the input is divisible by 3, return twice the integer quotient of dividing the input by 3; otherwise, return twice that quotient plus 1. Integer division is floor division. Equivalently, the output is floor((2 × input + 1) / 3), which is the nearest-integer rounding of 2 × input / 3. The output must be at least 1.

Given a signed 8-bit integer input that is assumed to be nonnegative (i.e., in the range 0 to 127), compute how many digits equal to 1 appear in its base-8 (octal) representation. The result must be returned as a signed 8-bit integer and is guaranteed to be nonnegative. Formally, for any nonnegative value v, define the count as: if v = 0 then the count is 0; otherwise, the count is the indicator of (v mod 8 = 1) plus the count of the octal-digit-1s in floor(v / 8). The function must return exactly this mathematically defined count for the given input. Since the input is less than 128, its octal representation has at most three digits, so the count is at most 3 and fits within the i8 type.
fn solve(a: i8) -> (count: i8)

Given a signed 8-bit integer in the range 1 to 99 inclusive, return whether it satisfies the following predicate:

- If 12 < value < 30, return false.
- If 69 < value < 80, return false.
- If value > 89, return false.
- Otherwise (i.e., for the remaining values 12, 30–69, and 80–89 inclusive), return true if and only if the last decimal digit (value mod 10) is not 1, 7, or 9; otherwise return false.
Assume the input value satisfies 1 <= value <= 99.
fn solve(n: i8) -> (result: bool)

You are given a position on a standard 8×8 chessboard encoded as a two-character array. The first character is the file ('a' through 'h') and the second character is the rank ('1' through '8'). Assume the input length is exactly two and both characters are within these ranges.

Compute the number of distinct squares a king could move to from this position on an otherwise empty board, considering standard king movement (one square in any of the eight directions) and staying within the board.

- If the position is a corner square (a1, a8, h1, h8), return 3.
- If the position lies on an edge but is not a corner (i.e., file is 'a' or 'h', or rank is '1' or '8', excluding corners), return 5.
- Otherwise (interior squares), return 8.

The result must be one of {3, 5, 8} and must match the classification above.

You are given a positive integer n, a positive integer s, and an array of n positive integers. Assume the following input constraints: n >= 1, s >= 1, the array length equals n, n <= 3000, s <= 3000, and every array element is in the range [1, 3000]. Compute an integer result in the range [0, 998244353) such that result is congruent to itself modulo 998244353. The required behavior is:

Additionally, the output must be 0 whenever every element of the array is strictly greater than s.

description:
You are given an 8-bit signed integer input. Assume the input value is between 30 and 100 inclusive. Define a category function on any integer x as follows: compute r = x mod 4; if r = 1, the category is 'A'; if r = 3, the category is 'B'; if r = 2, the category is 'C'; if r = 0, the category is 'D'. Your task is to return a pair (offset, category) where the offset is an integer in the range [0, 2] and the category is a character from {'A','B','C','D'}. The pair must satisfy that the category equals the category function applied to (input + offset). In addition, the returned category must be either 'A' or 'B'. The expected behavior is fully determined by the remainder of the input modulo 4:
- If input mod 4 = 1, return (0, 'A').
- If input mod 4 = 2, return (1, 'B').
- If input mod 4 = 3, return (2, 'A').
- If input mod 4 = 0, return (1, 'A').
The output must adhere to the constraints 0 <= offset <= 2 and category ∈ {'A','B','C','D'}, and the returned category must be either 'A' or 'B'.
fn solve(n: i8) -> (i8, char)

You are given two positive sizes and two arrays of signed 8-bit integers whose lengths match those sizes. Both arrays are sorted in non-decreasing order. Return "YES" if the comparison holds, otherwise return "NO".

description:

Compute the sum of the five values. If the sum is strictly positive and divisible by 5, return the sum divided by 5 (an integer). Otherwise, return -1. Under these assumptions, the sum is guaranteed to be between 0 and 500 inclusive, so the returned integer (when the sum is valid) will be between 0 and 100 inclusive.

You are given two signed 8-bit integers n and m and an array of signed 8-bit integers of length m. Assume n >= 2, m >= 1, the array length equals m, and every element of the array is in the inclusive range [1, n]. Return the zero-based position of the final task in the array, i.e., return the last array element minus 1. The returned value must be non-negative. Since the array is non-empty under the given assumptions, the result equals the last element minus 1 and therefore also satisfies the bounds: result >= last_element - 1 and result <= ((m - 1) * n) + last_element - 1.

description:
You are given three inputs: an integer n, an integer k, and a list of non-negative integers representing how many sessions each participant has already attended. Assume the following:

A participant is eligible if they can attend at least k more sessions, i.e., if 5 − attendance_count ≥ k. You may form teams of exactly three eligible participants. Compute and return the maximum number of disjoint teams that can be formed from the considered participants. The output is the integer quotient of the number of eligible participants divided by 3.

During the construction, maintain the property that for every index within the already-constructed prefix, the output character at that index equals the corresponding input character. You are given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers. Select and return one value that appears in the array such that its last occurrence position (the greatest index at which this value appears) is minimal among all values that appear in the array.

If multiple values would tie for the minimal last occurrence position, returning any one of them is acceptable (note that ties cannot occur between distinct values because a single index holds exactly one value).

Repeatedly perform such moves until no adjacent equal characters remain. Let the total number of pairs removed over the entire process be M. Return "Yes" if M is odd; otherwise return "No".

Assumptions:

Output requirements:

You are given two signed 8-bit integers representing two values. Assume both inputs lie within the range [-100, 100]. description:

Return a list of the same length containing these values. Formally, for each position, the output value must equal f(n), where f(n) = (n mod 2) if n >= 4, and f(n) = 4 - n if n < 4. The output must have the same length as the input, and each position must be computed independently according to this rule.

You are given a non-negative integer n and a sequence of exactly n moves, where each move is either 'U' (up) or 'R' (right). Starting at coordinate (0, 0), apply the moves in order: 'U' increases the y-coordinate by 1, and 'R' increases the x-coordinate by 1.

Define two regions of the plane relative to the main diagonal x = y:
- Region A: positions where x > y.
- Region B: positions where y > x.
Positions where x = y are considered to be on the border and belong to neither region.

Count the number of transitions between Region A and Region B as you walk the path. A transition is counted when, after performing a move, you are strictly in a different region than the most recent region you were in. The initial move from the starting border (0, 0) into a region does not count as a transition. Time spent on the border does not itself count transitions; transitions are only considered at steps where x != y, and leaving the border into a region is compared against the last region visited before being on the border.

Assume the input size n is non-negative. The sequence length equals n, and every character in the sequence is either 'U' or 'R'. The result must be a non-negative integer not exceeding n. If n = 0, the result is 0.

The output must have the same length as the input.

You are given three counts representing the numbers of items of three different colors. Determine whether it is possible to arrange all items in a single line so that no two adjacent items have the same color.

Assumptions:

Output:

This numeric condition is the necessary and sufficient criterion for the existence of such an arrangement without adjacent equal colors.

You may perform up to x unit moves, where each move can shift either of the two positions by one step to increase their separation, but positions must remain within the bounds [1, n]. Let the distance between the two positions be the absolute difference of their indices. Compute the maximum possible final distance after at most x moves. Assume 2 <= n <= 100, 0 <= x <= 100, 1 <= a <= n, 1 <= b <= n, and a != b. The returned value must be an integer in the range [0, n - 1]. In other words, the answer is min(|a - b| + x, n - 1).

Consider the infinite integer sequence defined for positive indices by a[i] = -i if i is odd, and a[i] = +i if i is even. For each query (l, r), return the sum of this sequence over indices i from l to r, inclusive.

The required output list must have the same length as the input list of queries, and the ith output must equal the exact mathematical sum for the ith query. Equivalently, if S(k) denotes the alternating-sign prefix sum of the sequence from 1 to k, where S(k) = k / 2 when k is even and S(k) = -(k + 1) / 2 when k is odd, then for each query (l, r) the answer must be S(r) - S(l - 1).

You are given an integer t representing the number of test cases and an array of t tuples. Each tuple consists of two non-negative integers and an array of integers. The output must contain exactly t entries. Each result is represented by an enum with two variants: Impossible, or Possible with an associated cost and a sequence of edges. description:
You are given two small nonnegative integers. Determine the winner based on their absolute difference. If the absolute difference between the two inputs is strictly greater than 1, return the string "Alice"; otherwise, return "Brown". Assume both inputs are nonnegative. The output must be either "Alice" or "Brown", and it must satisfy: the result is "Alice" if and only if the absolute difference of the inputs is greater than 1; the result is "Brown" if and only if the absolute difference of the inputs is at most 1.

description:
Given a positive 8-bit signed integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 127), determine nonnegative integers a, b, and c such that 3·a + 5·b + 7·c = n. Return the result as a vector of three 8-bit signed integers [a, b, c] if such a solution exists; otherwise, return a single-element vector [-1]. The output is required to follow these canonical rules:
- If n is divisible by 3, return [n/3, 0, 0].
- If n ≡ 1 (mod 3):
  - If n < 7, return [-1] (no nonnegative solution exists).
  - If n ≥ 7, return [ (n − 7)/3, 0, 1 ].
- If n ≡ 2 (mod 3):
  - If n < 5, return [-1] (no nonnegative solution exists).
  - If n ≥ 5, return [ (n − 5)/3, 1, 0 ].
Additionally, any returned triple must consist of nonnegative components that satisfy 3·a + 5·b + 7·c = n. When a solution exists, the output must match the specified canonical triple for the respective residue class of n modulo 3.
fn solve(n: i8) -> Vec<i8>

You are given an array of signed 8-bit integers representing multiple test cases for a deterministic number game. The first element of the array is a positive integer t indicating the number of test cases. The array contains exactly t additional elements, each a positive integer n describing a single game.

For each n, output the winner as either "Ashishgup" or "FastestFinger" according to the following rules. Let n be the given positive integer:
- If n = 1, the winner is "FastestFinger".
- Else if n = 2, the winner is "Ashishgup".
- Else if n is a power of two (i.e., n = 2^k for some integer k ≥ 0), the winner is "FastestFinger".
- Else if n mod 4 ≠ 2 (which includes all odd numbers and all numbers divisible by 4), the winner is "Ashishgup".
- Else (i.e., n mod 4 = 2), let p = n / 2. If p is "limited-prime", the winner is "FastestFinger"; otherwise the winner is "Ashishgup".

The notion of "limited-prime" is defined as follows:
- p ≤ 1 is not prime.
- p = 2 is prime.
- Any even p > 2 is not prime.
- Any odd p > 2 is considered prime.

Input assumptions:
- The input array length is at least 1.
- The first element t is positive.
- The array length equals t + 1.
- All t game values (the remaining elements) are positive integers.

Output requirements:
- Return an array of length t.
- Each output entry is either "FastestFinger" or "Ashishgup".
- The i-th output (0-indexed) must be the winner for the (i+1)-th game value provided in the input, based on the above rules.
fn solve(input: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<&'static str>

You are given two piles of cookies with non-negative counts and two numbers representing guest requirements. The total number of guests who need to be served is the sum of two groups: regular guests and special guests. Each regular guest requires exactly one cookie (from either pile), and each special guest requires exactly two cookies: one cookie must come from the first pile and one cookie must come from the second pile. Assume the total number of guests is positive.

Return true if and only if it is possible to satisfy all guests under these rules. This is equivalent to both of the following holding:
- The total number of available cookies across both piles is at least the total number of required cookie servings (regular guests each require one cookie; special guests each require two cookies, one from each pile, which totals to regular + special servings).
- Each pile individually contains at least as many cookies as the number of special guests, ensuring that each special guest can receive one cookie from each pile.

Otherwise, return false.
fn solve_cookie_distribution(a: u8, b: u8, n: u8, m: u8) -> (result: bool)

description:

Perform up to the given number of removals as follows: remove one item at a time from the first pile until it becomes zero or until the total number of removals is exhausted; if removals remain after the first pile is depleted, continue removing from the second pile using the remaining count, never allowing the pile size to drop below zero. Return the remaining sizes of the two piles as a pair of integers. Assume all inputs are non-negative and fit within the range of a signed 8-bit integer. The outputs must be non-negative and must exactly match the result of this described process.

description:

The output must always be either 0 or 1, and must exactly match the value specified by the table for the given input. The truth table is as follows: 1 → 1, 2 → 0, 3 → 0, 4 → 1, 5 → 0, 6 → 1, 7 → 0, 8 → 1, 9 → 1, 10 → 1, 11 → 0, 12 → 0, 13 → 1, 14 → 0, 15 → 1, 16 → 0.

You are given two integers. Assume both inputs are within the inclusive range [1, 100]. The result must be non-negative. Equivalently, if first_input > 2 × second_input, return first_input − 2 × second_input; otherwise, return 0.

You are given a list of signed 8-bit integers. Assume the input list has a positive length. If the list contains fewer than three elements, return an empty list. Otherwise, interpret the first three elements as: the container’s capacity (first value), the current amount already in the container (second value), and the incoming amount to be poured (third value). The intended inputs satisfy the following constraints: the second value is at least 1 and at most the first value; the first value is between 1 and 20 inclusive; the third value is between 1 and 20 inclusive. Compute the leftover amount that cannot fit into the container after pouring the incoming amount. This leftover is defined as max(0, incoming − (capacity − current)). Return a list containing exactly one element: the computed leftover as a signed 8-bit integer. If the computed leftover does not fit in the signed 8-bit range [−128, 127], clamp it to the nearest bound before returning.

Given three signed 8-bit integers representing positive quantities, compute a quotient under specific constraints. Assume all three inputs are at least 1 and that the second input plus twice the third input is less than or equal to the first input. Under these assumptions, compute and return the largest integer q such that q times the sum of the second and third inputs is less than or equal to the first input minus the third input. Equivalently, return q = floor((first − third) / (second + third)). The result must be non-negative and must satisfy the tight bounds q · (second + third) ≤ (first − third) < (q + 1) · (second + third). The precondition ensures that the intermediate values (first − third) and (second + third) are positive and fit within the signed 8-bit range.

Define the score of x as the total number of button presses needed to type, in this order, every boring apartment number from the start of the list up to and including x. You are given an array of characters representing an input of either length 3 or length 4. Assume the input length is at least 3. description:
You are given an 8-bit signed integer representing an input value. Assume the input is within the inclusive range [1, 23]. Compute and return the difference between 48 and the input. The returned value must equal 48 minus the input and will be within the inclusive range [25, 47] under the given input assumptions.
fn solve(m: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given two non-negative integers representing available quantities of two resource types. The goal is to compute the maximum number of groups that can be formed under the following rules:
- Mixed groups require 1 unit of the first resource and 2 units of the second resource.
- Pure groups require 4 units of the second resource and do not use the first resource.
The formation process is constrained as follows:
- First, form as many mixed groups as possible, which is limited by both the availability of the first resource and the second resource. The number of mixed groups is min(the first resource, floor(the second resource / 2)).
- Then, with the remaining units of the second resource after forming mixed groups, form pure groups. The number of pure groups is floor(remaining second resource / 4).
Return the total number of groups, i.e., the sum of mixed groups and pure groups.
Assume both input values are signed 8-bit integers and are non-negative (i.e., in the range 0 to 127). The result must be a non-negative signed 8-bit integer.
fn solve(n: i8, m: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn solve(n: i8, m: i8) -> i8

description:
You are given a 3×3 matrix of characters. Assume the input consists of exactly three rows, each row containing exactly three characters. Construct and return a list of characters of length 4 formed by:
- The three characters on the main diagonal (top-left, center, bottom-right) in order.
- Followed by a newline character '\n' as the fourth element.

Formally, if the input matrix is M with indices starting at 0, the output must be [M[0][0], M[1][1], M[2][2], '\n']. The output size must be exactly 4. No other transformations are performed.
fn solve(lines: Vec<Vec<char>>) -> Vec<char>

Implement a function that maps an integer to a specific sequence of characters. The function receives a single 32-bit signed integer. Assume the input value is within the inclusive range [1, 3000]. The function must return a vector of exactly four characters according to the following rule:
- If the input is less than 1200, return the characters 'A', 'B', 'C', followed by a newline character.
- Otherwise (input greater than or equal to 1200), return the characters 'A', 'R', 'C', followed by a newline character.
No other outputs are permitted. The result must strictly match one of the two sequences described above.
fn solve(x: i32) -> (result: Vec<char>)

You are given an integer n and an array of length n consisting only of the characters 'I' and 'D'. Assume 1 <= n <= 100, the array length equals n, and every element is either 'I' or 'D'. Start with a running counter at 0 and process the array from left to right: increment the counter by 1 for 'I', and decrement it by 1 for 'D'. Compute and return the maximum value attained by this running counter at any point during the scan, including the initial value 0. The returned value is guaranteed to be non-negative.
fn solve(n: i8, s: Vec<char>) -> i8
function_signature: fn solve(n: i8, s: Vec<char>) -> i8

description:
You are given three integers representing colors. Each input value is guaranteed to be within the inclusive range [1, 100]. Compute and return the number of distinct values among the three inputs. The output must be an integer in the range [1, 3] with the following precise interpretation: return 1 if all three input values are equal; return 2 if exactly two inputs are equal and the remaining one is different; return 3 if all three inputs are pairwise different. Assume the input values satisfy the stated range constraint. The result must be consistent with these equivalences: the result equals 1 if and only if all three are the same; equals 2 if and only if exactly two are the same; equals 3 if and only if all are different.
fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, c: i8) -> (result: i8)
function_signature: fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, c: i8) -> (result: i8)

description:
Given an input sequence of characters, construct a new sequence by updating the year prefix from "2017" to "2018" while preserving the remaining characters.

Assumptions:
- The input sequence length is exactly 10 characters.
- The input sequence begins with the four characters "2", "0", "1", "7" (using 0-based indexing, positions 0..3).

Required output properties:
- The returned sequence length is exactly 10.
- The first four characters of the returned sequence are "2", "0", "1", "8".
- For positions 4 through 9 (0-based indexing), the returned sequence must be identical to the corresponding positions in the input sequence.
- Equivalently, the returned sequence equals the concatenation of the four-character sequence ["2","0","1","8"] with the sub-sequence of the input starting at index 4 and extending to the end.

Your task is to implement a function that, under the above assumptions, returns a sequence satisfying all the listed properties. The rest of the characters beyond the year prefix must remain unchanged from the input to the output.

The function should operate on a vector of characters and produce a vector of characters as the result, adhering to the specified constraints and properties of the transformation.

fn solve(date_str: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given two arrays of characters representing binary numbers, return a comparison result indicating their numeric order. Each array encodes a non-negative integer in base 2 and must satisfy all of the following:
- Every element is either the character '0' or '1'.
- The array is non-empty.
- The representation is canonical: either the array has length 1 and contains '0' (representing zero), or its first character is '1' (no leading zeros for positive numbers).
Return -1 if the first number is smaller than the second, 0 if both numbers are equal, and 1 if the first number is larger. The numeric value of a binary array is defined in the standard way (most significant bit first).
Assume the inputs always satisfy the above constraints.
fn compare(s1: Vec<char>, s2: Vec<char>) -> (res: i32)
function_signature: fn compare(s1: Vec<char>, s2: Vec<char>) -> (res: i32)

description:
Implement a function that, given a non-negative size, returns a vector of characters consisting entirely of the digit '0'. The returned sequence must satisfy the following properties: its length equals the input size; it is a valid bit string (every character is either '0' or '1'); all characters are '0' (a vacuous condition for the empty sequence); and when the sequence is interpreted as a binary number with the most significant bit first, its integer value is 0. For clarity, the binary interpretation uses the following semantics: the empty sequence maps to 0; appending '0' to a sequence multiplies its value by 2; appending '1' multiplies its value by 2 and adds 1. Assume the input size is given as an unsigned integer (non-negative).
fn zeros(n: usize) -> Vec<char>

Given a fixed-length array of 10 boolean values, construct and return a dynamic vector of booleans that is an exact copy of the input. The returned vector must have length 10, and for every index k in the range [0, 10), the value at index k in the output must equal the value at index k in the input. Assume the input is a statically sized boolean array of length 10. The implementation must satisfy the property that the output preserves the order and values of all elements from the input array.
fn array_to_sequence(arr: &[bool; 10]) -> (res: Vec<bool>)

Implement a function that takes a read-only integer array and an integer value, and returns a new array equal to the original array with the value appended at the end.
