The input array must not be modified. The returned array must preserve the exact order of elements from the input and then have the given value as the final element. Formally, the output sequence must be equal to the concatenation of the input sequence with a single-element sequence containing the provided value. There are no additional preconditions. If the input array is empty, the result is a single-element array containing the provided value.
fn append(a: &Vec<i32>, b: i32) -> Vec<i32>

Given two read-only sequences of 32-bit integers, return a new sequence that is the concatenation of the first sequence followed by the second sequence. The result must preserve the order of elements from both inputs. The length of the result must equal the sum of the lengths of the two inputs. For every index i within the bounds of the first input, the element at position i in the result must equal the element at position i of the first input. For every index j within the bounds of the second input, the element at position j plus the length of the first input in the result must equal the element at position j of the second input. Inputs may be empty.
fn concat(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>

Given a nondecreasing (sorted in ascending order, allowing duplicates) array of 32-bit integers and a target value, return the insertion index that partitions the array into elements strictly less than the target and elements greater than or equal to the target. Assume the array is sorted in nondecreasing order. The result index must be within the bounds of the array length. Specifically:
- All positions strictly before the returned index contain values strictly less than the target.
- All positions at or after the returned index contain values greater than or equal to the target.
- If the returned index equals the array length, then all elements in the array are strictly less than the target.
This index is equivalent to the lower bound: the first position at which the target could be inserted while maintaining the sorted order. The function must handle empty arrays and any target value. If the target appears in the array, the returned index is the first occurrence of a value equal to the target.
fn binary_search(a: &[i32], key: i32) -> (n: usize)

Implement a function that constructs a new integer array by copying the destination array and replacing a specified contiguous subrange with elements taken from a specified subrange of a source array. The inputs are: a read-only source array, a read-only destination array, a starting index in the source, a starting index in the destination, and a replacement length. Assume both specified subranges are fully within bounds: the source array has at least s_start + len elements, and the destination array has at least d_start + len elements. The function returns a new array whose length equals the length of the destination array. All elements before the destination replacement starting index remain identical to the destination, all elements after the end of the replacement range remain identical to the destination, and the elements within the replacement range are exactly the elements from the source subrange of the requested length. The input arrays are not modified.
fn copy(src: &[i32], s_start: usize, dest: &[i32], d_start: usize, len: usize) -> (r: Vec<i32>)
function_signature: fn copy(src: &[i32], s_start: usize, dest: &[i32], d_start: usize, len: usize) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given a non-empty array of integers, return the maximum value contained in the array. Assume the input size is at least 1. The result must satisfy both of the following properties: (1) it is greater than or equal to every element in the array, and (2) it is equal to the value of at least one element in the array.
fn maxArray(a: &[int]) -> (m: int)

Given a non-empty array (slice) of 32-bit signed integers, return an integer that is less than or equal to every element in the array. Assume the input length is strictly positive. The returned value must satisfy the property that for all valid indices, the result is less than or equal to the element at that index. Conceptually, this corresponds to returning the minimum value of the array.
fn min_array(a: &[i32]) -> (r: i32)

Implement a function that returns the minimum of two integers. There are no preconditions on the inputs; they may be any integers. The function must satisfy the following postconditions: if the first input is less than or equal to the second, the result equals the first input; if the first input is greater than the second, the result equals the second input.
fn min(x: int, y: int) -> (z: int)
function_signature: fn min(x: int, y: int) -> (z: int)

description:
Given a mutable array of 32-bit integers and an integer threshold k, perform an in-place transformation such that for every valid index in the original array: if the original element is strictly greater than k, it becomes exactly -1; otherwise (if it is less than or equal to k) it remains equal to its original value. The array length must not change. The behavior is defined relative to the array’s initial contents; decisions are based on the pre-operation values, and no other modifications are allowed.
fn replace(arr: &mut Vec<i32>, k: i32)

Implement a function that reverses the elements of a mutable vector of 32-bit signed integers in place. After the function returns, for every index i such that 0 <= i < original_length, the element at index i in the resulting vector must equal the element at index (original_length - 1 - i) of the original vector. The vector’s length must remain unchanged. The function must correctly handle vectors of any length, including empty and single-element vectors. There are no additional preconditions.
fn reverse(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a nonnegative rotation offset, produce a new array that is the input array rotated to the left by the given number of positions. The rotation is defined modulo the array length: for each index i in the output, the element must equal the input element at index (i + offset) mod n, where n is the input length. The output array must have the same length as the input. The input array may be empty; in that case, return an empty array. The offset may be any nonnegative integer, and does not need to be less than the array length.
fn rotate(a: &[i32], offset: usize) -> Vec<i32>

Implement a function that takes two 32-bit signed integers and returns them in reversed order as a pair. There are no preconditions; the function must accept any valid 32-bit signed integers. The returned pair must have its first element equal to the original second input, and its second element equal to the original first input.
fn swap(x: i32, y: i32) -> (result: (i32, i32))
function_signature: fn swap(x: i32, y: i32) -> (result: (i32, i32))

description:
Implement a function that, given a mutable vector of 32-bit signed integers and two indices, swaps the elements at those two positions. Assume both indices are valid with respect to the vector’s length at the time of the call (i.e., each is strictly less than the length). After the function returns, the element that was originally at the first index must now be at the second index, and the element that was originally at the second index must now be at the first index. All other positions in the vector must retain their original values. The function may be called with equal indices; in that case, the vector must remain unchanged.
fn swap(arr: &mut Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize)
function_signature: fn swap(arr: &mut Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the zero-based index of the last (rightmost) occurrence of the maximum value in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned index must be within bounds of the array. The value at the returned index is at least as large as every element in the array. Additionally, for any position strictly after the returned index, the element there is strictly smaller than the returned value (i.e., there is no equal maximum to the right of the returned index).
fn mlast_maximum(v: &[i32]) -> (i: usize)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether all elements are identical. The function must return true for an empty array and for a single-element array. For a general array, the result must be true if and only if, for every pair of valid indices i and j, the element at index i equals the element at index j; otherwise, it must be false. There are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn mall_equal1(v: &[i32]) -> bool

You are given a mutable vector of 32-bit signed integers and two integer values, x and y. Modify the vector in place so that for every index within the vector’s original length, the final value at that index is y if and only if the original value at that index was equal to x; otherwise, the final value at that index must remain exactly the same as it was originally. There are no additional preconditions on the inputs. The modification must be in place and must not alter the contents of positions other than by replacing occurrences of x with y. Formally, for every index k in the range [0, original_length), if the original element at k equals x, then after the operation the element at k equals y; if the original element at k does not equal x, then after the operation the element at k equals the original element at k.
fn replace(v: &mut Vec<i32>, x: i32, y: i32)
function_signature: fn replace(v: &mut Vec<i32>, x: i32, y: i32)

description:
You are given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers and an index within the array bounds. Determine whether this index acts as a strict partition barrier: every element at indices less than or equal to the given index is strictly smaller than every element at indices greater than the given index. Return true exactly when this property holds; otherwise, return false.

Assumptions:
- The array length is positive.
- The index is a valid position in the array (strictly less than the length).

Interpretation of the property:
- The property evaluates all pairs of indices (k, l) such that k is at most the given index and l is strictly greater than the given index and still within the array. For the property to hold, the value at k must be strictly less than the value at l for all such pairs.
- If there are no indices greater than the given index (i.e., the given index is the last element), the condition is vacuously true and the function should return true.

Helper functionality semantics (if used):
- A helper that, given an array and an end index less than the array length, returns the maximum value along with an index where it occurs within the prefix [0..=end]. The returned index must lie within [0..=end], the returned value must equal the array value at that index, and every element in the prefix must be less than or equal to the returned value.
- A helper that, given an array and a start index less than the array length, returns the minimum value along with an index where it occurs within the suffix [start..n-1]. The returned index must lie within [start..n-1], the returned value must equal the array value at that index, and every element in the suffix must be greater than or equal to the returned value.

Equivalently, the barrier condition holds if and only if the maximum in the prefix up to the given index is strictly less than the minimum in the suffix starting at the next index.
fn find_max_in_prefix(v: &[i32], end: usize) -> (res: (i32, usize))
fn find_min_in_suffix(v: &[i32], start: usize) -> (res: (i32, usize))
fn barrier(v: &[i32], p: usize) -> (b: bool)

Implement a function that splits a given array of 32-bit signed integers into two arrays. The output must satisfy all of the following properties:
- The concatenation of the first returned array followed by the second returned array reconstructs the original array exactly (same elements in the same order).
- The sum of the lengths of the two returned arrays equals the length of the original array.
- If the original array has length greater than 1, then each of the returned arrays must be strictly shorter than the original array.
There are no preconditions on the input; the array may be empty. Returning two empty arrays is valid when the input is empty.
fn array_split(a: Vec<i32>) -> (Vec<i32>, Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return an index of a maximum element. Assume the array length is positive. The returned index must be within the array bounds, and the value at that index must be greater than or equal to every element in the array. If multiple elements share the maximum value, returning the index of any one of them is acceptable.
fn find_max(a: &[i32]) -> usize

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, compute the length of their longest common prefix. The longest common prefix is defined as the maximum number of initial elements that are equal in both arrays, taken from the start. The result must satisfy the following properties: (1) the returned length is less than or equal to the length of each array; (2) the subarray consisting of the first returned length elements of both arrays are element-wise equal; and (3) if the returned length is strictly less than the length of both arrays, then the elements at the next position (immediately after the prefix) in the two arrays are different. Arrays may be empty.
fn longest_prefix(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> usize

You are given an expression tree over mathematical integers with the following node types: a constant, a variable identified by a string, addition of two sub-expressions, and multiplication of two sub-expressions. The semantics of evaluation are:
- A constant evaluates to its integer value.
- A variable evaluates to the integer assigned to its name by a given store (a finite map from strings to integers). If a variable name is not present in the store, its value is defined to be -1.
- Addition evaluates to the sum of its two sub-expressions.
- Multiplication evaluates to the product of its two sub-expressions.

Implement a pure function that performs a single-step, local optimization at the root of the expression (do not traverse or optimize deeper sub-expressions). Apply only the following peephole rules:
- For multiplication:
  - If either operand is the constant 0, replace the whole multiplication with the constant 0 (absorbing element).
  - If either operand is the constant 1, replace the whole multiplication with the other operand (neutral element).
  - If both operands are constants, replace the multiplication with their product (constant folding).
- For addition:
  - If either operand is the constant 0, replace the whole addition with the other operand (neutral element).
  - If both operands are constants, replace the addition with their sum (constant folding).
- In all other cases, return the expression unchanged.

Prove that this optimization preserves the evaluation result for all expressions and for all stores: the value obtained by evaluating the original expression with any store is equal to the value obtained by evaluating the optimized expression with the same store. Assume standard integer arithmetic (no overflow) and that the function terminates.
enum Exp {
    Const(int),
    Var(String),
    Plus(Box<Exp>, Box<Exp>),
    Mult(Box<Exp>, Box<Exp>),
}

spec fn eval(e: Exp, store: Map<String, int>) -> int

spec fn optimize(e: Exp) -> Exp

fn optimize_correct(e: Exp, s: Map<String, int>)

Given an integer array and a target integer, compute the insertion position of the target within the first 1000 elements of the array. Assume the array length is at least 1000 and that the elements in positions 0 through 999 are sorted in nondecreasing order. The function must return an index k with 0 <= k <= 1000 such that the following two conditions hold simultaneously:
- For every index r with 0 <= r < k, the element at r is strictly less than the target.
- For every index r with k <= r < 1000, the element at r is greater than or equal to the target.
Equivalently, k is the lower bound of the target in the prefix of length 1000: if the target is less than or equal to the first element, k = 0; if the target is greater than all elements in the first 1000 positions, k = 1000. Only the first 1000 elements of the array are considered for this computation.
spec fn is_2_pow(n: int) -> bool
fn search_range_helper(a: &[i32], x: i32, lo: usize, hi: usize) -> (k: usize)
fn search_1000(a: &[i32], x: i32) -> (k: usize)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers and two indices, return the larger of the two elements located at those indices, one from each array. Assume both indices are valid: the first index is strictly less than the length of the first array, and the second index is strictly less than the length of the second array. The result must satisfy the following: if the selected element from the first array is strictly greater than the selected element from the second array, return the selected element from the first array; otherwise (including when they are equal), return the selected element from the second array.
fn max(a: &[i32], b: &[i32], i: usize, j: usize) -> (m: i32)
function_signature: fn max(a: &[i32], b: &[i32], i: usize, j: usize) -> (m: i32)

description:
You are given a mutable array of 32-bit integers and three indices. Assume each index is a valid position within the current array length and that the three indices are pairwise distinct. Perform a 3-cycle reassignment among these positions based on the array’s original contents: the element at the first index must become the original element at the second index, the element at the second index must become the original element at the third index, and the element at the third index must become the original element at the first index. All other elements of the array must remain exactly as they were before, and the array length must not change.
fn swap3(a: &mut Vec<i32>, h: usize, i: usize, j: usize)
function_signature: fn swap3(a: &mut Vec<i32>, h: usize, i: usize, j: usize)

description:
Given two positive 32-bit unsigned integers, return their greatest common divisor (GCD).
Assume both inputs are strictly greater than zero. The result must equal the mathematical GCD of the two inputs viewed as natural numbers: it is a positive integer that divides both inputs, and every common divisor of the inputs divides the result.
If an iterative approach is used, it must maintain that both intermediate values remain strictly positive at all times, that the GCD of the current pair is invariant (equal to the GCD of the original inputs), and that the measure used for termination strictly decreases each step (e.g., the sum of the two current values strictly decreases), guaranteeing termination.
fn gcd_calc(m: u32, n: u32) -> u32
function_signature: fn gcd_calc(m: u32, n: u32) -> u32

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers and a positive prefix length that does not exceed the array’s length, return an index within that prefix where a maximum element occurs. The returned index must be within [0, prefix length). The value at the returned index must be greater than or equal to every value in the specified prefix. Any index that satisfies these conditions is acceptable when there are ties.
Assumptions:
- The array length is greater than 0.
- The prefix length is strictly positive and at most the array length.
Output requirements:
- The returned index is in the range [0, prefix length).
- For every position in the prefix, the element at the returned index is at least as large as the element at that position.
fn findMax(a: &[i32], n: usize) -> (r: usize)

Implement an in-place operation on a non-empty array of 32-bit integers that reverses the inclusive prefix from index 0 up to a given index. Assume the array length is positive and the given index is strictly less than the array length (i.e., it is a valid position in the array). After the operation, for every position k in the range 0 <= k <= index, the element at position k equals the element originally at position (index - k). The array length must remain unchanged. Elements beyond the given index are not modified.
fn flip(a: &mut Vec<i32>, num: usize)

Given an array of mathematical integers and a start index within the array, return the index of a minimum element among all elements from the start index to the end of the array. Assume the start index is valid (0 <= start < length). The result must be an index within the suffix starting at the given start position and strictly less than the array length, and the element at the returned index must be less than or equal to every element at or after the start position. No assumptions are made about the ordering of the array; it may contain arbitrary values, including duplicates and negative numbers.
fn look_for_min(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (m: usize)

Implement an iterative function that takes a non-empty vector of characters and returns a new vector containing the same characters in reverse order. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The input must not be modified. The output must have the same length as the input, and for every valid index k in the output, the element at position k must equal the element at position (input_length − 1 − k) of the input. The solution must use iteration (a loop) rather than recursion. Do not introduce additional helper predicates, functions, or methods, and do not use any executable parallel quantification.
fn reverse(a: &Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

Given a non-empty vector of characters, return a new vector whose elements are the characters of the input in reverse order. Assume the input size is positive (length > 0). The output must have exactly the same length as the input. Formally, if n is the length of the input and 0 <= k < n, then the element at index k in the output must be equal to the element at index n - 1 - k in the input. The input collection should not be modified; produce a new collection containing the reversed sequence.
fn yarra(arr: &Vec<char>) -> (outarr: Vec<char>)

You are given a mutable array of 32-bit integers and a target integer. Modify the array in-place to remove all occurrences of the target value by overwriting elements. Return the number of elements that remain (i.e., the length of the prefix that consists of elements not equal to the target). The array length does not change; elements beyond the returned prefix length are unspecified and may take any values. There are no preconditions on the input: the array may be empty, and any integer value is allowed for the target. The returned value must satisfy the following property: for every index k with 1 ≤ k < returned_length and k within the bounds of the array, the element at index k is not equal to the target.
fn removeElement(nums: &mut Vec<i32>, val: i32) -> (i: usize)
function_signature: fn removeElement(nums: &mut Vec<i32>, val: i32) -> (i: usize)

description:
Given two positive integers, return their greatest common divisor (GCD). Assume both inputs are strictly greater than zero. The result must equal the mathematical GCD of the inputs, characterized by: when the two values are equal, the GCD is that value; when one value is larger, the GCD is unchanged by subtracting the smaller value from the larger one (i.e., gcd(x, y) = gcd(x − y, y) if x > y, and gcd(x, y) = gcd(x, y − x) if y > x). The computation must preserve that all intermediate values remain positive and must terminate, for example by ensuring that a well-founded measure strictly decreases at each step. When the two values become equal, return that value as the GCD.
fn gcd_i(m: int, n: int) -> (d: int)
function_signature: fn gcd_i(m: int, n: int) -> (d: int)

description:
Given an array of integer values and a starting index, return the index of a minimal element within the subarray that begins at the starting index and extends to the end of the array. Assume the array length is positive and the starting index is strictly less than the array length. The returned index must be within the bounds of the specified subarray, and the value at the returned index must be less than or equal to every value in that subarray.
fn find_min(a: &[int], lo: usize) -> (minIdx: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the index of the first occurrence of the minimum value in the array. Assume the input array length is positive. The returned index must be within bounds (0 <= index < length). The value at the returned index is less than or equal to every element in the array, and for all positions strictly before the returned index, the value is strictly greater than the value at the returned index. In other words, if the minimum value appears multiple times, the index of its leftmost occurrence must be returned.
fn get_mini(a: &[i32]) -> (mini: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum value present in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned value must be greater than or equal to every element in the array, and there must exist at least one position in the array where the element equals the returned value.
fn maxArrayReverse(arr: &[i32]) -> (max: i32)

Given an array of 32-bit integers, construct and return a new array that is the reverse of the input. The output must have the same length as the input. For every index i in the range [0, n), where n is the input length, the element at position i in the input must equal the element at position (n − 1 − i) in the output. No additional assumptions are required about the input; the function must handle empty arrays and any values within the 32-bit integer range.
fn reverse(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (a_rev: Vec<i32>)

Given a nondecreasing array of mathematical integers and a target integer, determine whether the target appears in the array. Assume the input array is sorted such that for all indices i and j with 0 <= i <= j < length, the element at index i is less than or equal to the element at index j. Return true if and only if there exists an index within bounds whose element equals the target; otherwise return false. The solution should work for empty arrays and arrays with duplicate values and must adhere to the stated sortedness assumption.
fn bin_search(a: &[int], k: int) -> (b: bool)

Given two non-empty arrays of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array that contains all elements of the first array followed by all elements of the second array. Assume both input arrays have positive length. The order of elements from each input must be preserved. The result must have a length equal to the sum of the lengths of the two inputs, and every element must appear exactly as it occurs in the inputs (no reordering or modification). For example, given [1, 5, 2, 3] and [4, 3, 5], the output should be [1, 5, 2, 3, 4, 3, 5].
fn single(x: &[i32], y: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>

Given a finite array of characters, construct and return a new dynamic array containing exactly the lowercase English vowels from the input, in their original order. The set of vowels is precisely {'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u'}. Characters not in this set (including uppercase letters and any other symbols) are excluded. The input may be empty. The result, when viewed as a mathematical sequence, must equal the subsequence of the input obtained by filtering out all non-vowel characters according to the given vowel set.
fn filter_vowels_array(xs: &[char]) -> (ys: Vec<char>)

Given two read-only arrays of 32-bit integers, construct and return a new array formed by appending the second array to the end of the first array. The output array must satisfy the following properties: its length equals the sum of the lengths of the two input arrays; for every index within the bounds of the first array, the value at that index in the output equals the corresponding value from the first input; for every index within the bounds of the second array, the value at that index in the output equals the corresponding value from the second input, shifted by the length of the first array. The input arrays may be empty.
fn append_array(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> (c: Vec<i32>)

Given a slice of mathematical integers, determine whether there exists a position where three consecutive elements are all equal. Return an index that witnesses such a triple when it exists; otherwise, return the length of the slice.

Formally, define that a triple exists if and only if there is some i with 0 <= i < len(a) - 2 such that a[i] = a[i+1] = a[i+2]. The function must satisfy the following postconditions:
- The return value is either an index less than len(a) - 1 or exactly len(a).
- The return value equals len(a) if and only if no triple exists in the slice.
- The return value is less than len(a) - 1 if and only if a triple exists.
- If the return value is less than len(a) - 1, then the three consecutive elements starting at that index are equal.

No preconditions are required; the input slice may have any length. In particular, if the slice length is less than 3, no triple can exist and the function must return the slice length.
fn get_triple(a: &[int]) -> (index: usize)

Implement a function that, given a non-empty sequence of characters, returns a new sequence containing the same characters in reverse order. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The result must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the range [0, n), where n is the input length, the character at position i in the output must equal the character at position n - i - 1 in the input.
fn reverse(a: &Vec<char>) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Given a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers, update the array in place so that every negative element is replaced with 0. All non-negative elements must remain unchanged, and the array length must remain exactly the same as before the operation. The function must accept any array, including an empty array. Upon completion, for every valid index, if the original value at that position was negative, the new value at that position must be 0; otherwise, the new value must be equal to the original value.
fn zap_negatives(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

You are given a non-empty list of rows, where each row is a list of 32-bit integers and has length at least two. Return the value from index 0 of a row whose index 1 value is minimal across all rows. If multiple rows share the same minimal value at index 1, returning the index 0 value from any one of those rows is acceptable. Assume the outer list has positive length, and every inner list has length at least two.
fn min_second_value_first(s: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (first_of_min_second: i32)

Implement a function that, given a finite array of booleans, returns the number of elements equal to true in the entire array. Define a mathematical prefix-count function for verification purposes: for any integer n such that 0 <= n <= the array length, the prefix count is the number of true values among the first n elements. Assume the prefix count is well-defined for all such n. The returned result must be exactly the prefix count computed at n equal to the array length. Additionally, the prefix count is guaranteed to be bounded and monotonic: for every valid n, 0 <= prefix_count(n) <= n, and for any i and j with 0 <= i <= j <= array length, prefix_count(i) <= prefix_count(j). The function should satisfy these properties, with the returned value being a non-negative integer not exceeding the array length.
fn count_true(a: &[bool]) -> (result: usize)

Implement a function that takes two mathematical integers and returns their product. The function has no preconditions; it must operate for all integer inputs, including negative values and zero. The required postcondition is that the returned value equals the mathematical multiplication of the two inputs. Treat integers as unbounded mathematical integers (there are no overflow concerns).
fn multiply(a: int, b: int) -> (result: int)
function_signature: fn multiply(a: int, b: int) -> (result: int)

description:
Implement a function that takes three 32-bit signed integers and returns the smallest value among them. There are no preconditions on the inputs. The returned value must be less than or equal to each of the three inputs and must be equal to one of them, guaranteeing that the result is a minimum element of the given values.
fn min_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> (min: i32)
function_signature: fn min_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> (min: i32)

description:
You are given two arrays of 32-bit integers A and B. Assume A is non-empty. Construct and return a new array defined as follows: take all elements of A except its last element, in their original order, and then append all elements of B, in order. The result must satisfy these properties:
- Its length equals (length of A minus 1) plus (length of B).
- For every index i with 0 <= i < length(A) - 1, the element at position i in the result equals A[i].
- For every index i with length(A) - 1 <= i < length(result), the element at position i in the result equals B[i - (length(A) - 1)].

Additionally, implement a generic helper operation for arrays of any copyable element type that returns the concatenation of two input arrays X and Y. The concatenation must satisfy:
- Its length equals length(X) + length(Y).
- For every index i with 0 <= i < length(X), the element at position i in the concatenation equals X[i].
- For every index i with length(X) <= i < length(concatenation), the element at position i in the concatenation equals Y[i - length(X)].
fn append_vectors<T: Copy>(v1: &Vec<T>, v2: &Vec<T>) -> Vec<T>
fn replace_last_element(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:
fn append_vectors<T: Copy>(v1: &Vec<T>, v2: &Vec<T>) -> Vec<T>
fn replace_last_element(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given an array of 32-bit integers and a target integer value, determine whether every element in the array is equal to the target. There are no preconditions; the array may be empty. The function must satisfy the following correctness guarantees: if it returns true, then for every valid index in the array, the element at that index equals the target value; if it returns false, then there exists at least one valid index where the element does not equal the target value.
fn all_elements_equal(a: &[i32], n: i32) -> bool

You are given an array of 32-bit signed integers. Return a new vector that contains the even values drawn from the input.

Constraints and guarantees:
- There are no special preconditions on the input; the input length may be zero.
- Every element in the returned vector must be even and must occur in the input array.
- For every index in the input array whose value is even, that value must appear at least once in the returned vector.
- No requirements are placed on the order of elements in the result beyond the above membership properties.
- If the input is empty or contains no even numbers, return an empty vector.

The function may allocate and return a new vector to hold the result.
fn remove_odd_numbers(arr: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>

You are given a vector of character vectors. Assume that every inner character vector is non-empty. Return a new vector of characters such that: (1) the output length equals the number of inner vectors in the input, and (2) the i-th output character equals the last character of the i-th inner vector. The outer vector may be empty.
fn extract_rear_chars(l: &Vec<Vec<char>>) -> (r: Vec<char>)

Implement two functions over read-only slices of 32-bit integers. The first function determines whether two given slices share at least one common element. It must return true if and only if there exist indices within bounds of each slice such that the elements at those indices are equal. Equivalently, returning false means that for all index pairs within bounds, the elements differ. The slices may be empty or non-empty; when either slice is empty, the result must be false because no in-bounds pair exists. The second function determines whether a given value occurs in a single slice. It must return true if and only if there exists some in-bounds index whose element equals the given value, and false otherwise.

Semantics to satisfy:
- Common-element function: true implies there exists a pair of indices in bounds of the two inputs whose elements are equal; false implies all pairs of in-bounds indices have unequal elements.
- Membership function: true implies the value equals the element at some in-bounds index; false implies the value differs from every in-bounds element.

No additional preconditions are assumed; both functions must handle empty and non-empty inputs and be total.
fn has_common_element(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> bool
fn slice_contains(s: &[i32], val: i32) -> bool
function_signature:
fn has_common_element(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> bool
fn slice_contains(s: &[i32], val: i32) -> bool

description:
Given an integer input value and an array of integers (which may be empty), determine whether the input value is strictly greater than every element in the array. Return true exactly when, for all valid indices within the array, the input value is greater than the corresponding element. If the result is false, it must indicate that there exists at least one valid index in the array such that the element at that index is greater than or equal to the input value. For an empty array, return true.
fn is_greater(n: i32, a: &[i32]) -> (result: bool)

Given a non-negative 32-bit signed integer, return its last decimal digit. Assume the input is at least 0. The output must be an integer in the range [0, 9] and must equal the remainder of the input when divided by 10.
fn last_digit(n: i32) -> (result: i32)

Given two non-negative integers representing a cost price and a selling price, compute the monetary loss. Assume both inputs are greater than or equal to zero. If the cost price is strictly greater than the selling price, the loss equals the difference between the cost price and the selling price. Otherwise, when the selling price is greater than or equal to the cost price, the loss is zero. Return the computed loss value.
fn calculate_loss(cost_price: i32, selling_price: i32) -> (loss: i32)
function_signature: fn calculate_loss(cost_price: i32, selling_price: i32) -> (loss: i32)

description:
Given an integer representing a month number in the Gregorian calendar (where 1 = January, ..., 12 = December), determine whether that month has 31 days. Assume the input month is within the inclusive range 1 to 12. The return value must be true if and only if the month is one of {1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 10, 12}; otherwise, it must be false.
fn month_has_31_days(month: i32) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that, given an array of arrays of 32-bit integers, returns a new array containing the first element of each inner array, in order. Assume every inner array in the input is non-empty. The output must have the same length as the input outer array. For each valid index i in the range [0, input length), the output at position i must equal the first element of the i-th inner array of the input.
fn get_first_elements(lst: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether there exists an adjacent pair of elements such that the second element is exactly one greater than the first. Formally, return true if and only if there exists an index i with 0 <= i < n - 1 (where n is the array length) for which array[i] + 1 == array[i + 1]. Otherwise, return false. Assume the input length is strictly positive.
fn contains_consecutive_numbers(a: &[i32]) -> bool

Given a vector of characters, return a new vector of the same length where each ASCII alphabetic character has its case toggled and all other characters remain unchanged. Specifically: for each index, if the input character is an ASCII lowercase letter (code points 97 through 122, i.e., 'a' to 'z'), the output character is the corresponding uppercase letter whose ASCII code is exactly 32 less than the input's. If the input character is an ASCII uppercase letter (code points 65 through 90, i.e., 'A' to 'Z'), the output character is the corresponding lowercase letter whose ASCII code is exactly 32 greater than the input's. If the input character is not an ASCII letter, the output character equals the input character. The output vector must have the same length as the input, and this per-index property must hold for every position.
fn toggle_case(s: Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the array is sorted in nondecreasing order. Return a boolean value. Assume the input size is positive. The result must satisfy the following properties: it is true if and only if every element at a lower index is less than or equal to every element at a higher index; and if the result is false, then there exists at least one pair of indices i < j such that the element at index i is greater than the element at index j.
fn is_sorted(a: &[i32]) -> (sorted: bool)

Given a non-negative 64-bit unsigned integer, compute the factorial of its last decimal digit. The last digit is defined as the remainder when dividing the input by 10. The factorial function is defined for non-negative integers with factorial(0) = 1 and factorial(k) = k × factorial(k−1) for k ≥ 1. The function must satisfy the following specification: the returned value equals factorial(last_digit). Assume the input is non-negative. Since the last digit is in the range 0 through 9, the result is at most 362,880 and fits within a 64-bit unsigned integer.
fn factorial_of_last_digit(n: u64) -> (fact: u64)
    requires n >= 0
    ensures fact == factorial((n % 10) as int)

Design a function that takes a read-only slice of 32-bit integers and returns a new vector containing exactly the same elements in the same order. There are no preconditions on the input; the slice may be empty or of any length. The result must satisfy the following properties: (1) the returned vector has the same length as the input slice, and (2) for every valid index, the element at that index in the returned vector is equal to the corresponding element in the input slice. In other words, the function must produce an exact copy of the input slice as a newly allocated vector.
fn array_to_seq(a: &[i32]) -> (s: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, perform an in-place operation that swaps the first element with the last element. Assume the input length is at least 1. After the operation, the length of the array must remain unchanged. The new first element must be equal to the original last element, and the new last element must be equal to the original first element. All elements strictly between the first and last positions must remain unchanged.
fn swap_first_and_last(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Implement a function that takes an array of 32-bit signed integers and an index, and returns a new array with the element at that index removed. Assume the index is strictly less than the length of the input array (so the array is non-empty). The returned array must satisfy all of the following properties: its length is exactly one less than the input array’s length; for every position i such that 0 <= i < index, the element at position i in the result is equal to the element at position i in the input; for every position i such that index <= i < result_length, the element at position i in the result is equal to the element at position i + 1 in the input. The input array is not modified and the relative order of the remaining elements is preserved.
fn remove_element(s: &Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> (v: Vec<i32>)

You are given two integer arrays of equal length. Assume the common length is positive and each array is sorted in nondecreasing order (every element is less than or equal to its successor). Define the output "median" as follows:
- If the common length is odd, return the middle element of the first array.
- If the common length is even, return the integer average of the last element of the lower half of the first array and the first element of the second array. Use integer division semantics (i.e., the average is computed as (x + y) / 2 with rounding down for negative sums).
Return this single integer value.
fn find_median(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> (median: i32)

Implement a function that, given a mutable vector of 32-bit integers, swaps the first and last elements in place. Assume the input vector is non-empty. The function must not change the overall length of the vector. After the operation, the new first element must equal the original last element, and the new last element must equal the original first element. All elements strictly between the first and last positions must remain unchanged.
fn swap_first_and_last(a: &mut Vec<i32>)

Given a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers and a prefix length, reverse the first prefix_length elements of the array in place while leaving all elements from index prefix_length onward unchanged. Assume the prefix length is at least 2 and at most the length of the array. The operation must not change the overall length of the array. Formally: for every index i with 0 ≤ i < prefix_length, the element at position i in the output equals the element at position (prefix_length − 1 − i) in the original array; for every index i with prefix_length ≤ i < array_length, the element at position i in the output equals the element at position i in the original array.
fn reverse_upto_k(s: &mut Vec<i32>, k: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit integers representing a binary heap in level-order (array-based heap representation), determine whether it satisfies the min-heap property. Assume the input size is positive. A min-heap requires that for every parent index i with 0 <= i < floor(n/2), where n is the array length, the parent value is less than or equal to each existing child: the left child at index 2i + 1 (if within bounds) and the right child at index 2i + 2 (if within bounds). Return true if and only if all such parent-child comparisons satisfy these constraints. Return false if and only if there exists at least one parent index i in this range such that an existing child has a value strictly less than its parent. The function should not be invoked on an empty array.
fn is_min_heap(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit integers, determine whether the array contains only one distinct value. Return true if and only if every element in the array is equal to every other element. The input array may be empty; treat empty and single-element arrays as having only one distinct value and return true in these cases. The function must satisfy the following correctness properties: if it returns true, then for all pairs of indices within bounds, the elements at those indices are equal; if it returns false, then there exist two indices within bounds whose elements differ.
fn has_only_one_distinct_element(a: &[i32]) -> bool

Implement a function that determines whether a given month has 30 days. The input is an integer representing a month number. Assume the input month number is between 1 and 12 inclusive. The function must return true if and only if the month is April (4), June (6), September (9), or November (11). For all other valid month numbers, the function must return false.
fn is_month_with_30_days(month: i32) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit integers, return a pair of indices (i, j) where i is the index of the first even element and j is the index of the first odd element when scanning from left to right. Assume the array length is at least 2. Also assume the array contains at least one even number and at least one odd number. The output must satisfy the following properties:
- The indices i and j are valid zero-based indices into the array.
- The element at index i is even, and for every index k with 0 <= k < i, the element at k is odd (i.e., i is the smallest index of an even element).
- The element at index j is odd, and for every index k with 0 <= k < j, the element at k is even (i.e., j is the smallest index of an odd element).
fn first_even_odd_indices(lst: Vec<i32>) -> (usize, usize)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers using 0-based indexing, determine whether every element at an even index is an even number. Return a boolean value that is true if and only if, for all indices i with 0 <= i < n (where n is the array length), if i is even (i mod 2 equals 0), then the element at position i is even (its value mod 2 equals 0). The array may be empty; odd-indexed elements are unconstrained.
fn is_even_at_index_even(lst: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Given three 32-bit signed integers, return an integer indicating how many of the inputs are equal. The result must satisfy the following properties:
- The output is between 0 and 3 inclusive.
- Return 3 if and only if all three inputs are equal.
- Return 2 if and only if exactly two of the inputs are equal and the remaining one is different (this includes any of the three possible pairings).
- Return 1 if and only if all three inputs are pairwise distinct.
Assume no additional constraints on the inputs.
fn count_equal_numbers(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> (count: i32)
function_signature: fn count_equal_numbers(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> (count: i32)

description:
Given an array of 32-bit integers, determine whether it contains any odd element and, if so, return the index of the first odd element. An integer is odd if its remainder when divided by 2 is not zero.

Return a pair (found, index), where:
- If found is false, then every element in the array is even. The index value may be arbitrary (the reference implementation returns 0).
- If found is true, then index is within the array bounds, the element at position index is odd, and every position strictly before index contains an even element (i.e., there is no odd element earlier in the array).

There are no preconditions on the input; the array may be empty.
fn find_first_odd(a: &[i32]) -> (bool, usize)

Given an integer array and two indices specifying a half-open subarray range [from, to), return an index of a minimum element within that subarray. Assume the subarray is non-empty and within bounds: 0 <= from < to <= length of the array. The returned index must satisfy from <= index < to. Moreover, for every position k in [from, to), the value at the returned index is less than or equal to the value at k, with respect to the array’s contents at the start of the call. If multiple positions attain the minimum value, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn find_min(a: &mut [i32], from: usize, to: usize) -> (index: usize)
function_signature: fn find_min(a: &mut [i32], from: usize, to: usize) -> (index: usize)

description:
Given a non-empty array of integers and two indices defining a half-open subarray, return the index of a minimum element within that subarray.

Assume the following preconditions hold:
- The array length is greater than 0.
- Two indices s and e are provided such that 0 <= s < e <= array length.

The function must return an index m that satisfies:
- m is within the half-open interval [s, e).
- For every index k in [s, e), the element at position m is less than or equal to the element at position k.

If multiple positions attain the minimum value in the specified range, returning any one of them is acceptable. The function reads the array and does not modify it.
fn find_min_index(a: &[int], s: usize, e: usize) -> (min_i: usize)
function_signature: fn find_min_index(a: &[int], s: usize, e: usize) -> (min_i: usize)

description:
You are given a mutable array of characters and three nonnegative integers: a prefix length l, a start index at, and a count p. Assume the following preconditions hold:
- 0 <= l <= current length of the array.
- 0 <= at and 0 <= p with at + p <= l.
Modify the array in place so that its final contents are exactly the concatenation of:
- the original characters at indices [0, at), followed by
- the original characters at indices [at + p, l).
In other words, delete p characters starting at index at from within the first l characters of the array, and remove all characters with original indices >= l. The resulting length must be l - p.
fn delete(line: &mut Vec<char>, l: usize, at: usize, p: usize)
function_signature: fn delete(line: &mut Vec<char>, l: usize, at: usize, p: usize)

description:
Given an integer array and three distinct indices into it, return the pair of indices corresponding to the two highest-valued elements among those three, ordered so that the value at the first returned index is greater than or equal to the value at the second returned index. Assume the array length is at least 20 and strictly less than 50, and that all three provided indices are within bounds and pairwise distinct. The result must be two of the three input indices, and there must exist a third index among the original three such that the values at the returned indices and this remaining index form a non-increasing sequence. In case of ties, any choice consistent with the non-increasing requirement is acceptable.
fn torneo(valores: &Vec<int>, i: usize, j: usize, k: usize) -> (res: (usize, usize))
function_signature: fn torneo(valores: &Vec<int>, i: usize, j: usize, k: usize) -> (res: (usize, usize))

description:
Given an array of 8-bit signed integers and a delimiter value of the same type, construct a new array that inserts the delimiter between every pair of consecutive elements of the input. Assume any array (including empty) and any delimiter are valid inputs. If the input array has length 0 or 1, return it unchanged. Otherwise, the result must have length equal to 2 * n - 1 where n is the input length; every element at even indices in the result (0, 2, 4, ...) must equal the corresponding element from the input (preserving order), and every element at odd indices in the result (1, 3, 5, ...) must equal the delimiter. No delimiter should be added at the beginning or end beyond those positions between consecutive input elements.
fn insert_delimiter(numbers: Vec<i8>, delimiter: i8) -> (result: Vec<i8>)
function_signature: fn insert_delimiter(numbers: Vec<i8>, delimiter: i8) -> (result: Vec<i8>)

description:
Given two arrays of characters that represent binary strings, compute their bitwise XOR, element by element, and return the resulting binary string as an array of characters. Assume both input arrays have the same length. Assume every character in each input is either '0' or '1'. The output array must have the same length as the inputs and must contain only '0' or '1'. For each index i from 0 to length−1, the output at position i is '0' if the two input characters at position i are equal, and '1' if they are different. Empty inputs are allowed as long as both are empty.
fn string_xor(a: Vec<char>, b: Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>
function_signature: fn string_xor(a: Vec<char>, b: Vec<char>) -> Vec<char>

description:
Given a finite list of character sequences, return the longest sequence. If the input list is empty, return None. Otherwise, return Some of a sequence that is exactly equal (in content) to one of the input sequences and satisfies all of the following properties:
- Its length is greater than or equal to the length of every sequence in the input.
- Among all sequences that achieve the maximum length, it is the one with the smallest index (i.e., the earliest occurrence of a maximum-length sequence). Equivalently, for every index strictly less than the index of the returned sequence, the length at that index is strictly smaller than the returned sequence’s length.
fn longest(strings: Vec<Vec<char>>) -> (result: Option<Vec<char>>)

Given a finite sequence of characters, construct the sequence of all its non-empty prefixes in order. If the input has length n, the output must be a sequence of length n. For each index i with 0 <= i < n, the i-th element of the output must be exactly the first i+1 characters of the input. For an empty input, return an empty sequence. The output must preserve the order of characters within each prefix and the prefixes must appear in increasing prefix length from 1 to n.
fn all_prefixes(s: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<Vec<char>>)

Given an 8-bit signed integer input greater than 1, return the largest proper divisor of the input. The result must be an integer d such that 1 ≤ d < input and input mod d = 0. Additionally, it must be guaranteed that no integer k with d < k < input divides the input (i.e., d is the maximal proper divisor). Assume the input value is strictly greater than 1.
fn largest_divisor(n: i8) -> i8

Given a sequence of characters, produce a new sequence of the same length where each ASCII alphabetic letter has its case flipped: lowercase letters 'a' through 'z' become uppercase, and uppercase letters 'A' through 'Z' become lowercase. Any character that is not within these ASCII ranges must remain unchanged. The result must preserve the input length exactly, and for every position, the character in the result must be the case-flipped version of the corresponding input character as defined above. There are no preconditions on the input; the sequence may be empty or contain any Unicode characters.
fn flip_case(s: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given a finite list of character sequences and a character sequence serving as a prefix, return a new list containing exactly those input sequences that start with the given prefix. A sequence is considered to start with the prefix if the prefix is empty, or if the sequence has length at least the prefix length and all characters in the sequence match the prefix at each position up to the prefix length. The result must preserve the relative order of the selected sequences from the input. The output may be empty. No additional assumptions are imposed on the inputs.

Formally, for every index in the result, the corresponding sequence begins with the specified prefix as defined above.
spec fn starts_with(s: Seq<char>, p: Seq<char>) -> bool
fn has_prefix(v: &Vec<char>, p: &Vec<char>) -> bool
fn filter_by_prefix(xs: Vec<Vec<char>>, p: Vec<char>) -> (filtered: Vec<Vec<char>>)

Implement a function that determines whether a given 8-bit signed integer is a prime number. Return true if and only if the input is at least 2 and it has no positive divisor in the range from 2 up to, but not including, the input value (i.e., for every integer k with 2 ≤ k < input, input mod k ≠ 0); otherwise, return false. The function must be specified so that its postcondition states logical equivalence between the returned boolean and this mathematical definition of primality for the given input. The implementation should handle all possible 8-bit signed values, including negative numbers and values less than 2, for which the result must be false. Use a loop that iterates candidate divisors in increasing order and maintain the following invariants throughout the loop: the input value is at least 2; the current candidate divisor is within [2, input]; and for all previously checked values j with 2 ≤ j < current candidate, input mod j ≠ 0. Provide a well-founded termination measure that strictly decreases on each iteration (e.g., input minus the current candidate) to ensure the loop terminates. If any divisor is found during iteration, the function must immediately return false; if the loop completes without finding a divisor, the function must return true, accompanied by a proof obligation that the absence of such divisors implies primality under the given definition.

fn is_prime(n: i8) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that, given an unsigned 8-bit integer, returns the number of digits equal to 7 in its base-10 representation.

Assumptions and constraints:
- The input is an unsigned 8-bit integer in the range [0, 255].

Required correctness properties:
- The returned value must equal the mathematically defined count of 7s in the decimal expansion of the input. That count is defined for any non-negative integer as follows: if the value is less than 10, the count is 1 if the value equals 7 and 0 otherwise; if the value is 10 or more, the count equals (1 if the last decimal digit is 7, else 0) plus the count for the integer obtained by integer division by 10.
- Because the input range is [0, 255], the count is at most 3.

The function should terminate and produce a result consistent with the specification above.
fn count7(x: u8) -> u8

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, return a new array of the same length that applies a specific transformation on consecutive triplets starting from the beginning. Let L be the length of the input array and P = L − (L mod 3), i.e., the largest multiple of 3 not exceeding L. For every index i in the range [0, P):
- If i mod 3 = 0, the output at position i must equal the input at position i + 2.
- If i mod 3 = 1, the output at position i must equal the input at position i − 1.
Additionally, for every index i in the range [P, L), the output must equal the input (the remaining suffix of length L mod 3 is unchanged). Assume no other preconditions; the function must work for any input length.
fn decode_cyclic(s: &Vec<i8>) -> (res: Vec<i8>)

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, return a new array of equal length where every consecutive block of three elements (starting from the beginning) is cyclically shifted left by one position. Specifically, for each complete block of three, the first element moves to the third position, the second moves to the first position, and the third moves to the second position. If the array length is not a multiple of three, any remaining one or two elements at the end are left unchanged. The output must preserve the input length. Formally:
- Let L be the largest multiple of 3 less than or equal to the input length (i.e., L = length − (length mod 3)). For every index i with 0 ≤ i < L:
  - If i mod 3 = 0, the result at position i equals the input at position i + 1.
  - If i mod 3 = 1, the result at position i equals the input at position i + 1.
  - If i mod 3 = 2, the result at position i equals the input at position i − 2.
- For every index i with L ≤ i < length, the result at position i equals the input at position i.
Assume the input may be empty or of any length; there are no additional preconditions.
fn encode_cyclic(s: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<i8>

Given an array of characters, determine whether it forms a palindrome under ASCII case-insensitive comparison. Return true if and only if, for every index in the array, the lowercase version of the character at that index equals the lowercase version of the character at the mirrored index from the end. Lowercasing is defined only for ASCII uppercase letters 'A' through 'Z'; these are mapped to their corresponding lowercase letters, and all other characters remain unchanged. The input array may be empty or have any length, and must be handled correctly for all cases.
fn is_palindrome(text: Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of characters representing a lowercase English string, produce a new array where each character is decoded using a Caesar cipher with a left shift of 5 positions. Assume every input character is a lowercase letter from 'a' to 'z'. The output must have the same length as the input, and for each valid index in the array, the output character is obtained by converting the input character to its 0–25 alphabet index relative to 'a', subtracting 5, applying modulo 26 to wrap around within the alphabet, and converting back to a lowercase letter. This implies, for example, that 'a' decodes to 'v', 'f' decodes to 'a', and 'z' decodes to 'u'.
fn decode_shift(s: Vec<char>) -> (t: Vec<char>)

You are given an array of Unicode characters that represents a lowercase English word. Assume every element of the input array is a lowercase letter from 'a' to 'z' (the input may be empty). Return a new array of the same length where each character is encoded by shifting it 5 positions forward in the alphabet, wrapping around at 'z' back to 'a'. Formally, for each position i in the input, the output at position i must equal the character computed as ((input[i] - 'a' + 5) mod 26 + 'a'). The output must preserve the input length exactly.
fn encode_shift(s: Vec<char>) -> (t: Vec<char>)

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers and a signed 8-bit threshold value, determine whether every element in the array is strictly less than the threshold. Return true if, for all valid indices, the element at that index is less than the threshold; otherwise, return false. If the array is empty, return true. The result must coincide with the mathematical property that, for the integer sequence corresponding to the array elements, all entries are strictly less than the integer form of the threshold.
fn check_below_threshold(l: Vec<i8>, t: i8) -> (result: bool)
function_signature: fn check_below_threshold(l: Vec<i8>, t: i8) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given two finite sequences of characters, determine whether they contain exactly the same set of distinct characters. The order of characters and their multiplicities are irrelevant; only the presence or absence of each character matters. Return true if and only if every character appears in the first sequence exactly when it appears in the second sequence. There are no restrictions on input lengths, and empty sequences are allowed.
fn same_chars(s0: Vec<char>, s1: Vec<char>) -> bool
function_signature: fn same_chars(s0: Vec<char>, s1: Vec<char>) -> bool

description:
You are given a list of 8-bit signed integers. Construct and return a pair of lists with the following properties:
- The first returned list is a rearrangement of the input: it contains exactly the same elements as the input with the same multiplicities (i.e., the same multiset) and has the same length as the input.
- The second returned list has the same length as the input and is defined index-wise from the first returned list as follows. Let n be the input length. For every index i in [0, n):
  - If i is even, the i-th element of the second list equals the element at position i/2 in the first list.
  - If i is odd, the i-th element of the second list equals the element at position n - (i - 1)/2 - 1 in the first list.
This definition yields a front–back interleaving order: first element of the first list, then last element of the first list, then second, then second-to-last, and so on. There are no restrictions on the input; the input may be empty, and the elements may take any i8 values.
fn strange_sort_list_helper(s: Vec<i8>) -> (Vec<i8>, Vec<i8>)

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, compute the minimum number of element modifications required to make the array palindromic. An array is palindromic if, for every index i from 0 to floor(n/2) − 1, the element at position i equals the element at position n − 1 − i.
Assume no preconditions on the input beyond standard array semantics. The result must satisfy the following properties:
- The returned value is equal to the number of index pairs (i, n − 1 − i) whose elements are different.
- The returned value is at most floor(n/2).
- If the array length is 0 or 1, the returned value is 0.
- For any non-negative integer c, it is possible to make the array palindromic using at most c changes if and only if c is at least the returned value; in particular, it is not possible with any c strictly smaller than the returned value, and it is possible with c equal to the returned value.
This problem asks for the minimal number of changes, where a "change" can set an element to any value. Only mirrored comparisons are relevant; the middle element in odd-length arrays does not affect the count.
fn smallest_change(arr: Vec<i8>) -> usize

Implement a function that, given an 8-bit unsigned integer input, returns the integer cube root floored to the nearest integer not exceeding the true cube root. Let cube(x) denote x × x × x. The function must return a value r (as an 8-bit unsigned integer) such that cube(r) ≤ input and input < cube(r + 1). Additionally, it must guarantee that r ≤ input. There are no preconditions on the input, and the input can be any 8-bit unsigned integer (0 through 255). Note that for 8-bit inputs, the largest possible result is 6 because 7³ = 343 exceeds 255.
fn cube_root(n: u8) -> (r: u8)

Given a sequence of characters, determine whether it is "happy". A sequence is considered happy if and only if its length is at least 3 and, for every starting index from 0 up to length minus 3 (inclusive), the contiguous block of three consecutive characters at that position consists of three pairwise distinct characters (no two of the three are equal). Return true if these conditions hold; otherwise, return false.
fn is_happy(s: Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Given an unsigned 8-bit integer, return the number of 1-bits in its binary representation (the Hamming weight or popcount). The input range is 0 through 255, and the result must fit within an unsigned 8-bit integer. Since the input is 8 bits wide, the result is at most 8. The returned value must equal the mathematical popcount defined as: popcount(0) = 0 and, for n > 0, popcount(n) = (n mod 2) + popcount(floor(n / 2)).
fn solve(n: u8) -> (r: u8)

You are given a vector of characters. Produce a new vector of the same length by applying the following transformation to each character independently:
- If the character is a lowercase English letter ('a' to 'z'), replace it with the letter that is 4 positions later in the lowercase alphabet, wrapping around at 'z' (i.e., 'w' -> 'a', 'x' -> 'b', 'y' -> 'c', 'z' -> 'd').
- If the character is an uppercase English letter ('A' to 'Z'), replace it with the letter that is 4 positions later in the uppercase alphabet, wrapping around at 'Z' (i.e., 'W' -> 'A', 'X' -> 'B', 'Y' -> 'C', 'Z' -> 'D').
- All other characters (digits, punctuation, spaces, or any non-letter Unicode characters) remain unchanged.
Assume the input size is nonnegative (the input may be empty). The output must have the exact same length as the input, and for every valid index, the output character must equal the result of applying the above transformation to the corresponding input character.
fn encrypt(s: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, return the second-largest value present in the array if the array has at least two elements; otherwise, return None. The output must satisfy the following properties:
- If the array length is less than 2, the function returns None.
- If the function returns Some(value), then value is equal to one of the elements of the array.
- For every pair of distinct indices i and j with i < j, at least one of the two elements at positions i or j is less than or equal to the returned value. In other words, the returned value is not smaller than the second-largest element in the array.
Assume no additional constraints on the input beyond element type and length.
fn rolling_max(s: &Vec<i8>) -> (res: Option<i8>)

Implement a function that takes three signed 8-bit integers and returns true if and only if one of them is exactly equal to the sum of the other two. There are no preconditions; all signed 8-bit integer values are allowed as inputs. The comparison must use standard mathematical integer semantics (i.e., treat sums as exact integers, not as modular arithmetic with overflow).
fn any_int(x: i8, y: i8, z: i8) -> (result: bool)
function_signature: fn any_int(x: i8, y: i8, z: i8) -> (result: bool)

description:
You are given a message represented as an array of characters. Assume every character in the input is either an uppercase letter 'A'–'Z', a lowercase letter 'a'–'z', or a space character ' '. No other characters appear in the input.

Transform the message into a new array of the same length using the following rules applied independently to each character at the same index:
- If the character is a space ' ', the output character is also a space ' '.
- If the character is a vowel, where vowels are exactly one of {'a','e','i','o','u','A','E','I','O','U'}, first replace it according to the mapping:
  - 'a' → 'c', 'e' → 'g', 'i' → 'k', 'o' → 'q', 'u' → 'w'
  - 'A' → 'C', 'E' → 'G', 'I' → 'K', 'O' → 'Q', 'U' → 'W'
  Then swap its case (uppercase becomes lowercase, lowercase becomes uppercase).
- If the character is a letter but not a vowel (i.e., a consonant), swap its case (uppercase becomes lowercase, lowercase becomes uppercase).

The output array must have exactly the same length as the input array, and each position must reflect the rule above for the corresponding input character. The transformation does not insert or remove characters.

Assume the input strictly adheres to the allowed character set, and the transformation is applied sequentially across the array while producing the result.
fn encode(message: Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given two positive 8-bit signed integers representing the inclusive lower and upper bounds of an interval, return the largest even integer contained in that interval. If the lower bound is greater than the upper bound, return -1. If the interval contains no even integers, return -1.

Assumptions:
- Both inputs are strictly positive (greater than zero).

Required output properties:
- If the lower bound exceeds the upper bound, the result must be -1.
- Otherwise, if every integer in the inclusive interval is odd, the result must be -1.
- Otherwise, the result must be an even integer within the inclusive interval such that every even integer in the interval is less than or equal to the result (i.e., it is the largest even integer in the interval).
fn choose_num(x: i8, y: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn choose_num(x: i8, y: i8) -> i8

description:
Given a finite vector of 8-bit signed integers, return a new vector containing the elements of the input in reverse order. The input may be empty. The result must have the same length as the input. For every valid index k in the range [0, n), where n is the input length, the element at position k in the result must equal the element at position n − 1 − k in the input.
fn reverse(s: Vec<i8>) -> (rev: Vec<i8>)

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, determine whether there exist four distinct indices in strictly increasing order such that the elements at those indices are, respectively, 0, 0, 1, and 1. The indices do not need to be consecutive. Assume no preconditions on the array contents or length; if the length is less than 4, the condition cannot be satisfied and the result must be false. The function must return true if and only if such indices exist, and false otherwise.
fn is_nested(s: Vec<i8>) -> (res: bool)

Given an input string, determine whether its last character is a standalone ASCII alphabetic letter. Return true if and only if all of the following hold: the string is non-empty; the last character is an ASCII letter (one of 'a'–'z' or 'A'–'Z'); and either the string has exactly one character, or the character immediately preceding the last is a space (' '). Interpret the string as a sequence of Unicode scalar values (characters), not bytes. There are no other preconditions.
fn check_if_last_char_is_a_letter(txt: &str) -> bool

Given an array of signed 8-bit integers, compute the largest and the smallest values present. The function returns a pair of optional values (max_opt, min_opt). If the array is empty, both outputs must be None. Otherwise, max_opt must be Some(M) where M is the maximum element of the array, and min_opt must be Some(m) where m is the minimum element of the array. The order of the returned pair is (maximum, minimum). The comparison uses standard integer ordering. The input array may have any length (including zero), and may contain duplicates or any valid i8 values.
fn largest_smallest_integers(arr: Vec<i8>) -> (Option<i8>, Option<i8>)

Given two mathematical integers, implement a function that returns the minimum of the two. There are no preconditions on the input values. The returned value must satisfy both of the following: (1) it is exactly equal to one of the two inputs (the function must not produce any value other than one of the inputs), and (2) it is less than or equal to each of the two inputs. If the two inputs are equal, return that value.
fn min(a: int, b: int) -> (result: int)
function_signature: fn min(a: int, b: int) -> (result: int)

description:
Implement a function that searches for a target value in a sorted array of 64-bit unsigned integers and returns an index where the target occurs.

Input:
- A vector of u64 values (the array).
- A u64 value k (the target).

Assumptions:
- The array is sorted in nondecreasing order; i.e., for all valid indices i and j with i <= j, the element at i is less than or equal to the element at j.
- The target value k appears at least once in the array (there exists an index within bounds whose value equals k).

Output:
- Return an index result such that 0 <= result < array length and the element at that index equals k.
- If the target appears multiple times, returning any one of its indices is acceptable.

The function must not access elements out of bounds and must satisfy the stated preconditions and postconditions.
fn binary_search(v: &Vec<u64>, k: u64) -> (result:usize)

Given four arrays of equal length n ≥ 0: two arrays of unsigned 64-bit integers and two arrays of booleans. Assume that for every index i in [0, n), at least one of the two boolean arrays is true at i. Also assume every integer in both integer arrays is strictly less than 1000. Construct an output array of length n such that, for each index i:
- If both boolean values at i are true, the output at i is the integer average of the two integers at i, computed as (a + b) / 2 using integer division (fractional parts, if any, are discarded).
- If only the first boolean value at i is true, the output at i is the integer from the first array at i.
- If only the second boolean value at i is true, the output at i is the integer from the second array at i.
The output array must be exactly of length n and must overwrite any previous contents of the provided output collection.
fn conditional_average(vals_1: &Vec<u64>, vals_2: &Vec<u64>, conds_1: &Vec<bool>, conds_2: &Vec<bool>, avgs: &mut Vec<u64>)
function_signature: fn conditional_average(vals_1: &Vec<u64>, vals_2: &Vec<u64>, conds_1: &Vec<bool>, conds_2: &Vec<bool>, avgs: &mut Vec<u64>)

description:
Implement a procedure that takes a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers and a positive length value. Assume the array’s initial length is exactly equal to the given length value. Modify the array in-place so that every element has the same parity as the length value; specifically, for every valid index from 0 up to but excluding the length value, the element modulo 2 equals the length value modulo 2. The procedure must not change the array’s length at any point.

Correctness requirements:
- Precondition: the length value is positive; the array’s initial length equals the length value.
- Postcondition: for all indices in the range [0, length value), each element modulo 2 equals the length value modulo 2.
- Preservation: throughout execution, the array length must remain equal to the length value.
- If an iterative approach is used, it must maintain that the step counter never exceeds the length value and that all positions strictly before the current step already satisfy the parity property.

fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, N: u32)

Given an integer n, construct a vector containing the first n Fibonacci numbers using signed 32-bit integers. Assume n is at least 2. Additionally, assume the mathematical Fibonacci number at index n is strictly less than 2^31, ensuring that all sequence values up to index n fit in a signed 32-bit integer.
