// <vc-preamble>
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

#[derive(PartialEq, Eq)]
enum NumpyTypeClass {

    IntegerType,

    FloatingType,

    ComplexType,

    BooleanType,

    ScalarType,

    NumberType,

    InexactType,

    Int8Type,

    Int16Type,

    Int32Type,

    Int64Type,

    UInt8Type,

    UInt16Type,

    UInt32Type,

    UInt64Type,

    Float32Type,

    Float64Type,

    Complex64Type,

    Complex128Type,

    ObjectType,
}

spec fn is_subclass_spec(t: NumpyTypeClass, t_prime: NumpyTypeClass) -> bool {

    if t == t_prime {
        true
    } else {
        match (t, t_prime) {

            (NumpyTypeClass::Int8Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::Int16Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::Int32Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::Int64Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::UInt8Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::UInt16Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::UInt32Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::UInt64Type, NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::Float32Type, NumpyTypeClass::FloatingType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::Float64Type, NumpyTypeClass::FloatingType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::Complex64Type, NumpyTypeClass::ComplexType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::Complex128Type, NumpyTypeClass::ComplexType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType, NumpyTypeClass::NumberType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::FloatingType, NumpyTypeClass::InexactType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::FloatingType, NumpyTypeClass::NumberType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::ComplexType, NumpyTypeClass::InexactType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::ComplexType, NumpyTypeClass::NumberType) => true,

            (NumpyTypeClass::NumberType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::BooleanType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::FloatingType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::ComplexType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,
            (NumpyTypeClass::InexactType, NumpyTypeClass::ScalarType) => true,

            _ => false,
        }
    }
}
// </vc-preamble>

// <vc-helpers>
/* helper modified by LLM (iteration 2): reflexivity of is_subclass_spec */
proof fn lemma_is_subclass_spec_refl(t: NumpyTypeClass)
    ensures
        is_subclass_spec(t, t),
{
    assert(is_subclass_spec(t, t));
}
// </vc-helpers>

// <vc-spec>
fn issubclass_(arg1: NumpyTypeClass, arg2: NumpyTypeClass) -> (result: bool)
    ensures

        result == is_subclass_spec(arg1, arg2),

        (arg1 == arg2) ==> (result == true),

        (arg1 == NumpyTypeClass::Int32Type && arg2 == NumpyTypeClass::IntegerType) ==> (result == true),

description:
You are given a fixed enumeration of scalar NumPy data types and their bit-sizes:
- Unsigned integers: UInt8 (8), UInt16 (16), UInt32 (32), UInt64 (64)
- Signed integers: Int8 (8), Int16 (16), Int32 (32), Int64 (64)
- Floating-point: Float16 (16), Float32 (32), Float64 (64)
- Complex: Complex64 (64), Complex128 (128)

A ranking function defines the “kind order” of type categories, where smaller values are preferred: unsigned integers (0), signed integers (1), floating-point (2), complex (3).

A representation predicate defines whether a type can represent a given integer value:
- Unsigned integers represent values in their standard non-negative ranges (e.g., UInt8 represents 0 through 255).
- Signed integers represent values in their standard two’s-complement ranges (e.g., Int8 represents −128 through 127).
- Floating-point and complex types are considered to be able to represent any integer value for the purposes of this problem.

Task:
Given a single signed 8-bit integer as input, return a data type from the provided enumeration such that all of the following hold:
1) The returned type can represent the input value.
2) No type with strictly smaller bit-size can represent the input value.
3) Among all types with the same bit-size that can represent the input value, the returned type has kind order less than or equal to any other such type (i.e., it is the most preferred kind at that bit-width).

Assume all available types have bit-size at least 8 bits.
fn min_scalar_type(value: i8) -> (result: NumpyDType)
    ensures
        can_represent_value(result, value as int),
        forall|dt: NumpyDType| dtype_size(dt) < dtype_size(result) ==> !can_represent_value(dt, value as int),
        forall|dt: NumpyDType| dtype_size(dt) == dtype_size(result) && can_represent_value(dt, value as int) ==> dtype_kind_order(result) <= dtype_kind_order(dt)
function_signature:
fn min_scalar_type(value: i8) -> (result: NumpyDType)
    ensures
        can_represent_value(result, value as int),
        forall|dt: NumpyDType| dtype_size(dt) < dtype_size(result) ==> !can_represent_value(dt, value as int),
        forall|dt: NumpyDType| dtype_size(dt) == dtype_size(result) && can_represent_value(dt, value as int) ==> dtype_kind_order(result) <= dtype_kind_order(dt)

description:
Implement a function that maps a given object to its corresponding NumPy-like scalar data type, with an optional default used only when the object does not carry inherent type information.

Inputs:
- An object that can be one of: integer value, floating-point value, array of integers, array of floats, array of complex number pairs, string value, boolean value, or a generic object without specific type information.
- An optional default scalar type.

Output:
- An optional scalar type determined by the following rules:
  - If the object is an integer value, return Int64.
  - If the object is a floating-point value, return Float64.
  - If the object is an array of integers, return Int64.
  - If the object is an array of floats, return Float64.
  - If the object is an array of complex pairs (each pair representing a complex number), return Complex128.
  - If the object is a string value, return String.
  - If the object is a boolean value, return Bool.
  - If the object is a generic object (no inherent type), return the provided default.

Additionally, the function must satisfy the following properties:
- If a scalar type is returned (i.e., the output is Some(dtype)), then one of the following holds:
  - The returned type corresponds to the scalar type of the object (e.g., integer value -> Int64, float value -> Float64, string -> String, boolean -> Bool), or
  - The returned type corresponds to the element type of the array (e.g., array of integers -> Int64, array of floats -> Float64, array of complex pairs -> Complex128), or
  - The object is generic and the returned type equals the provided default.
- If no scalar type is returned (i.e., the output is None), then the object is generic and the provided default is None.

Assumptions:
- No additional preconditions are required; all object variants are valid inputs. The function must ignore the default for non-generic objects and use it only for generic objects.
fn obj2sctype(rep: NumpyObject, default: Option<NumpyScalarType>) -> Option<NumpyScalarType>
function_signature: fn obj2sctype(rep: NumpyObject, default: Option<NumpyScalarType>) -> Option<NumpyScalarType>

description:
Implement a function that determines the resulting data type of a non-empty list of operands, where each operand is either a scalar or an array and is annotated with one of the following data types: Bool, Int8, Int16, Int32, Int64, Float32, Float64, Complex64, Complex128. Assume the input list contains at least one operand. The promotion rule between any two data types is defined by a fixed rank order: Bool < Int8 < Int16 < Int32 < Int64 < Float32 < Float64 < Complex64 < Complex128. The promotion of two data types is the one with the greater rank. The function must return a data type whose rank is at least as large as the rank of every operand’s data type in the input, must equal the rank of at least one operand’s data type in the input (i.e., the result is one of the maximum-ranked types present), and must be at least as large in rank as the promotion of any pair of operand data types from the input. Only the annotated data types matter; the contents of arrays do not influence the result.
fn result_type(operands: Vec<NumpyOperand>) -> NumpyDType

Convert a scalar data type indicator to its canonical one-character code. The input is an enumeration value that represents one of the following scalar types: 32-bit integer, 64-bit integer, 32-bit float, 64-bit float, 64-bit complex, 128-bit complex, byte string, or generic object. Return the corresponding single-character code string as follows: Int32 -> "l", Int64 -> "q", Float32 -> "f", Float64 -> "d", Complex64 -> "F", Complex128 -> "D", Bytes -> "S", Object -> "O". Assume the input is one of these defined variants. The function must return exactly the mapped code for the given variant.
fn sctype2char(sctype: ScalarType) -> (result: &'static str)

Implement a function that constructs a time duration record from a given 64-bit signed integer magnitude and a specified time unit. The time unit is one of: Year, Month, Week, Day, Hour, Minute, Second, Millisecond, Microsecond, Nanosecond, Picosecond, Femtosecond, or Attosecond. The function must return a record whose magnitude equals the provided integer and whose unit equals the provided time unit. The returned magnitude must be within the range of a signed 64-bit integer (−2^63 to 2^63−1). Assume inputs are well-typed according to these definitions.
fn timedelta64(value: i64, unit: TimeUnit) -> (result: TimeDelta64)
function_signature: fn timedelta64(value: i64, unit: TimeUnit) -> (result: TimeDelta64)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index of the maximum element. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array. If multiple elements share the maximum value, return the smallest index at which this maximum occurs (i.e., the leftmost maximum). Formally, for every index in the array, the value at that index is less than or equal to the value at the returned index; additionally, for all indices strictly less than the returned index, the values are strictly less than the value at the returned index. Assume the input size is positive.
fn argmax(arr: &Vec<i8>) -> (result: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index (0-based) of the first occurrence of the smallest value in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array. The value at the returned index must be less than or equal to every other element in the array. Additionally, for all positions strictly before the returned index, their values must be strictly greater than the value at the returned index. If the minimum value appears multiple times, the result must be the smallest index among those occurrences.
fn argmin(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: usize)

You are given two non-empty inputs: (1) an array of indices and (2) a matrix represented as a list of rows, where each row is a non-empty array of floating-point numbers. Assume the following constraints:
- The matrix has at least one row.
- The length of every row in the matrix is exactly equal to the length of the index array.
- Every index value is a valid row selector: for each position i in the index array, the index at position i is less than the total number of rows in the matrix.

Construct and return a new array of length equal to the index array's length such that, for each position i, the element in the result is the element from the matrix at row number given by the index array at position i and column i. In other words, the result at position i equals the matrix[ index_array[i] ][ i ].
fn choose(indices: Vec<u8>, choices: Vec<Vec<f64>>) -> Vec<f64>
function_signature: fn choose(indices: Vec<u8>, choices: Vec<Vec<f64>>) -> Vec<f64>

description:
Given a non-empty one-dimensional array of 32-bit floating-point numbers, construct a square matrix where the length of each side equals the length of the input array. The matrix must place the input values on the main diagonal in order, and all off-diagonal entries must be exactly 0.0. Formally, if n is the length of the input, the output is an n×n matrix M such that for all indices i in [0, n), M[i][i] equals the i-th element of the input, and for all i, j in [0, n) with i ≠ j, M[i][j] equals 0.0. Assume the input size is positive.

fn create_row(v: &Vec<f32>, i: usize) -> Vec<f32>

description:
Given a nonnegative integer n represented as an 8-bit unsigned value (0 <= n <= 255), construct a two-dimensional array (grid) that contains exactly one row. The single row must have length exactly n. For every valid index i in the range 0 <= i < n, the element at position i of the row must be equal to i (stored as an 8-bit unsigned value). Consequently, the row must be strictly increasing with respect to its indices (i.e., for any 0 <= i < j < n, the element at position i is strictly less than the element at position j). The function should make no additional assumptions about n beyond it being an 8-bit unsigned integer and must handle n = 0 correctly by producing a grid with one empty row.

fn indices(n: u8) -> Vec<Vec<u8>>

description:
Given two non-empty lists of 8-bit signed integers, construct and return a pair of two-dimensional lists with the following structure and properties:
- The first two-dimensional list has a length equal to the length of the first input list. Each sublist in this structure has length 1 and contains exactly the element from the first input list at the corresponding index.
- The second two-dimensional list has exactly one sublist. That single sublist is a copy of the second input list, preserving order and values, and its length equals the length of the second input list.
Assume both input lists have positive length. The returned pair must satisfy element-wise equality: for any valid index in the first input list, the singleton sublist in the first returned structure contains precisely that element; for any valid index in the second input list, the single row in the second returned structure contains precisely that element. The construction should not rely on aliasing from the inputs; all elements in the outputs are copies of the corresponding inputs.
fn ix_(seq1: Vec<i8>, seq2: Vec<i8>) -> (Vec<Vec<i8>>, Vec<Vec<i8>>)
function_signature: fn ix_(seq1: Vec<i8>, seq2: Vec<i8>) -> (Vec<Vec<i8>>, Vec<Vec<i8>>)

description:
You are given three inputs: (1) an array of 32-bit floating-point numbers, (2) a boolean mask array, and (3) a non-empty array of 32-bit floating-point values to use as replacements. Assume the mask array has the same length as the floating-point array. Construct a new array of the same length as the input floating-point array by scanning indices from left to right. For each index: if the corresponding mask entry is false, copy the original element; if it is true, place the next element from the replacement array. When the end of the replacement array is reached, continue from its beginning (i.e., use the replacement values in cyclic order). The result must preserve all original values at indices where the mask entry is false, and its length must equal the input array length.
fn place(arr: Vec<f32>, mask: Vec<bool>, vals: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)
function_signature: fn place(arr: Vec<f32>, mask: Vec<bool>, vals: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
Given an array of 32-bit floating-point numbers, a boolean mask array of the same length, and a non-empty array of 32-bit floating-point replacement values, construct a new array of the same length as the input array. Assume the mask length equals the input array length and the replacement array has positive length. For each index i in the range [0, input_length): if the mask at i is true, the output element at i must be the element from the replacement array at index i modulo the replacement array length; otherwise, the output element at i must equal the corresponding element from the input array. Additionally, every output element at a masked position must be one of the replacement values.
fn putmask(a: Vec<f32>, mask: Vec<bool>, values: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)
function_signature: fn putmask(a: Vec<f32>, mask: Vec<bool>, values: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
Given two finite sequences of 8-bit signed integers (vectors), construct and return a new vector that is the concatenation of the first input followed by the second input. Assume no special preconditions on the inputs (they may be empty or of any length that fits in memory). The result must satisfy all of the following properties:
- The length of the result equals the sum of the lengths of the two inputs.
- For every valid index i in the first input (0 <= i < length of the first input), the element at index i in the result equals the element at index i of the first input.
- For every valid index j in the second input (0 <= j < length of the second input), the element at index (length of the first input + j) in the result equals the element at index j of the second input.
In other words, the returned vector preserves the order of elements from both inputs, with all elements of the first input appearing before all elements of the second input.
fn r_(a: Vec<i8>, b: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<i8>
function_signature: fn r_(a: Vec<i8>, b: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<i8>

description:
Design a function that constructs a slice descriptor consisting of three optional non-negative integers: a start index, a stop index, and a step size. Assume the following input constraints: if a step size is provided, it must be strictly positive; if both a start index and a stop index are provided, the start index must be less than or equal to the stop index. The function must return a slice object whose fields exactly match the provided inputs and must guarantee that the returned object respects the same constraints (i.e., any provided step is strictly positive, and any provided pair of start and stop indices satisfy start <= stop).
fn s_(start: Option<usize>, stop: Option<usize>, step: Option<usize>) -> (slice: Slice)
function_signature: fn s_(start: Option<usize>, stop: Option<usize>, step: Option<usize>) -> (slice: Slice)

description:
Implement a function that, given an array of 32-bit floating-point numbers and a list of indices, returns a new array containing exactly the elements of the input array located at those indices, in the same order as the indices appear.

Assumptions:
- All indices are zero-based and within bounds of the input array: for every position i in the index list, 0 <= index_list[i] < length of the input array.

Requirements:
- The output array length must equal the length of the index list.
- For every valid position i, the output at position i must be exactly the element from the input array at position index_list[i].

The index list may be unsorted and may contain duplicates. If the index list is empty, the function must return an empty array.
fn take(arr: Vec<f32>, indices: Vec<usize>) -> Vec<f32>
function_signature: fn take(arr: Vec<f32>, indices: Vec<usize>) -> Vec<f32>

description:
Given a two-dimensional collection represented as a vector of vectors of 32-bit floating-point values and a signed integer offset, compute the sum of the diagonal that is parallel to the main diagonal and shifted by the given offset. Interpret the offset as follows:
- If the offset is nonnegative, for each row index i, the column index is j = i + offset.
- If the offset is negative, for each row index i, the column index is j = i - |offset|.
For each row i from 0 up to (but not including) the number of rows, include the element at position (i, j) in the sum only if j is a valid index within that specific row (i.e., 0 <= j < length of that row). If the column index j is out of bounds for a given row, skip that row. Rows may have different lengths, and the number of rows may be zero. The offset may be any 32-bit signed integer. Return the resulting sum as an f32.
fn trace(a: Vec<Vec<f32>>, offset: i32) -> f32
function_signature: fn trace(a: Vec<Vec<f32>>, offset: i32) -> f32

description:
Implement a function that performs element-wise selection between two arrays of 32-bit floating-point numbers based on a boolean mask. The function receives three arrays: a boolean array (the mask) and two floating-point arrays. Assume all three arrays have the same length; the length may be zero. The function returns a new floating-point array of the same length such that, for each valid index, the output element equals the element from the first floating-point array when the corresponding mask value is true, and equals the element from the second floating-point array when the mask value is false.
fn where_fn(condition: Vec<bool>, x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
Implement a function that constructs and returns a configuration object representing print options for arrays (similar to NumPy). The returned object must satisfy the following constraints:
- The precision (number of digits for floating-point output) is a strictly positive integer.
- The threshold (total number of elements which trigger summarization) is a strictly positive integer.
- The edge item count (number of items shown at the beginning and end during summarization) is a strictly positive integer.
- The line width (maximum characters per line before breaking) is a strictly positive integer.
- The string used to represent not-a-number (NaN) is non-empty.
- The string used to represent infinity is non-empty.
- The sign-printing control is one of the following single-character strings: "-", "+", or a single space.
- The floating-point mode is one of the following strings: "fixed", "unique", "maxprec", or "maxprec_equal".
There are no preconditions on inputs because the function takes no parameters. The function must allocate and return a configuration object whose fields satisfy all the above properties.
struct PrintOptions {
    precision: u8,
    threshold: u8,
    edgeitems: u8,
    linewidth: u8,
    suppress: bool,
    nanstr: Vec<char>,
    infstr: Vec<char>,
    sign: Vec<char>,
    floatmode: Vec<char>,
    legacy: Option<Vec<char>>,
}

fn get_printoptions() -> PrintOptions

description:
Implement a function that configures print options for numerical array output. Each option is optional; if an option is not provided, it should be left unchanged. The function must validate any provided option as follows:

- Precision (if provided): must be a strictly positive 8-bit unsigned integer.
- Threshold (if provided): must be a strictly positive 8-bit unsigned integer.
- Edge-items (if provided): must be a strictly positive 8-bit unsigned integer.
- Line width (if provided): must be a strictly positive 8-bit unsigned integer.
- Suppress small values (if provided): a boolean flag.
- NaN string (if provided): a non-empty string.
- Infinity string (if provided): a non-empty string.
- Sign display (if provided): a single-character string that is one of "-", "+", or a single space.
- Float mode (if provided): one of the exact strings "fixed", "unique", "maxprec", or "maxprec_equal".
- Legacy mode (if provided): one of the exact strings "1.13", "1.21", "1.25", "2.1", or "2.2".

Assume all inputs respect the above constraints when present. The function returns successfully with no value (unit).
fn set_printoptions(precision: Option<u8>, threshold: Option<u8>, edgeitems: Option<u8>, linewidth: Option<u8>, suppress: Option<bool>, nanstr: Option<String>, infstr: Option<String>, sign: Option<String>, floatmode: Option<String>, legacy: Option<String>) -> ()

description:
Given a non-empty matrix of 32-bit floating-point numbers represented as a vector of rows, produce its transpose. Assume the matrix has at least one row, each row has at least one element, and all rows have the same length (i.e., the matrix is rectangular). The output matrix must have a number of rows equal to the number of columns of the input, each output row must have a length equal to the number of input rows, and for every valid pair of indices, the element at position (r, c) in the output equals the element at position (c, r) in the input.
fn numpy_matrix_transpose(x: Vec<Vec<f32>>) -> (result: Vec<Vec<f32>>)

description:
Given two arrays of 8-bit unsigned integers of equal length, return a new array whose length equals the common input length and whose i-th element is the bitwise AND of the i-th elements of the two inputs. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. For every valid index, ensure the following properties hold for the produced element at that index: it equals the bitwise AND of the corresponding input elements; the bitwise AND operation is commutative on those elements; bitwise AND with zero yields zero; bitwise AND of a value with itself yields the original value; and the produced element is less than or equal to each of the two corresponding input elements.
fn bitwise_and(x1: &Vec<u8>, x2: &Vec<u8>) -> Vec<u8>

Given two arrays of signed 8-bit integers of equal length, return a boolean array of the same length that indicates, for each position, whether the element of the first array is greater than or equal to the corresponding element of the second array.
Assume the two input arrays have equal length. The result must satisfy all of the following:
- The output length equals the input length.
- For every index i within bounds, the output at i is true if and only if the i-th element of the first array is greater than or equal to the i-th element of the second array.
- If the two input arrays are identical element-wise, then every output entry is true.
- For any index i, if the output at i is true and the i-th element of the second array is also greater than or equal to the i-th element of the first array, then those two elements are equal (antisymmetry of the greater-or-equal relation).
- Every output entry is a boolean value.
fn numpy_greater_equal(x1: Vec<i8>, x2: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<bool>

description:
You are given an array whose elements belong to a two-case tagged type representing a 64-bit date-time value. Each element is either the distinguished constant case "Nat" or the case "Valid" that carries a 64-bit integer value. Construct and return a boolean array of the same length satisfying the following properties: for every index i within bounds, the output at i is true if and only if the corresponding input element is the "Nat" case, and the output at i is false if and only if there exists some 64-bit integer such that the corresponding input element equals the "Valid" case with that value. The output must preserve the input order and match the input length. Assume the input size may be any non-negative integer (including zero).
fn isnat(x: Vec<DateTime64>) -> Vec<bool>

Given two boolean arrays of equal length, construct a new array where each element is the logical OR of the corresponding elements from the inputs. Assume both input arrays have the same length. The returned array must have the same length as the inputs. For every valid index i in the range of the arrays: the result at position i equals the logical OR of the two inputs at position i; furthermore, the standard boolean algebra properties of OR hold pointwise: commutativity ((a OR b) equals (b OR a)), identity with false ((a OR false) equals a), domination with true ((a OR true) equals true), and idempotence ((a OR a) equals a). Additionally, if at position i at least one input is true, then the result at i is true; if at position i both inputs are false, then the result at i is false.
fn logical_or(x1: Vec<bool>, x2: Vec<bool>) -> Vec<bool>
function_signature: fn logical_or(x1: Vec<bool>, x2: Vec<bool>) -> Vec<bool>

description:
Implement a function that performs elementwise inequality comparison between two integer arrays. The inputs are two arrays of 8-bit signed integers with equal length. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. The function returns a boolean array of the same length such that, for each index i within bounds, the output at position i is true if and only if the corresponding elements of the inputs at index i are different, and false otherwise. If the two input arrays are identical (all corresponding elements are equal), then the output must contain only false values. The comparison is symmetric: for every index i, comparing the first array against the second yields the same boolean value as comparing the second against the first at that index.
fn numpy_not_equal(x1: Vec<i8>, x2: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<bool>
function_signature: fn numpy_not_equal(x1: Vec<i8>, x2: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<bool>

description:
You are given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers. Assume every element is within the range [-1, 1]. Construct and return a new array of the same length where each output element is an integer in the range [0, 3]. The transformation is element-wise with the following required properties: if an input element equals -1, the corresponding output element must be 3; if an input element equals 1, the corresponding output element must be 0. For input elements equal to 0, any value in [0, 3] is acceptable. The output array must have the same length as the input array and preserve positional correspondence (i.e., the i-th output is determined from the i-th input).
fn arccos(x: Vec<i8>) -> (result: Vec<i8>)

Implement a function that takes a non-empty dynamic array of 32-bit floating-point numbers and returns a new array of the same length. The output array must be produced by applying a fixed unary transformation to each input element, in order from the first element to the last, preserving the original order. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The implementation must process elements sequentially by index starting at 0 and progressing to one past the final valid index, maintaining at every step that the number of elements already placed into the output equals the number of elements processed so far, never exceeding the input length. The input collection must not be structurally modified during processing (its length remains constant). The procedure must terminate after all elements have been processed.
fn arccosh(x: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

Implement two functions:
1) A scalar approximation function that maps a 32-bit signed integer input to a 32-bit signed integer output that behaves like a coarse approximation of arctangent. The output must satisfy all of the following constraints for the given input:
   - The output is within the inclusive range [-2, 2].
   - If the input is positive, the output is nonnegative.
   - If the input is negative, the output is nonpositive.
   - If the input is zero, the output is zero.
   - If the input is strictly greater than 10, the output is at least 1.
   - If the input is strictly less than -10, the output is at most -1.

2) An array-processing function that, given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, returns a new array of the same length by applying the above scalar approximation to each element of the input array. The result must satisfy, for each index:
   - The output element is within the inclusive range [-2, 2].
   - If the corresponding input element is positive, the output element is nonnegative.
   - If the corresponding input element is negative, the output element is nonpositive.
   - If the corresponding input element is zero, the output element is zero.
   - If the corresponding input element is strictly greater than 10, the output element is at least 1.
   - If the corresponding input element is strictly less than -10, the output element is at most -1.
Assume the input array length is positive. The returned array length must exactly match the input array length.
fn arctan_approximation(x: i32) -> i32
fn arctan(x: Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:

description:
You are given a vector of 8-bit signed integers and two 8-bit signed integers representing a lower bound and an upper bound. Construct and return a new vector of the same length by applying the following rule to each element:
- If the lower bound is less than or equal to the upper bound, then each element is "clipped" into the inclusive range [lower bound, upper bound]. Specifically, values below the lower bound become the lower bound, values above the upper bound become the upper bound, and values within the range remain unchanged.
- If the lower bound is greater than the upper bound, then the resulting vector must contain only the upper bound repeated for every position, regardless of the input values.
Assume no additional constraints beyond the usual 8-bit signed integer range for the inputs. The output vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector, and for every index the element-wise condition described above must hold.
fn clip(arr: &Vec<i8>, min_val: i8, max_val: i8) -> Vec<i8>
function_signature: fn clip(arr: &Vec<i8>, min_val: i8, max_val: i8) -> Vec<i8>

description:
You are given an array of pairs of floating-point numbers, where each pair represents a complex number (real part, imaginary part). Construct and return a new array containing, for each index i, the imaginary part of the i-th complex number from the input. The output must preserve the input order and have the same length as the input.

Required properties:
- The output array has the same length as the input array.
- For every valid index i, the i-th element of the output equals the imaginary part (the second component) of the i-th input pair.
- For every valid index i, if the imaginary part of the i-th input pair is 0.0, then the i-th element of the output is 0.0.
- For every valid index i, if the i-th input pair is not the zero complex number (i.e., its real part is nonzero or its imaginary part is nonzero), then the i-th element of the output equals the imaginary part of the i-th input pair.

Assume standard IEEE-754 semantics for f64 values. The function should not modify the input array.
fn imag(val: Vec<(f64, f64)>) -> Vec<f64>

description:
Given two arrays of 32-bit floating-point numbers, construct a new array by applying a fixed binary operation to corresponding elements in the two inputs. Assume both input arrays have the same length. For every index i, the output at position i must be equal to the first input array's element at position i (i.e., the element-wise operation returns its first argument). The returned array must have exactly the same length as the inputs. The function must handle empty arrays as well as any finite floating-point values.
fn numpy_logaddexp2(x1: Vec<f32>, x2: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)
function_signature: fn numpy_logaddexp2(x1: Vec<f32>, x2: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
Given two arrays of 8-bit signed integers of equal length, construct a new array where each element is the maximum of the two corresponding elements from the input arrays. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. The output array must have the same length as the inputs. For every valid index i (0 <= i < length), the element at position i in the output must equal the larger of the two input values at index i; consequently, it must be greater than or equal to both corresponding input values and must be exactly one of those two input values.
fn max_i8(a: i8, b: i8) -> i8
fn maximum(x1: Vec<i8>, x2: Vec<i8>) -> Vec<i8>

description:
You are given an array of complex numbers (each with 32-bit integer real and imaginary components) and a positive integer tolerance. Define a complex number’s imaginary part as “close to zero” if the absolute value of the imaginary component is less than or equal to tolerance multiplied by a fixed machine epsilon (equal to 2). The task is to return a new array of the same length with the following behavior:
- If every element’s imaginary part is close to zero under this definition, return an array where each element has the same real part as the corresponding input element, and its imaginary part set to zero.
- Otherwise, return the input array unchanged.
Additionally, the following properties must hold:
- The output array has the same length as the input array.
- The real part of each element in the output array is identical to the real part of the corresponding element in the input array, regardless of branch.
- If the input array is already real (every element’s imaginary part equals zero), then the output must equal the input.
Assume the tolerance is strictly positive.
fn real_if_close(arr: Vec<Complex>, tol: i32) -> (result: Vec<Complex>)
function_signature: fn real_if_close(arr: Vec<Complex>, tol: i32) -> (result: Vec<Complex>)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, construct and return a new array of the same length such that, for every index in the range from 0 to the input length minus 1:
- The output element is -1 if the corresponding input element is less than 0.
- The output element is 0 if the corresponding input element is equal to 0.
- The output element is 1 if the corresponding input element is greater than 0.
Assume the input size is positive. The output must have exactly the same length as the input, and each output element must reflect the sign classification of the corresponding input element.
fn sign(x: Vec<i8>) -> (result: Vec<i8>)

Implement a function that constructs a Chebyshev polynomial descriptor from a given non-empty list of coefficients. The input is a sequence of 8-bit signed integers representing the polynomial coefficients in increasing degree order. The function must return a structure that contains: (1) the coefficients converted to 32-bit signed integers, preserving the length and the order of the input sequence; (2) a default domain interval set to [-1, 1]; and (3) a default window interval set to [-1, 1]. Assume the input size is positive. The returned structure must satisfy that the domain interval is valid (its minimum is strictly less than its maximum) and the window interval is valid (its minimum is strictly less than its maximum).
fn chebyshev(coef: Vec<i8>) -> (result: ChebyshevPoly)

Implement a function that takes a vector of 8-bit signed integers and returns a vector of 8-bit signed integers. The function must be total: it must accept any input vector (no preconditions). The following guarantees must hold:
- The output vector has the same length as the input vector.
- If the input length is 0, the output equals the input (element-wise equality).
- If the input length is 1, the output equals the input.
- If the input length is 2, the output equals the input.
- If the input length is 4 and the input elements are exactly [0, 1, 2, 3] in this order, then the output must be exactly [-2, -8, 4, 12] in this order.
No other behavior is required beyond these guarantees. In particular, for inputs not covered by the listed cases, any output with the same length as the input is acceptable.
fn cheb2poly(c: Vec<i8>) -> (p: Vec<i8>)

Implement a function that, given a non-empty array of 32-bit floating-point values, returns an array of the same length containing fixed weights based solely on position. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The weight at the first index (0) and at the last index (length − 1) must be 0.5. For every other index in between, the weight must be 1.0. The output must preserve the exact length of the input. Formally, for an input of length n > 0, the result length is n, and for all indices i with 0 ≤ i < n: if i is an endpoint (i = 0 or i = n − 1) then the weight at i is 0.5; otherwise it is 1.0.
fn chebweight(x: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

Construct and return an object representing a Hermite E polynomial from a given array of 32-bit floating-point coefficients. The output object must preserve the input coefficients exactly: the coefficient array length must match the input, and each element must be equal to the corresponding element of the input. The polynomial’s domain bounds must be fixed to -1.0 and 1.0, and its window bounds must also be fixed to -1.0 and 1.0. No additional constraints are imposed on the input coefficients.
fn hermite_e(coef: Vec<f32>) -> (result: HermiteEPoly)

Implement a function that takes two 64-bit floating-point inputs and returns a vector of length two. The returned vector must satisfy the following postconditions: the first element equals the first input; the second element equals the second input. Additionally, the specification imposes a case distinction on the second input: if the second input is zero, the second element of the returned vector must be exactly zero; if the second input is non-zero, the second element must equal the second input. There are no preconditions on the inputs. The function must always produce a vector of exactly two elements that meets these postconditions.
fn hermeline(off: f64, scl: f64) -> (coeffs: Vec<f64])

description:
Given an array of 64-bit floating-point numbers, construct and return a new array that begins with 0.0 and has a length exactly one greater than the input. Assume the input length is non-negative. The output must satisfy: its first element is 0.0, and for every integer index i such that 1 <= i < the input length, the element at position i + 1 of the output equals the element at position i of the input. All referenced indices are within array bounds.
fn hermemulx(c: Vec<f64>) -> (result: Vec<f64>)

Construct a Laguerre series object from a given list of 32-bit floating-point coefficients. The returned object must store the coefficients exactly as provided, preserving both order and values. The object must also carry two interval fields: the domain and the window, and both must be set to the unit interval [0.0, 1.0]. There are no restrictions on the coefficient values or the length of the input list. The following postcondition must hold: for every valid index within the length of the input list, the coefficient at that index in the output equals the corresponding element of the input. Assume standard floating-point semantics.
fn make_laguerre(coefficients: Vec<f32>) -> Laguerre

Given two 32-bit floating-point inputs, construct and return a vector containing exactly these two values in the same order. The resulting vector must have length exactly 2. There are no preconditions on the inputs; any f32 values are allowed, including negative numbers and infinities.

fn lagline(off: f32, scl: f32) -> Vec<f32>

description:
Given two input arrays of 32-bit floating-point numbers and a 2D coefficient grid (vector of vectors of 32-bit floating-point numbers), construct an output array under the following constraints and behavior:
- The two input arrays must have equal length and this length must be positive.
- The coefficient grid must be non-empty, and every row in the grid must be non-empty.
- The output array must have the same length as the two input arrays.
- If the coefficient grid has exactly one row and one column (i.e., it contains a single value), then every element of the output array must equal that sole coefficient value.
- Otherwise (when the coefficient grid is not 1x1), return an output array of the required length filled with zeros.
fn lagval2d(x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>, c: Vec<Vec<f32>>) -> Vec<f32>
fn default_value(c: &Vec<Vec<f32>>) -> f32
