description:
Implement a function that, given three arrays of 32-bit floating-point numbers and a three-dimensional tensor of 32-bit floating-point coefficients, produces an output array of 32-bit floating-point numbers subject to the following constraints and guarantees:
- The three input arrays must have equal length, and this length must be positive.
- The coefficient tensor must be non-empty in all three dimensions (its first dimension length is positive; its first subarray has positive length; and its first sub-subarray has positive length). This ensures that the single element at the first index of each dimension is well-defined.
- The output array must have the same length as each of the three input arrays.
- Special case: if the coefficient tensor has shape 1×1×1 (its lengths in all three dimensions are exactly one), then every element of the output array must equal the sole coefficient value in the tensor.
- In all other cases (when the tensor is not 1×1×1), return an output array of the required length where every element is zero.

You may assume standard contiguous vectors for all arrays and that the function should produce the output deterministically based only on the provided inputs.
fn mk_filled_vec(n: usize, val: f32) -> Vec<f32>
fn lagval3d(x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>, z: Vec<f32>, c: Vec<Vec<Vec<f32>>>) -> Vec<f32>
function_signature:
fn mk_filled_vec(n: usize, val: f32) -> Vec<f32>
fn lagval3d(x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>, z: Vec<f32>, c: Vec<Vec<Vec<f32>>>) -> Vec<f32>

description:
Implement a constructor function that creates and returns a data structure representing a Legendre polynomial descriptor. The inputs are: a vector of floating-point coefficients; a domain interval represented by an array of two floating-point values; a window interval represented by an array of two floating-point values; and a symbol string. There are no preconditions on the inputs.

The function must return an object whose fields are exactly equal to the corresponding inputs: the sequence of coefficients in the returned object must have the same length and elements, in the same order, as the input sequence; the domain interval in the returned object must be element-wise equal to the input domain interval; the window interval in the returned object must be element-wise equal to the input window interval; and the symbol string in the returned object must be equal to the input symbol string.

Additionally, the function must preserve the special interval property: if the input domain interval equals [-1.0, 1.0], then the returned object's domain interval must also equal [-1.0, 1.0]; and if the input window interval equals [-1.0, 1.0], then the returned object's window interval must also equal [-1.0, 1.0].
fn mk_legendre(coef: Vec<f64>, domain: [f64; 2], window: [f64; 2], symbol: String) -> Legendre
function_signature: fn mk_legendre(coef: Vec<f64>, domain: [f64; 2], window: [f64; 2], symbol: String) -> Legendre

description:
Given a non-empty array of floating-point numbers, a non-negative 8-bit integer, and an additional floating-point parameter, produce a truncated array according to the following rules:
- Assume the input array length is at least 1.
- If the integer is zero, return the array unchanged (same length and identical elements).
- The output length must be equal to: if the input length is greater than the integer, then the input length minus the integer; otherwise, 1.
- If the integer is greater than or equal to the input length, return an array of length 1 containing the first element of the input.
- If the integer is strictly between 0 and the input length, return the first (input length minus the integer) elements of the input, preserving order.
Note: The additional floating-point parameter does not affect the required output.
fn trunc_len(n: usize, m: usize) -> usize
fn legder(c: Vec<f64>, m: u8, scl: f64) -> Vec<f64>
function_signature:
fn trunc_len(n: usize, m: usize) -> usize
fn legder(c: Vec<f64>, m: u8, scl: f64) -> Vec<f64>

fn vec2(a: f32, b: f32) -> (result: Vec<f32>)
fn legline(off: f32, scl: f32) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
You are given a non-empty array of 32-bit floating-point numbers representing the coefficients of a polynomial p(x) = c0 + c1·x + c2·x^2 + … + c_{n−1}·x^{n−1}. Construct and return a new array representing the coefficients of x·p(x). Assume the input size is positive. The output array must have length equal to the input length plus one, the first element of the output must be 0.0, and for every index j in [0, n), the element at position j+1 in the output must equal the element at position j in the input.
fn legmulx(c: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

Implement a function that constructs the coefficients of a linear polynomial from two signed 8-bit inputs. The function must return a fixed-size array of two signed 8-bit integers representing the polynomial P(x) = c + m·x, where index 0 holds the constant term c and index 1 holds the linear coefficient m. The returned array must have length exactly 2. Additionally, when interpreting the elements and inputs as mathematical integers (i.e., without overflow), evaluating the polynomial at x = 1 yields c + m. There are no preconditions; any signed 8-bit inputs are allowed.
fn polyline(off: i8, scl: i8) -> (result: [i8; 2])

Implement a function that takes an array of 32-bit floating-point numbers and returns a new array formed by prefixing a single 0.0 at the start and then copying all elements of the input array after it in the same order. The output length must be exactly one more than the input length. The first element of the output must be exactly 0.0, and for every valid index i in the input (0 <= i < input length), the element at position i + 1 in the output must equal the element at position i in the input. Assume no special preconditions; the function must handle empty inputs correctly.

Verification-oriented requirements to guide a correct solution:
- Maintain a loop that processes the input from left to right, and use a counter representing how many elements have been copied so far. The counter must always range between 0 and the input length (inclusive).
- At every loop iteration, the partially constructed output must have length equal to the counter plus one (accounting for the initial 0.0), and its first element must be 0.0.
- For every position k that has already been processed (0 <= k < processed count), the element at position k + 1 in the output must equal the element at position k in the input.
- The loop must be proven terminating by a variant that strictly decreases, such as the difference between the input length and the number of elements processed.

The function should be linear-time and allocate a new output array. Preserve the order of all input elements and do not modify the input.
fn polymulx(c: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return a 2-element array containing the smallest value and the largest value in the input. The first element of the output must be less than or equal to the second element. Every element of the input must lie within the inclusive range defined by the returned values. Additionally, both returned values must be values that appear in the input array. Assume the input size is positive.
fn getdomain(x: Vec<i8>) -> (result: Vec<i8>)

Implement a function that initializes a bit generator state from an optional 64-bit unsigned seed. The function must return a state object containing two fields: the original seed and an internal 64-bit unsigned state. The following properties must hold for the returned object:
- The seed field equals the input seed.
- If a seed is provided, the internal state must be a non-zero 64-bit unsigned integer.
- If no seed is provided, the internal state must be exactly zero.
Assume no additional constraints on the input beyond it being an optional 64-bit unsigned integer. Any non-zero internal state is acceptable when the seed is present, provided the above conditions are satisfied.
fn compute_internal_state(seed: Option<u64>) -> (s: u64)
fn numpy_random_bit_generator(seed: Option<u64>) -> (result: BitGeneratorState)
function_signature:
fn compute_internal_state(seed: Option<u64>) -> (s: u64)
fn numpy_random_bit_generator(seed: Option<u64>) -> (result: BitGeneratorState)

description:
Implement a function that initializes and returns the internal state array of the MT19937 (Mersenne Twister) pseudorandom number generator from a given 32-bit unsigned seed. Assume the input seed is any 32-bit unsigned integer. The function must return an array (vector) of length 624. The first element of the returned array must equal the input seed. For each index i in the range 0 to 622 (inclusive), the next element must satisfy the MT19937 seeding recurrence: state[i + 1] = 1812433253 * (state[i] XOR (state[i] >> 30)) + (i + 1), where all arithmetic is performed with 32-bit wrap-around semantics (i.e., modulo 2^32), and >> denotes logical right shift on a 32-bit unsigned integer. The function should return the fully initialized 624-element state array.
fn mt19937(seed: u32) -> (state: Vec<u32>)

Design a lightweight random state interface that produces a deterministic pseudo-random floating-point number in the half-open interval [0, 1). The input is a state object containing an 8-bit unsigned integer seed. The function must always return a valid result without any preconditions. The returned value is determined solely by the seed, by taking the seed modulo 8 and mapping it to one of eight equally spaced values in [0, 1): 0.0, 0.125, 0.25, 0.375, 0.5, 0.625, 0.75, or 0.875. The behavior is deterministic: identical seeds yield identical outputs, and all outputs are guaranteed to satisfy 0.0 <= result < 1.0.
fn random(state: RandomState) -> (result: f32)

Design a constructor function for a 256-bit state consisting of four 64-bit unsigned components, used to initialize a simple random generator state from an optional seed. The function takes an optional 64-bit unsigned integer as input and returns a freshly initialized state with the following guarantees:
- If no seed is provided (the input is None), all four components of the returned state must be zero.
- If a seed is provided (the input is Some(value)), the returned state must not be the all-zero state. To ensure this, set the first component to the provided value; if the provided value is zero, set the first component to 1 instead. The remaining components must be zero.
There are no preconditions on the seed value; the function must satisfy the above postconditions for all possible inputs.
fn sfc64(seed: Option<u64>) -> (state: SFC64State)

Implement initialization of a random-bit generator from an optional 64-bit seed. Provide a function that constructs and returns a generator object with the following properties:
- The returned generator must be marked as initialized (its initialization flag is true).
- The generator contains an embedded component that stores the provided seed exactly as given (including the case when no seed is provided).
- The embedded component also stores a 64-bit internal state derived from the input seed. If a seed is provided, the internal state must be non-zero. If no seed is provided, the internal state may be zero.
Additionally, provide a helper function that computes the internal state from the optional seed and guarantees that when a seed is present, the computed state is not zero. There are no preconditions on the input beyond being an optional 64-bit unsigned integer.
fn compute_state(seed: Option<u64>) -> u64
fn default_rng(seed: Option<u64>) -> Generator
function_signature:
fn compute_state(seed: Option<u64>) -> u64
fn default_rng(seed: Option<u64>) -> Generator

description:
Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index of an element whose value is maximal in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned index must be within array bounds, and the element at that index must be greater than or equal to every other element in the array. If multiple elements share the same maximal value, return the smallest index among those positions (i.e., the earliest occurrence of the maximum).
fn argmax(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index of the minimum element. Assume the input array length is strictly positive. The returned index must be within the bounds of the array. If multiple positions share the minimum value, return the smallest (earliest) index among them. Formally, the result index r satisfies: (1) 0 <= r < length of the array; (2) for every valid index j, the value at r is less than or equal to the value at j; and (3) for every valid index j whose value equals the minimum value, r <= j. The function operates on standard Rust Vec<i8> and returns a usize index.
fn numpy_argmin(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index of the first occurrence of the maximum element. Assume the input array length is positive. The returned index must be within bounds. The value at the returned index is greater than or equal to every other element in the array. If multiple positions share the same maximal value, return the smallest index among those positions.
fn nanargmax(a: Vec<i8>) -> (idx: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers, return the index of the minimal element. Assume the input length is positive. The returned index must be within bounds of the array. The value at the returned index must be less than or equal to the value at every position in the array. In case multiple positions share the same minimal value, return the smallest index among them (i.e., the first occurrence). Formally: for all valid indices j, the value at the returned index is less than or equal to the value at j; additionally, for all indices j strictly less than the returned index, the value at j is strictly greater than the value at the returned index.
fn nanargmin(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: usize)

Given three arrays of equal length: a boolean mask, a first numeric array, and a second numeric array (all elements are 32-bit floating-point numbers). Produce a new array of the same length such that, for each index i, the resulting element equals the element from the first array if the mask at index i is true, and equals the element from the second array otherwise.
Assume the mask and both numeric arrays have the same length. The result must have the same length as the inputs. For every valid index i (0 <= i < n), the result at i must equal the corresponding element from the first array when the mask at i is true, and equal the corresponding element from the second array when the mask at i is false.
The computation processes elements in order and maintains that, after processing any prefix of the arrays, the constructed output prefix has the same length as the number of processed elements and already satisfies the selection rule for all indices in that prefix. All indexing must be safe (no out-of-bounds access).
fn numpy_where(condition: Vec<bool>, x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)
function_signature: fn numpy_where(condition: Vec<bool>, x: Vec<f32>, y: Vec<f32>) -> (result: Vec<f32>)

description:
Given a non-empty array of signed 8-bit integers, return the maximum value contained in the array. Assume the array length is strictly positive. The returned value must be an element of the array, and every array element must be less than or equal to the returned value. If the maximum value appears multiple times, the specification requires that there exists a smallest index at which the returned value occurs, and every other occurrence of that same value has an index greater than or equal to that smallest index (i.e., the maximum value is witnessed by its first occurrence). If all elements in the array are equal to each other, the returned value must equal the first element. The function returns only the value, not the index.
fn amax(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: i8)

Given a non-empty vector of 8-bit signed integers, return the maximum element in the vector. Assume the input size is positive. The output must be equal to the value of some element within the vector and must be greater than or equal to every element in the vector. In the case where the maximum value appears multiple times, the result must correspond to the earliest occurrence of the maximum: there exists a witness index within bounds such that its value equals the result and it is not later than any other index holding the same maximum value. Additionally, if all elements in the vector are equal, the output must equal the first element.
fn max(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: i8)

Given a non-empty array of signed 8-bit integers, return the minimum value contained in the array. Assume the input size is greater than zero. The returned value must be equal to one of the elements in the array and must be less than or equal to every element in the array.
fn min(a: Vec<i8>) -> i8

Given a non-empty vector of signed 8-bit integers, return the maximum value contained in the vector. Assume the input size is strictly positive. The returned value must be equal to some element of the input vector (i.e., it must match the value at a valid index within the vector). Additionally, the returned value must be greater than or equal to every element in the vector. If multiple elements share the maximum value, returning that maximum value is acceptable.
fn nanmax(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: i8)

You are given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers. Return the minimum element in the array. Assume the input size is positive. The returned value must be equal to some element of the array and must be less than or equal to every element in the array (i.e., it is a true minimum with respect to the usual integer ordering).
fn nanmin(a: Vec<i8>) -> (result: i8)

Implement a function that takes two inputs: a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers and an integer quantile percentage in the range [0, 100]. The function must return a value that satisfies the following specification:
- The return value is bounded by the elements of the array: it is greater than or equal to the array's minimum element and less than or equal to the array's maximum element.
- When the quantile percentage is 0, the function returns the minimum element of the array (i.e., the value of some index that attains the minimum).
- When the quantile percentage is 100, the function returns the maximum element of the array (i.e., the value of some index that attains the maximum).
Assume the input array length is positive and the quantile percentage is between 0 and 100 inclusive.
fn get_min_val(a: &Vec<i8>) -> i8
fn get_max_val(a: &Vec<i8>) -> i8
fn nanquantile(a: Vec<i8>, q: i8) -> i8
function_signature:
fn get_min_val(a: &Vec<i8>) -> i8
fn get_max_val(a: &Vec<i8>) -> i8
fn nanquantile(a: Vec<i8>, q: i8) -> i8

description:
You are given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers and an integer q that represents a percentile. Assume the array length is positive and q is within the inclusive range [0, 100]. Implement a function that returns an 8-bit integer result subject to the following requirements:

- General existence requirements for any q in [0, 100]:
  • If an element of the array is less than or equal to result, then there exists (possibly a different) element of the array that is greater than or equal to result.
  • If an element of the array is greater than or equal to result, then there exists (possibly a different) element of the array that is less than or equal to result.
  These conditions ensure the array contains at least one element on each side of result (greater-or-equal and less-or-equal), i.e., result lies within the range covered by the array.

- Boundary percentiles:
  • If q equals 0, result must be less than or equal to every element of the array. Together with the existence requirements above, this implies result is equal to the minimum element present in the array.
  • If q equals 100, result must be greater than or equal to every element of the array. Together with the existence requirements above, this implies result is equal to the maximum element present in the array.

The function should return a value that satisfies these properties. In particular, for q = 0 it must return the minimum element; for q = 100 it must return the maximum element; and for any other q it may return any value that ensures there exists at least one array element greater than or equal to it and at least one array element less than or equal to it (returning any existing array element satisfies this).
fn vec_min_i8(arr: &Vec<i8>) -> (m: i8)
fn vec_max_i8(arr: &Vec<i8>) -> (m: i8)
fn percentile(arr: Vec<i8>, q: i8) -> (result: i8)

You are given a non-empty array of 8-bit signed integers and a percentile value q in the range [0, 100]. Return the extreme quantile defined as follows: if q equals 100, return the maximum element of the array; otherwise (including q = 0), return the minimum element of the array.

Assume the input array length is positive and q is within [0, 100]. The returned value must be within the range of the dataset: there exists an element in the array that is less than or equal to the returned value, and there exists an element in the array that is greater than or equal to the returned value. Additionally, for q = 0, the returned value must be less than or equal to every element in the array, and for q = 100, the returned value must be greater than or equal to every element in the array.
fn quantile(a: Vec<i8>, q: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn quantile(a: Vec<i8>, q: i8) -> i8

description:
Implement a function that right-justifies a sequence of characters to a given target width using a specified fill character. The inputs are: (1) a vector of characters representing the original sequence, (2) a target width given as an 8-bit unsigned integer, and (3) a fill character. Assume the target width fits in an 8-bit unsigned integer (0 to 255). The function must return a new vector of characters whose length equals the maximum of the original length and the target width. If the original length is at least the target width, the result must be exactly the original sequence (unchanged) and have the same length. If the original length is less than the target width, the result must have length equal to the target width and the original sequence must appear as a suffix of the result. In the case where padding is needed, the extra leading positions are filled with the given fill character. If the original sequence is empty, the result length must equal the target width.
fn rjust(orig: Vec<char>, width: u8, fillchar: char) -> (res: Vec<char>)
function_signature: fn rjust(orig: Vec<char>, width: u8, fillchar: char) -> (res: Vec<char>)

description:
Implement a function that processes a list of strings by performing a right-split operation on each string using a given separator and a maximum number of splits.

Inputs:
- A list of strings.
- A separator string that is guaranteed to be non-empty.
- A maximum number of splits provided as an unsigned 8-bit integer.

Output:
- A list of lists of strings, where the ith inner list corresponds to the split result of the ith input string.

Requirements and properties to satisfy:
- The separator string is non-empty.
- The output list has the same length as the input list.
- Each inner list is non-empty.
- If the maximum number of splits is zero, then no splitting is performed: each inner list contains exactly one element equal to the original string.
- For any input string, the number of parts produced is at most the maximum number of splits plus one.
- If an input string is empty, its split result is a single empty string.

The intent is to mirror a right-split (rsplit) behavior with the above constraints; only the listed properties must hold for all outputs.
fn rsplit(a: Vec<String>, sep: String, maxsplit: u8) -> Vec<Vec<String>>
function_signature: fn rsplit(a: Vec<String>, sep: String, maxsplit: u8) -> Vec<Vec<String>>

description:
Implement a function that transforms a non-empty list of strings into a list of string lists. Assume the input list length is positive. The output must satisfy the following properties: (1) the output list has exactly the same number of elements as the input list, and (2) for every position, the corresponding inner list of strings is non-empty (contains at least one element). A boolean flag is provided as a parameter; it may influence how strings are partitioned (for example, whether line-ending characters are retained), but the only required guarantees are the two length properties above.
fn splitlines(a: Vec<String>, keepends: bool) -> Vec<Vec<String>>
function_signature: fn splitlines(a: Vec<String>, keepends: bool) -> Vec<Vec<String>>

description:
Given a non-empty array of 64-bit floating-point numbers, return the value of its last element. Assume the input length is strictly positive. The function must satisfy these postconditions: if the length is exactly 1, it returns the only element; if the length is greater than 1, it returns the element at index length − 1 (the final position).
fn reduce(arr: Vec<f64>) -> (result: f64)

You are given an operation selector over the integers with four possible cases: addition, multiplication, logical AND, and logical OR. For logical operations, interpret any nonzero integer as true and zero as false; the result of a logical operation must be the integer 1 for true and 0 for false. Define the identity element for an operation as an integer id such that, for all integers x, both applying the operation with id on the right and on the left leaves x unchanged. Formally, id is an identity element for the chosen operation if, for all integers x, op(x, id) = x and op(id, x) = x, where op denotes the semantics of the chosen operation.

Write a function that, given the operation selector, returns the identity element if it exists, and returns None otherwise. The function must satisfy the following specification:
- If it returns Some(id), then id is an identity element for the selected operation, i.e., for all integers x, op(x, id) = x and op(id, x) = x.
- If it returns None, then there exists no integer that is an identity element for the selected operation (i.e., for every integer candidate id, there is some x for which at least one of op(x, id) ≠ x or op(id, x) ≠ x holds).

Assume no preconditions on the input; the function must handle all four operation selectors. The intended behavior corresponds to returning 0 for addition, 1 for multiplication, and reporting that no identity exists for the defined integer-semantics of logical AND and logical OR.
enum OpType { Add, Multiply, LogicalAnd, LogicalOr }
fn ufunc_identity(op: OpType) -> (result: Option<i8>)
function_signature:
enum OpType { Add, Multiply, LogicalAnd, LogicalOr }
fn ufunc_identity(op: OpType) -> (result: Option<i8>)

description:
Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, return the length of the longest contiguous strictly increasing streak. A streak is defined as a maximal contiguous segment where each element is strictly greater than its immediate predecessor. If the array is empty, return 0. The returned value must never exceed the array length.
fn longest_increasing_streak(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> usize

Given a non-empty vector of unsigned integers (usize), return the maximum element in the vector. Assume the input size is greater than zero. The result must be equal to the value at some valid index of the input, and every element in the input must be less than or equal to the returned value.
fn max_of_list(lst: &Vec<usize>) -> (result: usize)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum value in the array. Assume the input length is strictly greater than 0. The returned value must be equal to the value at some index of the input (i.e., it must be one of the elements of the array), and every element in the array must be less than or equal to the returned value. Indices are 0-based.
fn max_of_list(lst: Vec<i32>) -> (result: i32)

Implement a function that returns the maximum value among three 32-bit signed integers. There are no preconditions on the inputs. The function must satisfy the following properties: the returned value is greater than or equal to each of the three input values, and the returned value is equal to one of the input values.
fn max2_exec(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32
fn max_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> i32
function_signature:
fn max2_exec(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32
fn max_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> i32

description:
Given a finite array of 32-bit signed integers, compute how many distinct values it contains. Assume the array is non-decreasing (i.e., sorted in ascending order with duplicates allowed). The array length may be zero. The function must not modify the input. The return value must be less than or equal to the array length. For an empty array, return 0; for a non-empty array, return the number of unique values appearing in the array.
fn remove_duplicates(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: usize)

Given a vector of characters, return a new vector containing the characters in reverse order. The output must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the output, the element at position i must equal the element from the input at position (input_length − 1 − i). There are no additional preconditions; the function must handle empty inputs and any finite sequence of characters.
fn reverse_string(s: &Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given a strictly increasing array of 32-bit signed integers and an integer target value, return the smallest index k (0-based) such that all elements at indices less than k are strictly less than the target, and if k is less than the array length, then the element at index k is greater than or equal to the target. This is the insertion position of the target that preserves the strict ascending order of the array. Assume the array is strictly sorted in increasing order (for all valid indices i < j, a[i] < a[j]). The returned index must be within the range [0, n], where n is the array length. Formally: for all indices i in [0, k), a[i] < target, and if k < n then target <= a[k].
fn search_insert(xs: &Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> (result: usize)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a 32-bit signed target value, return the indices of two distinct elements whose sum equals the target. If such a pair exists, return the lexicographically smallest pair of indices (first minimize the first index; among those, minimize the second index) under the constraint that the first index is strictly less than the second index. If no such pair exists, return None. A pair whose sum would overflow the 32-bit signed integer range must not be considered a match. When returning a pair, both indices must be within the bounds of the array, and the sum of the selected elements must equal the target. When returning None, it must be the case that no two distinct indices in the array produce the target sum.
fn two_sum(nums: Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> Option<(usize, usize)>
function_signature: fn two_sum(nums: Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> Option<(usize, usize)>

description:
Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a target 32-bit signed integer, assume the array length is at least 2. It is guaranteed that there exists exactly one pair of distinct indices (a lower index and a higher index) such that 0 <= lower < higher < array length and the sum of the two corresponding elements equals the target. Return a vector containing exactly these two indices in strictly increasing order. The two indices must be within bounds of the input array, and the sum of the elements at the returned indices must equal the target.
fn two_sum(nums: &Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> (result: Vec<usize>)

Implement a function that, given a finite array of natural numbers (non-negative integers), returns the smallest element if it exists. The function should return an optional value: return None if the array is empty, and return Some(v) otherwise, where v is a value that appears in the array and v is less than or equal to every element in the array. There are no additional input preconditions beyond the type and finite length of the array. If the array is non-empty, the returned value must be equal to one of the array elements and must be a lower bound for all elements in the array.
fn find_smallest(s: &Vec<nat>) -> (result: Option<nat>)

Given a 32-bit signed integer, determine whether it is divisible by 11. Return true if and only if there exists an integer k such that k * 11 equals the input value. The function must be total over all 32-bit signed integers, including zero and negative numbers. There are no preconditions. Zero is considered divisible by 11. The returned boolean must exactly match the mathematical property: the result is true if and only if the input is an integer multiple of 11, and false otherwise.
fn is_divisible_by_11(n: i32) -> (result: bool)

Given three 32-bit signed integers, compute and return the minimum value among them. The result must be less than or equal to each of the three inputs and must be exactly equal to one of the input values. There are no additional assumptions; any 32-bit signed integers are valid inputs.
fn min_i32(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32
fn min_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> i32
function_signature:
fn min_i32(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32
fn min_of_three(a: i32, b: i32, c: i32) -> i32

description:
Given two non-empty arrays of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether they share at least one common value. Assume both input arrays have length greater than zero. Return true if and only if there exist indices i and j within the respective bounds of the two arrays such that the element at position i in the first array equals the element at position j in the second array; otherwise, return false. Duplicates may appear in either array, and the arrays need not be sorted.
fn has_common_element(a: &Vec<i32>, b: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Given a 32-bit integer and a non-empty array of 32-bit integers, determine whether the integer is strictly greater than every element in the array. Assume the array length is positive. The function must return true if and only if, for all valid indices in the array, the given integer is greater than the element at that index.
fn is_greater(n: i32, a: &Vec<i32>) -> bool

Given a UTF-8 string, determine whether it contains at least one occurrence of the character 'z' or 'Z'. Treat the string as a sequence of Unicode scalar values (i.e., the length and indexing are by Unicode characters, not bytes). Return true if and only if there exists an index within the valid character positions such that the character at that position is either 'z' or 'Z'; otherwise, return false. There are no preconditions on the input string (it may be empty). The result must be logically equivalent to the existence of such a position within the character-level length of the string.
fn contains_z(s: &str) -> (result: bool)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether there exists an adjacent pair of elements where the second element is exactly one greater than the first. Formally, return true if and only if there exists an index i such that 0 <= i < n - 1 and array[i] + 1 == array[i + 1], where n is the length of the array. If the array has fewer than two elements, the result is false. No assumptions are made about sorting or distinctness of elements.
fn contains_consecutive_numbers(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Implement a function that takes a read-only vector of characters and returns a new vector of the same length where each character is converted to its ASCII lowercase form if it is an uppercase ASCII letter. Specifically, for every position i in the input: if the character at position i is between 'A' and 'Z' inclusive, then the corresponding output character must be the lowercase version obtained by adding 32 to its code point; otherwise, the output character must be exactly the same as the input character. The input can be any vector of Unicode scalar values (Rust char), but only ASCII uppercase letters ('A'..'Z') are transformed. The output must not modify the input, and the output vector length must equal the input vector length. The specification requires that for all indices within bounds, the described per-element mapping holds.
fn to_lowercase(s: &Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return a new array where the first and last elements are swapped, and all interior elements remain unchanged. Assume the input length is strictly greater than zero. The returned array must have the same length as the input. For every index i such that 1 <= i < n - 1 (where n is the array length), the element at index i in the result must be equal to the element at index i in the input. The first element of the result must equal the last element of the input, and the last element of the result must equal the first element of the input. If the input length is 1, the result is identical to the input.
fn swap_first_and_last(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given a finite array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether every element located at an odd index (i.e., indices 1, 3, 5, ...) is an odd integer. Return true if and only if this condition holds for all odd indices within the array bounds; otherwise, return false. An odd integer is one that is not divisible by 2. There are no additional preconditions on the input; empty arrays are allowed and should return true by vacuous truth. The function’s result must be logically equivalent to the universal property: for all indices i with 0 <= i < length and i is odd, the element at index i is odd.
fn is_odd_at_index_odd(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the index of the first odd element if one exists; otherwise, return None. Assume the input length is strictly positive. An integer is considered odd if its remainder when divided by 2 is nonzero. The result must satisfy the following properties:
- If the function returns Some(index), then index is a valid position within the array, the element at that position is odd, and every element at a position strictly less than index is not odd.
- If the function returns None, then every element of the array is not odd.
fn find_first_odd(a: &Vec<i32>) -> Option<usize>

Implement a function that compares two 32-bit signed integers and returns a boolean indicating whether they are equal. The function must be total over all i32 values. Postconditions: it must return true if and only if the two inputs are equal, and false otherwise. There are no preconditions beyond the inputs being valid 32-bit signed integers.
fn compare(a: i32, b: i32) -> (result: bool)
function_signature: fn compare(a: i32, b: i32) -> (result: bool)

description:
You are given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, a source array and a destination array, along with a source start index, a destination start index, and a nonnegative length. Assume that the specified slice of the source array and the specified slot in the destination array are both fully within bounds: the source start index plus the length does not exceed the source array length, and the destination start index plus the length does not exceed the destination array length. Construct and return a new array whose length equals the destination array length. The returned array must be identical to the destination array except that the contiguous segment of length equal to the given length, starting at the destination start index, is replaced by the corresponding segment taken from the source array starting at the source start index. Elements before the destination start index and elements after the replaced segment must remain exactly as they are in the destination array. If the length is zero, the result must be exactly the destination array.
fn copy(src: &Vec<i32>, s_start: usize, dest: &Vec<i32>, d_start: usize, len: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
function_signature: fn copy(src: &Vec<i32>, s_start: usize, dest: &Vec<i32>, d_start: usize, len: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
You are given two sequences of characters. Consider only the first L characters of the first sequence and the first P characters of the second sequence. You are also given a position A within the range [0, L]. Construct and return a new sequence defined by inserting the considered P-character prefix of the second sequence into the considered L-character prefix of the first sequence at position A. Formally:
- Assume L does not exceed the length of the first sequence.
- Assume P does not exceed the length of the second sequence.
- Assume A is between 0 and L (inclusive).
The result must have length L + P and be exactly the concatenation of three segments:
1) The first A characters of the first sequence.
2) The first P characters of the second sequence.
3) The remaining (L - A) characters from the first sequence starting at position A (i.e., up to position L).
Equivalently, for every valid index i:
- For 0 <= i < A, the i-th element of the result equals the i-th element of the first sequence.
- For 0 <= i < P, the element at position A + i in the result equals the i-th element of the second sequence.
- For 0 <= i < (L - A), the element at position A + P + i in the result equals the element at position A + i in the first sequence.
No elements beyond the first L characters of the first sequence or beyond the first P characters of the second sequence are used.
fn insert(oline: &Vec<char>, l: usize, nl: &Vec<char>, p: usize, at_pos: usize) -> Vec<char>
function_signature: fn insert(oline: &Vec<char>, l: usize, nl: &Vec<char>, p: usize, at_pos: usize) -> Vec<char>

description:
Implement a function that performs a linear search over an array of 32-bit integers to locate a given target value. The function scans the array from the beginning and returns the index of the first occurrence of the target if it is present. If the target does not appear in the array, the function returns the array’s length. There are no preconditions on the inputs; the array may be empty. The returned value must satisfy the following correctness properties: (1) it is within bounds (0 ≤ return value ≤ array length); (2) either the return value equals the array length (indicating the target is not present) or the element at the returned index equals the target; and (3) for all indices strictly less than the returned value, the corresponding elements are not equal to the target. These properties ensure the result is the minimal index of the target when it exists, and the array contains no instances of the target when the result equals the array length.
fn linear_search(a: &Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> usize

Given two arrays of characters, return their longest common prefix as an array of characters. The prefix must consist exactly of the sequence of characters, starting from index 0, that both inputs share in the same order. The output length must be less than or equal to each input's length. For every index in the returned prefix, the character at that index must equal the character at the same index of both inputs. Additionally, the returned prefix must be maximal: either it is equal to one of the entire inputs (meaning one input is a prefix of the other), or, if both inputs have a character immediately after the returned prefix, those two next characters must differ. The inputs may be empty.
fn longest_common_prefix(str1: &Vec<char>, str2: &Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)
    ensures
        result.len() <= str1.len(),
        result.len() <= str2.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < result.len() ==> result[i] == str1[i] && result[i] == str2[i],
        result.len() == str1.len() || result.len() == str2.len() || 
            (result.len() < str1.len() && result.len() < str2.len() && str1[result.len() as int] != str2[result.len() as int]),
function_signature:
fn longest_common_prefix(str1: &Vec<char>, str2: &Vec<char>) -> (result: Vec<char>)
    ensures
        result.len() <= str1.len(),
        result.len() <= str2.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < result.len() ==> result[i] == str1[i] && result[i] == str2[i],
        result.len() == str1.len() || result.len() == str2.len() || 
            (result.len() < str1.len() && result.len() < str2.len() && str1[result.len() as int] != str2[result.len() as int]),

description:
Implement a function that, given a vector of 32-bit integers and a single 32-bit integer value, returns a new vector that is the original vector with the value appended to its end. The output must satisfy the following properties: (1) the length of the returned vector is exactly one more than the length of the input vector; (2) for every valid index in the range of the input vector, the element at that index in the returned vector is equal to the corresponding element in the input vector (i.e., the entire original prefix is preserved in order); and (3) the element at the index equal to the original length is exactly the provided value. The input vector may be empty or non-empty, and any 32-bit integer value is permitted. The behavior is purely functional with respect to the output properties; no additional side effects or constraints are assumed beyond these postconditions.
fn append(a: &Vec<i32>, b: i32) -> Vec<i32>

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum value contained in the array. Assume the input length is at least 1. The returned value must be greater than or equal to every element in the array and must be exactly equal to the value of some element in the array (i.e., it is one of the array elements).
fn max_array(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: i32)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the minimum value present in the array. Assume the array length is strictly greater than zero. The returned value must be less than or equal to every element of the array and must be exactly equal to at least one element in the array (i.e., it is the minimum element drawn from the array). No assumptions are made about the ordering of the array or the uniqueness of elements.
fn min_array(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: i32)

Implement a function that returns the minimum of two 32-bit signed integers. The function must satisfy the following behavior: if the first input is less than or equal to the second input, the result must be the first input; otherwise, the result must be the second input. The result must always be exactly one of the two inputs. Assume the standard total order for 32-bit signed integers.
fn my_min(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32
function_signature: fn my_min(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, create and return a new array formed by removing the first element of the input. Assume the input size is positive. The output must preserve the relative order of the remaining elements and have length exactly one less than the input. For every valid index i in the output, the element at position i must equal the element at position i + 1 in the input. The input array must not be modified.
fn remove_front(a: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a 32-bit signed integer threshold, return a new array of the same length such that, for each position i, the output element is -1 if the corresponding input element is strictly greater than the threshold, and is equal to the input element otherwise. The output must preserve the original order of elements. There are no additional preconditions; the input array may be empty or contain any valid 32-bit signed integers, and the threshold may be any valid 32-bit signed integer. Formally: the output length equals the input length, and for every index i with 0 ≤ i < length, if input[i] > threshold then output[i] = -1, otherwise output[i] = input[i].
fn replace(arr: &Vec<i32>, k: i32) -> Vec<i32>

Implement a function that takes a vector of 32-bit integers and returns a new vector containing the elements in reverse order. The input may be empty. The returned vector must have the same length as the input. For every valid index i in the range [0, n), the element at position i in the output must equal the element at position (n − 1 − i) in the input, where n is the length of the input.
fn reverse(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and two indices, return a new array that is identical to the input except that the elements at the two given indices are swapped. Assume 0-based indexing and that both indices are valid for the input array (each strictly less than the array length). The returned array must have the same length as the input. The element at the first index in the output equals the element at the second index in the input, and vice versa. For every other position, the output element equals the corresponding input element. If the two indices are equal, the output array is identical to the input.
fn swap(arr: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
function_signature: fn swap(arr: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
You are given an array of 32-bit integers and an index. Assume the index is strictly less than the length of the array (i.e., the index is valid). Create and return a new array that has the same length as the input. In the returned array, the element at the specified index must be set to the constant value 60. All other positions must retain the corresponding values from the original array.
fn test_array_elements(a: &Vec<i32>, j: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Implement a function that returns three times a given 32-bit signed integer. Assume the input is chosen so that multiplying it by 3 does not overflow the range of a signed 32-bit integer. The function must guarantee that the returned value divided by 3 equals the original input, and that multiplying this quotient by 3 reconstructs the returned value. In other words, the result is exactly three times the input and is divisible by 3 without remainder.
fn triple(x: i32) -> i32

Implement two functions:
1) A character classifier that returns true if and only if the input character is one of the ASCII digits '0' through '9'.
2) A string predicate that returns true if and only if every character in the input string is an ASCII digit as defined above.
Assume the input string may be empty and may contain arbitrary Unicode characters; treat the string as a sequence of Unicode code points. The empty string should be classified as true (vacuously all characters are digits). The result of the string predicate must be logically equivalent to the universal condition: for all indices from 0 (inclusive) to the string length (exclusive), the character at that position is an ASCII digit. A correct solution must be consistent with the following invariant when conceptually processing the string left-to-right: after examining any prefix of length i, the intermediate boolean reflects that all characters in that prefix are ASCII digits. The implementation must terminate for any finite input length.
fn is_ascii_digit(c: char) -> bool
fn all_digits(s: String) -> bool
function_signature:
fn is_ascii_digit(c: char) -> bool
fn all_digits(s: String) -> bool

description:
Implement a function that takes a vector of 64-bit unsigned integers and a single 64-bit unsigned integer to append, and returns a new vector. Assume the input vector length is at most 100. The returned vector must satisfy all of the following: (1) its length equals the input length plus one; (2) for every valid index in the range [0, input length), the element at that index in the returned vector equals the corresponding element from the input vector; and (3) the last element of the returned vector equals the provided element to append. The input vector is not modified.
fn append(v: &Vec<u64>, elem: u64) -> Vec<u64>

Given two arrays of 64-bit unsigned integers, return a new array that is the concatenation of the first array followed by the second array. Assume each input array has length at most 100. The resulting array must have length equal to the sum of the lengths of the two inputs. For every index i with 0 <= i < length of the first array, the element at position i in the result must equal the element at position i in the first array. For every index i with length of the first array <= i < length of the result, the element at position i in the result must equal the element at position (i - length of the first array) in the second array.
fn concat(a: &Vec<u64>, b: &Vec<u64>) -> Vec<u64>

Implement a function that takes a vector of 64-bit unsigned integers and returns a new vector that is an exact copy of the input. Assume the input vector’s length is at most 100. The returned vector must have the same length as the input, and for every valid index from 0 (inclusive) to the input length (exclusive), the element in the returned vector at that index must be equal to the corresponding element in the input. The order of elements must be preserved.
fn copy(a: &Vec<u64>) -> (b: Vec<u64>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array containing the element-wise products of the inputs. Assume both input arrays have the same length n, where 0 <= n <= 100. Additionally, for every valid index i (0 <= i < n), the product of the corresponding elements is strictly less than 1000. The returned array must have the same length as the inputs, and each element at index i must equal the product of the two input elements at index i.
fn product(a: &Vec<u32>, b: &Vec<u32>) -> (c: Vec<u32>)

Given two arrays of non-negative 32-bit integers, return a new array containing the element-wise sums. Assume the following:
- The two input arrays have the same length.
- The length is at most 100.
- For every valid index, the sum of the corresponding elements from the two arrays is strictly less than 1000.
The output array must have exactly the same length as the inputs, and at each index it must equal the sum of the elements from the two inputs at that index.
fn sum(a: &Vec<u32>, b: &Vec<u32>) -> (c: Vec<u32>)

Implement a function that searches for a target value in a sorted array of 64-bit unsigned integers and returns an index where the target occurs.
Assume the following:
- The array is non-empty.
- The array is sorted in non-decreasing order (for all indices i and j with 0 <= i <= j < length, the element at i is less than or equal to the element at j).
- The target value is guaranteed to appear at least once in the array.
The function must return an index that is strictly within the array bounds, and the element at that returned index must equal the target value. If the target appears multiple times, returning any one of its valid indices is acceptable.
The algorithm should maintain correctness during execution by preserving that the active search interval stays within the array bounds, that at least one occurrence of the target remains within the current interval, and that the interval strictly decreases in size at each step, ensuring termination.
fn binary_search(v: &Vec<u64>, k: u64) -> (r: usize)

Implement a function that returns the quotient and remainder when dividing 191 by 7, using 32-bit unsigned integers. The function takes no input and must return a pair (quotient, remainder) such that:
- The quotient equals 27 and the remainder equals 2.
- The remainder is always non-negative during the computation.
- At all times during the computation, the identity 7 * quotient + remainder = 191 holds.
- The computation terminates once the remainder becomes strictly less than 7, and a strictly decreasing measure based on the remainder guarantees termination.
Return the pair (27, 2).
fn cal_div() -> (u32, u32)

Given a 64-bit unsigned integer as input, determine whether it is a prime number. Assume the input is strictly greater than 1. The returned boolean must exactly match the mathematical definition of primality: a number is prime if it is greater than 1 and has no positive integer divisors other than 1 and itself. Formally, the output is true if and only if for all integers f with 1 < f < n, n mod f is not zero; otherwise, the output is false.
spec fn divides(factor: nat, candidate: nat) -> bool
spec fn is_prime(candidate: nat) -> bool
fn test_prime(candidate: u64) -> (result: bool)

Given a dynamic array of 32-bit signed integers and a target value, assume the target value occurs at least once in the array. Return the index of the first occurrence of the target when scanning from the beginning of the array. The returned index must satisfy the following properties: it is within the array bounds; the element at that index equals the target; and every index strictly before the returned index contains an element that is not equal to the target. If the target appears multiple times, the smallest such index (the first occurrence) must be returned.
pub fn linear_search(a: &Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> (n: usize)
function_signature: pub fn linear_search(a: &Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> (n: usize)

description:
Given an array of unsigned 32-bit integers and a target unsigned 32-bit integer, find two distinct indices whose elements sum to the target. Assume the array length is at least 2. Assume that for every pair of distinct indices in the array, the sum of the corresponding elements is strictly less than 256. Also assume that there exists at least one pair of indices whose elements sum to the target. Return a pair of indices (p, q) such that 0 <= p < q < n and array[p] + array[q] == target. If multiple pairs satisfy the condition, return the lexicographically smallest pair by indices: the one with the smallest first index; among pairs with the same first index, the one with the smallest second index. Additionally, guarantee that no earlier pair in this lexicographic order has a sum equal to the target.
fn two_sum(nums: &Vec<u32>, target: u32) -> (r: (usize, usize))

You are given: (1) a positive integer representing the size N, (2) a mutable integer array of length N, and (3) a mutable single-element integer array used as an accumulator. Assume N > 0, the array length equals N, and the accumulator array has length 1.

Write a procedure that performs two passes over the data:
- First pass: overwrite every element of the array so that all positions hold the value 1. During this pass, the processed prefix must always consist of ones.
- Second pass: compute the sum of the array elements from index 1 up to index N − 1 (i.e., excluding the first element). Store the resulting total in the single element of the accumulator array. During this accumulation, after processing any prefix of length t > 0, the current accumulator value must be at most t.

At the end, the procedure must guarantee that the final accumulator value is at most N.

All indexing is 0-based, and all reads and writes must remain within the bounds of the given arrays.
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)
function_signature: pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)

description:
You are given a mutable integer array and a separate mutable single-element array that will hold a running total, along with a positive integer N. Assume the integer N satisfies 0 < N < 1000, the array length is exactly N, and the single-element array length is exactly 1. Your task is to:

- Overwrite the integer array so that each even index holds the value 2 and each odd index holds the value 0. Consequently, after initialization and for the remainder of the computation, every element of the array must be either 0 or 2.
- Traverse the first N elements to compute a running total stored in the single-element array. On the first step, set the total to 0; on each subsequent step, add the current array element to the total.

The computation must maintain the following properties:
- The array length remains N during the entire execution.
- The single-element total container length remains 1 during the entire execution.
- For any prefix of length i > 0 considered during the traversal, the running total does not exceed 2 · i.
- Upon completion, the final total does not exceed 2 · N.

Indices are zero-based. No additional allocations are required; the operation must be performed in-place under the above constraints.
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)
function_signature: pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)

description:
You are given three inputs: a mutable integer array, a mutable single-element integer array used as an accumulator, and an integer N. Assume the main array’s initial length equals N, the accumulator array’s initial length is exactly 1, N is positive, and N is strictly less than 1000.

Perform two passes:
1) Overwrite the main array so that for every index from 0 to N−1, the value is either 0 or 3, and specifically 3 at indices divisible by 3 and 0 otherwise. During this pass, maintain that all processed elements satisfy the property “value is either 0 or 3.”
2) Set the accumulator’s single element to 0 at the start of the second pass. Then iterate over the array, and for each step after the first, add the current array element to the accumulator. Throughout this accumulation, maintain that after processing i items (for any i > 0), the accumulator’s value is at most 3·i.

At the end of the function, guarantee that the accumulator’s final value is at most 3·N.
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)
function_signature: pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)

description:
Implement a function that modifies an integer array and computes a bounded sum under specific constraints. The function receives a mutable array of 32-bit integers, a mutable single-element array used to hold a sum, and an integer representing the array size. Assume the array length equals the given size, the sum array has length 1, the size is positive, and the size is strictly less than 1000. First, overwrite the array such that each position whose index is divisible by 4 contains the value 4, and every other position contains 0. Then, initialize the single-element sum to 0 and accumulate the values of the array for indices greater than 0 into this single-element sum. The function must ensure that the final sum value is at most four times the given size.
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)
function_signature: pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)

description:
Implement a procedure that operates on two mutable integer arrays: a data array of length N and a single-element array used to store a running sum. Assume the data array initially has length exactly N, the sum container initially has length exactly 1, N is positive, and N is strictly less than 1000. The procedure must first overwrite the data array such that every position whose index is divisible by 5 contains the value 5 and every other position contains the value 0. Next, it must iterate through the array to accumulate a sum into the single-element container: at the first step the sum is initialized to 0, and at each subsequent step the container is updated by adding the current array element to the stored sum. The result must satisfy that the final sum is at most 5 times N. Throughout the computation, the array length must remain N and the sum container length must remain 1. Additionally, during the accumulation phase, it must always hold that all array elements are either 0 or 5, and for any processed prefix of length i (with i ranging from 0 up to N), the running sum is at most 5 · i.
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<i32>, sum: &mut Vec<i32>, N: i32)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum possible value of a[i] - a[j] over all pairs of indices i and j. Assume every element of the array is strictly greater than i32::MIN / 2 and strictly less than i32::MAX / 2 so that no arithmetic overflow can occur during the computation. The returned result must be an upper bound on all pairwise differences in the array; that is, for any indices i and j within bounds, a[i] - a[j] is less than or equal to the returned value.
fn max_difference(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> i32

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers (the first as the source and the second as a filter), return a new array obtained by removing from the source all values that appear at least once in the filter. Preserve the relative order of the remaining elements in the result. Arrays may be empty, and values may repeat.
Correctness requirements:
- Every element in the result must be an element of the source and must not be present in the filter.
- For every element in the source, either it appears in the filter or it appears in the result. (In other words, the result contains exactly those values from the source whose value is not present in the filter; no extra values are introduced.)
You may implement and use a helper function that determines whether a given value is present in a given array; this helper should return true if and only if there exists an index at which the array holds that value.
fn contains(list: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn remove_elements(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

fn contains(list: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn remove_elements(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given a list of 64-bit signed integers and two indices specifying a closed range, compute the sum of all elements whose positions lie between the two indices, inclusive. The indices must be valid: 0 ≤ start ≤ end < n, where n is the length of the list. The result must equal the exact mathematical sum of the elements in that subarray. The sum is returned as a 128-bit signed integer to accommodate large totals. The computation should be specified so that, at any point during iteration, the running total equals the mathematical sum of the already-processed prefix of the specified range, and all intermediate prefix sums are bounded between i64::MIN × t and i64::MAX × t, where t is the number of elements processed so far. The procedure must be shown to terminate by a strictly decreasing measure corresponding to the number of remaining elements to process. Inputs violating the stated preconditions are out of scope.
fn sum_range_list(arr: &Vec<i64>, start: usize, end: usize) -> (sum: i128)
function_signature: fn sum_range_list(arr: &Vec<i64>, start: usize, end: usize) -> (sum: i128)

description:
Given two byte strings (arrays of bytes representing characters), return a new sequence formed by removing from the first input all bytes that appear anywhere in the second input. Inputs may be empty. Treat the second input as the set of forbidden characters. The result must satisfy the following properties: (1) Every byte in the output occurs in the first input and does not occur in the second input. (2) For every position in the first input, the byte at that position is either present somewhere in the second input or present somewhere in the output.
fn remove_chars(str1: &[u8], str2: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8>
fn contains(str: &[u8], key: u8) -> bool

description:
Implement a function that takes a read-only slice of bytes (representing a string) and a byte value, and returns a new vector of bytes where every occurrence of the ASCII space character (code 32) in the input is replaced by the given byte. All other bytes must be preserved exactly as they appear in the input. The output must have the same length as the input. Formally: for each index i with 0 <= i < n (where n is the input length), the output at position i is equal to the replacement byte if the input at position i is 32, and equal to the input byte at position i otherwise. Assume the input length can be zero or greater; there are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn replace_blanks_with_chars(str1: &[u8], ch: u8) -> (result: Vec<u8])

You are given two lists of 32-bit integers. Construct and return a new list formed by removing the last element of the first list and then appending all elements of the second list in their original order. Assume the first list is non-empty. The resulting list must preserve the relative order of the retained elements from the first list and all elements of the second list. If the second list is empty, the result is simply the first list without its last element. If the first list has exactly one element, the result equals the second list. The inputs must not be modified.
fn replace_last_element(first: &Vec<i32>, second: &Vec<i32>) -> (replaced_list: Vec<i32>)

fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (result: bool)
fn intersection(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given a list of integers and a single integer value, create and return a new list formed by inserting the given value immediately before each element of the original list. The output list must have exactly twice the length of the input list. For every index k in the range [0, n), where n is the length of the input list, the element at position 2k in the output must be the given value, and the element at position 2k + 1 must be the element from the input list at index k. There are no additional constraints on the input list or the given value; the input may be empty, contain negative values, duplicates, or any valid 32-bit integers.
fn insert_before_each(arr: &Vec<i32>, elem: i32) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of unsigned 32-bit integers, return a new array formed by dividing corresponding elements of the first array by those of the second array using integer division. Assume both input arrays have the same length. Assume no element of the second array is zero. Also assume that for every index, the quotient of the corresponding elements fits within the range of a 32-bit unsigned integer. The returned array must have the same length as the inputs, and for each valid index i, the element at position i in the result equals the integer division of the i-th element of the first array by the i-th element of the second array. Empty arrays are allowed and should yield an empty result.
fn element_wise_division(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)

Given a non-empty list of 32-bit integers and a split index L, return a pair of lists that results from splitting the input at position L. Assume the input list length is greater than 0 and L is strictly between 0 and the length of the list (i.e., 0 < L < length). The first returned list must contain exactly the first L elements of the input, in their original order. The second returned list must contain the remaining elements from position L to the end, in their original order. The outputs must be equal to the corresponding contiguous subranges of the input: the first list equals the subrange from index 0 up to (but not including) index L, and the second list equals the subrange from index L up to the end of the input. Consequently, the lengths of the returned lists are L and (length − L), respectively.
fn split_array(list: &Vec<i32>, l: usize) -> (Vec<i32>, Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, produce a new array whose elements are the element-wise differences of the first array minus the second array. Assume both input arrays have the same length. Additionally, assume that for every index within the common length, the subtraction of the corresponding elements does not overflow or underflow the 32-bit signed integer range. The returned array must have the same length as the inputs, and for each valid index i, the i-th element of the output equals the i-th element of the first input minus the i-th element of the second input. Empty inputs are allowed and should produce an empty output.
fn element_wise_subtract(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Given a finite array of 32-bit integers and a target integer, determine whether every element of the array is equal to the target. The function must return true if and only if for every valid index i with 0 <= i < array length, the element at index i equals the target; otherwise, it must return false. There are no preconditions on the input. An empty array should be considered as satisfying the condition and must yield true.
fn all_elements_equals(arr: &Vec<i32>, element: i32) -> bool

Given a non-empty array of integer arrays, return a reference to one of the inner arrays that has the maximum length. Assume the outer array contains at least one inner array. The returned reference must point to an inner array from the input (i.e., its contents exactly match one of the inner arrays) and the length of the returned array must be greater than or equal to the length of every inner array in the input. If multiple inner arrays share the maximum length, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn max_length_list(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (max_list: &Vec<i32>)

Given an unsigned integer input n, determine whether n is non-prime (composite). Assume n is at least 2. Return true if and only if there exists an integer k such that 2 ≤ k < n and k divides n (i.e., n mod k = 0); otherwise return false. If an iterative approach is used, maintain the invariant that no candidate less than the current one divides n, and ensure the loop is terminating via a strictly decreasing measure.
spec fn is_divisible(n: int, divisor: int) -> bool
fn is_non_prime(n: u64) -> bool
function_signature:
spec fn is_divisible(n: int, divisor: int) -> bool

description:
Implement a function that takes a reference to a list of 64-bit unsigned integers and returns a new list that is a deep copy of the input. The output must have exactly the same number of elements as the input, and for every valid index i, the element at position i in the output must be equal to the element at position i in the input. The order of elements must be preserved, including duplicates. The function should work for any input length, including empty lists.
fn list_deep_clone(arr: &Vec<u64>) -> (copied: Vec<u64>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers of equal length, return a new array where each element is the bitwise XOR of the corresponding elements in the two input arrays. Assume both input arrays have the same length. The output array must have the same length as the inputs. For every index i in the range 0 <= i < n, the i-th output element equals the bitwise XOR of the i-th elements from the two inputs.
fn bit_wise_xor(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given a list of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new list containing exactly the elements of the input that are even (i.e., divisible by 2), preserving their original relative order. The input list may be empty. The output must be equal to the result of applying an order-preserving filter with the predicate "value % 2 == 0" to the input list.
fn remove_odds(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Implement two functions over sequences of 32-bit signed integers. The first function takes a sequence and a key value and returns true if and only if the key appears at some valid index within the sequence; otherwise, it returns false. The second function takes two sequences and returns true if and only if there exists an index in the first sequence such that the value at that index is present somewhere in the second sequence; otherwise, it returns false. No additional assumptions are required: both sequences may be empty, and values may repeat. The returned results must exactly match these logical predicates.
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn any_value_exists(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
