description:
Given a sequence of byte sequences (each representing a string), produce a new byte sequence containing the last byte of each input sequence, in order. Assume every input sequence is non-empty. The result must have the same length as the input sequence of sequences. If the input collection is empty, the result is also empty.
fn extract_rear_chars(s: &Vec<Vec<u8>>) -> (result: Vec<u8>)

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array containing exactly those elements whose value is odd (value modulo 2 is not zero). Preserve the original relative order of the retained elements. If the input is empty, the output is empty. No additional assumptions are required on the input values. The returned array must be equal to the result of filtering the input sequence by the predicate "value % 2 != 0".
fn filter_odd_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Given two finite lists of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether they share at least one common element. Return true if and only if there exists a value that appears in both lists; otherwise return false. Inputs may be empty. The result must exactly reflect the existence of at least one pair of valid indices, one in the first list and one in the second list, such that the elements at those positions are equal.
fn has_common_element(list1: &Vec<i32>, list2: &Vec<i32>) -> bool

Given an array of 32-bit signed integers and a single 32-bit signed integer value, determine whether the value is strictly greater than every element in the array. Return true if and only if, for all valid indices in the range [0, array length), the value is greater than the element at that index; otherwise return false. The array may be empty; in that case, the condition holds vacuously and the function should return true. There are no additional preconditions on the inputs.
fn is_greater(arr: &Vec<i32>, number: i32) -> bool

Given a list of 32-bit signed integers, return a new list containing exactly those elements that are strictly less than zero, preserving their original relative order. There are no preconditions on the input list; it may be empty or of any length. The result must be equal to the subsequence of the input obtained by filtering with the predicate x < 0. Equivalently: (1) every element in the result is negative and appears in the input; (2) every negative element in the input appears in the result the same number of times; and (3) the relative order of elements in the result matches their order in the input. The input must not be modified.
fn find_negative_numbers(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (negative_list: Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers, compute an element-wise multiplication of the two arrays. Assume the two arrays have the same length. Additionally, for every index, assume that the product of the corresponding elements falls within the 32-bit signed integer range (between i32::MIN and i32::MAX inclusive), i.e., no overflow or underflow occurs when multiplying any pair of corresponding elements. Return a new array of the same length where each position contains the product of the elements from the two input arrays at that index. The result must preserve the order of indices and have exactly the same length as the inputs.
fn element_wise_multiplication(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Implement a function that, given an array of 32-bit signed integers, returns a new array where each element is the cube of the corresponding input element. Assume the input length can be zero. Assume for every element in the input, both its square and its cube fit within the 32-bit signed integer range (i32::MIN..=i32::MAX). The result must have the same length as the input, and for every valid index i in the input length, the element at position i in the result equals the cube of the element at position i in the input. The order of elements must be preserved.
fn cube_element(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Given a non-empty list of integer sublists, return a reference to one of the sublists that has the minimum length among all sublists in the input. Assume the input list contains at least one sublist. The returned sublist must be exactly one of the sublists from the input (i.e., a reference to an existing sublist, not a newly constructed list). The length of the returned sublist must be less than or equal to the length of every sublist in the input. If multiple sublists share the minimum length, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn min_sublist(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> &Vec<i32>

Given a list of lists of 32-bit signed integers, return a new list containing the first element of each sublist, in the same order as they appear. Assume every sublist in the input is non-empty. The output length must equal the number of sublists in the input. If the outer list is empty, the result must be an empty list.
fn get_first_elements(arr: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given a slice of bytes, return the number of positions whose byte value corresponds to an uppercase ASCII letter. Specifically, count how many bytes are in the inclusive range 65 to 90 (characters 'A' through 'Z'). The input slice may be empty. Return the result as an unsigned 64-bit integer.

Correctness requirements:
- The result must be between 0 and the length of the input slice (inclusive lower bound, and it cannot exceed the length).
- The result must equal the mathematically defined count of uppercase letters in the sequence: for an empty sequence the count is 0; otherwise it is the count for the sequence without its last element plus 1 if the last byte is in [65, 90], and plus 0 otherwise.

Do not count lowercase letters, digits, punctuation, or any non-letter bytes outside the uppercase ASCII range.
fn count_uppercase(text: &[u8]) -> u64

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the array contains consecutive numbers in the sense that every adjacent pair satisfies next = previous + 1. Return true if and only if, for all valid adjacent indices, the successor element equals the predecessor plus one; otherwise return false. Assume the input size is at least 1. Additionally, assume that for every element x in the array, x + 1 is a valid 32-bit signed integer and non-negative, i.e., 0 ≤ x + 1 < i32::MAX.
fn contains_consecutive_numbers(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (is_consecutive: bool)

Given a read-only array of bytes and two byte values (an old value and a new value), return a new array of bytes of the same length where every occurrence of the old value is replaced by the new value. All other bytes must remain unchanged. The output length must be exactly equal to the input length. For every valid index i in the input range, the output at position i equals the new value if and only if the input at position i equals the old value; otherwise it equals the original input value at that position. There are no additional preconditions; the input may be empty.
fn replace_chars(str1: &[u8], old_char: u8, new_char: u8) -> (result: Vec<u8>)
function_signature: fn replace_chars(str1: &[u8], old_char: u8, new_char: u8) -> (result: Vec<u8>)

description:
Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the sum of its smallest element and its largest element. Assume the input size is positive. Additionally, assume every element in the array is strictly greater than i32::MIN / 2 and strictly less than i32::MAX / 2. These bounds guarantee that both the minimum and maximum values, and their sum, fit within the 32-bit signed integer range without overflow. The function must produce exactly min(array) + max(array) as an i32.
fn sum_min_max(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (sum: i32)

Given a read-only slice of bytes representing ASCII text, return a new vector of bytes of the same length where each uppercase ASCII letter is converted to its lowercase counterpart. Specifically, for each index i, if the byte at position i is an uppercase ASCII letter (i.e., its value is between 65 and 90 inclusive, corresponding to 'A'..'Z'), the output at position i must be that byte plus 32 (its lowercase counterpart). For all other bytes (including lowercase letters, digits, punctuation, and any non-ASCII values), the output must be exactly the same as the input at that position. The function must not assume any particular input size (it may be empty) and must not modify the input slice.
fn to_lowercase(str1: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8>

Given an array of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array consisting of exactly the odd values from the input, preserving their original order. Even values must be excluded. The output must be equal to the mathematical filter of the input under the predicate value mod 2 != 0. The input length may be zero or more. Duplicates are preserved, and no new elements are introduced.
fn find_odd_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Implement a function that toggles the case of characters in a byte slice interpreted with ASCII semantics. For each position, if the byte is an ASCII lowercase letter ('a' to 'z', decimal 97 to 122), convert it to its uppercase counterpart by subtracting 32. If the byte is an ASCII uppercase letter ('A' to 'Z', decimal 65 to 90), convert it to its lowercase counterpart by adding 32. All other bytes (including digits, punctuation, spaces, and any non-ASCII values) must remain unchanged. The function must return a new byte vector whose length is exactly equal to the length of the input slice. Assume the input may be empty and may contain arbitrary byte values. The per-index transformation must follow the described specification for every valid index of the input slice.
fn to_toggle_case(str1: &[u8]) -> (toggle_case: Vec<u8>)

Given a nonempty list of 32-bit signed integers, determine whether the list is sorted in nondecreasing (ascending) order. Assume the input size is strictly greater than zero. Return true if and only if, for every pair of indices i and j with 0 <= i < j < n, the element at index i is less than or equal to the element at index j. Equality between adjacent or non-adjacent elements is allowed and does not violate sortedness.
fn is_sorted(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (is_sorted: bool)

description:
Implement two functions over arrays of 32-bit integers. The first function determines whether a given candidate array appears as a contiguous subarray of a given main array. The second function checks, for a specified starting index, whether the candidate array exactly matches the contiguous segment of the main array that begins at that index.

Assumptions and constraints:
- The candidate array length does not exceed the main array length.
- For the index-based checker, the starting index is within bounds: 0 <= starting_index <= (main_length - candidate_length).

Required properties:
- Index-based checker: Return true exactly when the contiguous segment of the main array of length equal to the candidate, starting at the given index, is elementwise equal to the candidate. Otherwise, return false.
- Subarray search: Return true exactly when there exists some starting index k such that the contiguous segment of the main array of length equal to the candidate, starting at k, is elementwise equal to the candidate. Otherwise, return false.

Note: An empty candidate array should be considered present (i.e., a subarray) at any valid position.
fn sub_array_at_index(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>, idx: usize) -> (result: bool)
fn is_sub_array(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)
function_signature:
fn sub_array_at_index(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>, idx: usize) -> (result: bool)
fn is_sub_array(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given three arrays of 32-bit signed integers, implement a function that returns a new array formed by interleaving the three inputs element-by-element. Assume all three input arrays have the same length n, and that the value 3 × n is within the range of a 32-bit signed integer (i.e., 0 ≤ 3 × n ≤ 2,147,483,647). The result must have exactly 3 × n elements. For every index i in [0, n), the element at position 3 × i in the result must be the i-th element of the first array, the element at position 3 × i + 1 must be the i-th element of the second array, and the element at position 3 × i + 2 must be the i-th element of the third array.
fn interleave(s1: &Vec<i32>, s2: &Vec<i32>, s3: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Given two finite lists of 32-bit signed integers, return a new list containing exactly the values that appear in exactly one of the two input lists (the symmetric difference). The output must not contain duplicates: each qualifying value appears at most once. Any value that appears in both input lists must not appear in the output. If a value appears multiple times within an input, it is still included at most once in the output if it is not present in the other input. The relative order in the output must be stable: first list the qualifying values from the first input in their original left-to-right order, then list the qualifying values from the second input (that are not already included and are not present in the first input) in their original left-to-right order. The input lists may be empty.
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn find_dissimilar(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn find_dissimilar(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Implement a function that performs a left rotation on a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers by a given positive offset. The function receives an input vector and an index n such that 0 < n < length of the vector. It must return a new vector formed by concatenating the subarray from position n (inclusive) to the end with the subarray from position 0 (inclusive) to position n (exclusive). The relative order of elements within each segment must be preserved, and no elements may be lost or duplicated. Formally, the result equals the concatenation of the slice [n, len) followed by the slice [0, n). Assume the input vector length is greater than 0 and the rotation offset n strictly satisfies 0 < n < length.
fn split_and_append(list: &Vec<i32>, n: usize) -> (new_list: Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers, compute the difference between the largest and the smallest element in the vector. Assume the input size is positive. Additionally, assume every element is strictly greater than i32::MIN / 2 and strictly less than i32::MAX / 2. Return an i32 equal to (maximum of all elements) minus (minimum of all elements). For a single-element input, the result is 0. The returned value must exactly match the mathematical difference between the maximum and minimum values computed over the entire input.
spec fn max_rcur(seq: Seq<i32>) -> int
spec fn min_rcur(seq: Seq<i32>) -> int
fn difference_max_min(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (diff: i32)

Given a read-only sequence of bytes (a slice of u8), find the earliest position where the byte at that position appears more than once in the entire sequence. Return an optional pair containing the index and the corresponding byte. If no byte appears more than once, return None.

More formally:
- Let n be the length of the input (n may be zero).
- If there exists any byte that occurs at least twice in the input, return Some(i, b) where i is the smallest index such that the byte at position i occurs more than once in the whole input, and b is the byte at that position.
- If the result is Some(i, b), then for every index k with 0 <= k < i, the byte at position k occurs at most once in the entire input, and the returned byte b occurs more than once in the entire input.
- If the result is None, then every byte in the input occurs at most once in the entire input.

Also provide a helper function that, given a byte slice and a target byte, returns the total number of occurrences of that byte in the slice.
fn count_frequency(arr: &[u8], key: u8) -> usize
fn first_repeated_char(str1: &[u8]) -> Option<(usize, u8)>

fn count_frequency(arr: &[u8], key: u8) -> usize
fn first_repeated_char(str1: &[u8]) -> Option<(usize, u8)>

fn prime_num(n: u64) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given an array of integers and a 1-based position to remove, return a new array that contains all elements of the input except the element at that position. Assume the input array length is positive and the removal position strictly satisfies 1 ≤ position < n, where n is the array length (i.e., the last element cannot be removed). The relative order of the remaining elements must be preserved, and the output must be exactly the concatenation of the prefix of elements strictly before the given position with the suffix of elements strictly after the given position. The input array is not modified.
fn remove_kth_element(list: &Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> (new_list: Vec<i32>)

Given two arrays of 32-bit unsigned integers of the same length, compute the element-wise modulo and return the results in a new array. Assume the input arrays have equal length. Assume every element of the second array is nonzero. Additionally, assume that for each index, the remainder of dividing the corresponding element of the first array by the element of the second array fits within the signed 32-bit integer range (i.e., between i32::MIN and i32::MAX). The returned array must have the same length as the inputs, and its ith element must equal the remainder of the ith element of the first array divided by the ith element of the second array. If the inputs are empty, the result should be an empty array.
fn element_wise_module(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Given two arrays of 32-bit unsigned integers, return a new array containing the element-wise integer division of the first array by the second array. Assume the two input arrays have the same length. Assume every element in the second array (the divisor array) is non-zero. Also assume that, for every index i, the quotient of the unsigned division arr1[i] / arr2[i] fits within the 32-bit signed integer range. The result array must have the same length as the inputs, and for each valid index i, result[i] equals arr1[i] divided by arr2[i] using integer division (truncating toward zero for unsigned integers). Empty arrays are allowed and should produce an empty result.
fn element_wise_divide(arr1: &Vec<u32>, arr2: &Vec<u32>) -> Vec<u32>

Given a non-empty list of 32-bit signed integers, return the smallest value present in the list. Assume the input length is strictly positive. The result must be less than or equal to every element in the list and must be equal to at least one element of the list. The list may be unsorted and may contain duplicates or negative values.
fn smallest_num(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> (min: i32)

Implement a function that takes a slice of bytes and returns a new vector of bytes in which every lowercase ASCII letter is converted to its uppercase counterpart. Specifically, treat any byte in the range 97..=122 (corresponding to 'a'..'z') as a lowercase letter and convert it to uppercase by subtracting 32 from its ASCII code. All other bytes must remain unchanged. There are no preconditions on the input; the function must handle empty inputs and arbitrary byte values. The output length must be equal to the input length. Correctness requirement: for every index i in the range [0, n), where n is the input length, the i-th output byte equals input[i] - 32 if input[i] is between 97 and 122 inclusive, and equals input[i] otherwise.
fn to_uppercase(str1: &[u8]) -> (result: Vec<u8>)

Implement a function that, given a vector of 32-bit unsigned integers, returns a new vector consisting of exactly the even elements of the input, in the same relative order as they appear in the input. The input may be of any length, including empty. The returned vector must be equal to the mathematical filter of the input sequence using the predicate x % 2 == 0: every element in the result is even and originates from the input; no odd elements are included; no new elements are introduced; and the order of the retained elements is preserved. The function must terminate by processing a finite number of elements.
fn find_even_numbers(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (even_numbers: Vec<u32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers and an integer n, produce a new array where the first n elements of the input are reversed and the remainder of the array stays in the same order. Assume 0 < n < length of the array (i.e., n is at least 1 and strictly less than the array length). The original array is not modified. The returned array must be exactly the concatenation of: (a) the reverse of the prefix consisting of the first n elements of the input, followed by (b) the unchanged suffix from position n to the end of the input.
fn reverse_to_k(list: &Vec<i32>, n: usize) -> (reversed_list: Vec<i32>)

Given two finite lists of 32-bit integers, determine whether the second list appears as a contiguous sublist of the first list.

Assumptions for the main function:
- The length of the candidate sublist is less than or equal to the length of the main list.

Behavior of the main function:
- Return true if and only if there exists a starting position in the main list such that the contiguous segment of length equal to the candidate sublist is exactly equal to the candidate sublist. Otherwise, return false.
- An empty sublist is considered to be contained in any list and should yield true.

A helper function is also defined to check matching at a specific starting index:
- Inputs: a main list, a candidate sublist, and a starting index.
- Assumptions: the candidate sublist’s length is at most the main list’s length, and the starting index is within the inclusive range [0, main_length − sub_length].
- Behavior: return true if and only if the contiguous segment of the main list starting at the given index and having the same length as the candidate sublist is exactly equal to the candidate sublist.
fn is_sub_list_at_index(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>, idx: usize) -> bool
fn is_sub_list(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>) -> bool
function_signature:
fn is_sub_list_at_index(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>, idx: usize) -> bool
fn is_sub_list(main: &Vec<i32>, sub: &Vec<i32>) -> bool

description:
Given a non-empty list of integer sequences (arrays), determine whether all sequences have exactly the same length. Return true if and only if, for every pair of valid indices in the list, the lengths of the sequences at those indices are equal. Assume the list contains at least one sequence. The contents of the sequences are irrelevant; only their lengths matter. Sequences of length zero are permitted and should be treated like any other length when checking equality.
fn all_sequence_equal_length(seq: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (result: bool)

Given two arrays of 32-bit signed integers with equal length, return a new array whose elements are the pairwise sums of the corresponding positions. Assume for every index within bounds, the sum of the two input elements at that index fits within the 32-bit signed integer range (from i32::MIN to i32::MAX). The result must have the same length as the inputs and, for each valid index, its element equals the sum of the corresponding input elements. Empty arrays are allowed as long as both inputs have the same length.
fn add_list(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Given an array of bytes (interpreted as ASCII characters), produce a new array of the same length where every occurrence of a space (ASCII 32), comma (ASCII 44), or dot (ASCII 46) is replaced with a colon (ASCII 58). All other bytes must remain unchanged and in their original positions. Assume the input length may be zero. The output length must equal the input length. Formally, for each valid index i in the array, the output byte at position i equals 58 if the input byte at position i is 32, 44, or 46; otherwise, it equals the original input byte.
spec fn is_space_comma_dot_spec(c: u8) -> bool
fn replace_with_colon(str1: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8>
function_signature:
spec fn is_space_comma_dot_spec(c: u8) -> bool
fn replace_with_colon(str1: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8>

description:
Given a sorted array of integers in nondecreasing order and a target integer, return the index of the first occurrence of the target in the array. If the target does not occur in the array, return an indication that it is absent. Assume the input array is sorted such that for any two indices i and j with 0 ≤ i < j < length, the element at i is less than or equal to the element at j. If the result is present, it must be within array bounds, the element at that index must equal the target, and no earlier index contains the target. If the result indicates absence, then the target does not appear anywhere in the array.
fn find_first_occurrence(arr: &Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> Option<usize>

Given a finite sequence of bytes representing characters, determine whether all elements in the sequence are equal to the first element. Return true if and only if, for every index i with 1 <= i < length, the element at position i equals the element at position 0. An empty sequence or a single-element sequence is considered trivially uniform and should return true. There are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn all_characters_same(char_arr: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Given a non-empty list of unsigned 32-bit integers and a non-negative rotation count, return a new list obtained by rotating the original list to the right by the specified number of positions. Assume the input list length is strictly positive. The rotation amount is applied modulo the list length. The resulting list must have the same length as the input and must equal the concatenation of the suffix starting at index (length − (rotation count mod length)) followed by the prefix up to that index, preserving the relative order of elements.
fn rotate_right(list: &Vec<u32>, n: usize) -> (new_list: Vec<u32>)

Given an array of integers with length at least 2, return a pair of distinct indices (i_min, i_second) satisfying the following properties:
- i_min is the index of a minimum value in the entire array.
- i_second is an index different from i_min such that, for every index k in the array with k != i_min, the value at k is greater than or equal to the value at i_second. In other words, i_second points to a minimum value among all positions excluding i_min.
- If the minimum value occurs more than once, i_second may be the index of another occurrence of that same minimum.
- Both indices must be valid (0 <= i_min < n and 0 <= i_second < n where n is the array length) and must be distinct (i_min != i_second).
Assume the input array length is at least 2. The returned indices must satisfy that for every position k != i_min, the array[k] is greater than or equal to array[i_second], and there exists some position k != i_min whose value equals array[i_second] (ensuring i_second references an actual element among the non-minimum index positions).
spec fn min_spec(seq: Seq<i32>) -> int
fn second_smallest(numbers: &Vec<i32>) -> (usize, usize)
function_signature:
spec fn min_spec(seq: Seq<i32>) -> int
fn second_smallest(numbers: &Vec<i32>) -> (usize, usize)

description:
Given a finite list of 32-bit integers, determine whether the list contains only one distinct value. The function must return true if and only if every element at positions 1 through length − 1 (if any) is equal to the first element of the list. The function must correctly handle empty lists and single-element lists by returning true. Equivalently, it returns false if and only if there exists an index i with 1 ≤ i < length such that the element at position i differs from the first element. There are no additional preconditions on the input.
fn has_only_one_distinct_element(arr: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

Given a read-only slice of bytes representing a string, return the number of characters that are decimal digits. A character is considered a digit if its byte value is between 48 and 57 inclusive, corresponding to the ASCII characters '0' through '9'. The result must satisfy the following conditions:
- The result is an integer between 0 and the length of the input slice (inclusive).
- The result equals the value of a recursive specification that counts digits over the sequence abstraction of the input: for an empty sequence the count is 0; otherwise the count is the count of the sequence with its last element removed plus 1 if the last element is a digit, and plus 0 otherwise.
Additionally, if the implementation uses a loop to process the input:
- At each iteration over a prefix of length k (0 ≤ k ≤ input length), the running count equals the recursive specification applied to that prefix.
- The running count always stays between 0 and k.
- The iteration variable stays within the bounds 0 ≤ k ≤ input length, and the measure input length − k strictly decreases, ensuring termination.
spec fn is_digit(c: u8) -> bool
spec fn count_digits_recursively(seq: Seq<u8>) -> int
fn count_digits(text: &[u8]) -> (count: usize)

Given two lists of 32-bit signed integers, return a new list containing exactly the values that appear in exactly one of the two input lists (the symmetric difference). The output must contain no duplicates: each qualifying value appears at most once. Preserve the relative order of first appearance within each list: first include all qualifying values from the first list in their original order, then include all qualifying values from the second list in their original order.
Assume the lists may be empty and may contain repeated values. The following semantic properties must hold:
- Coverage from the first list: for every position in the first list, if the value at that position does not occur anywhere in the second list, then that value must occur somewhere in the output.
- Coverage from the second list: for every position in the second list, if the value at that position does not occur anywhere in the first list, then that value must occur somewhere in the output.
- Uniqueness: no two distinct positions in the output contain the same value.
Additionally, provide a helper that reports whether a given key occurs in a list (true if the key appears in any position, false otherwise).
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn difference(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature:
fn contains(arr: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> bool
fn difference(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given a zero-based array of non-negative integers, determine whether every index has the same parity as the value stored at that position. Specifically, return true if and only if for all indices i from 0 (inclusive) to the array length (exclusive), i modulo 2 equals the element at position i modulo 2. If the array is empty, the condition is vacuously true. The function must not modify the input.
fn is_odd_at_odd_index(arr: &Vec<usize>) -> bool

Given an array of non-negative integers, determine whether each position has a value whose parity matches the parity of its index. Return true if and only if, for every valid index i in the range [0, n), the element at position i is even when i is even, and the element at position i is odd when i is odd. The array length may be zero; in that case, the condition is considered satisfied and the function should return true.
fn is_even_at_even_index(arr: &Vec<usize>) -> bool

function_signature:
spec fn sum_to(arr: Seq<i64>) -> int
fn sum(arr: &Vec<i64>) -> (sum: i128)

description:
Implement a function that, given an array of 32-bit signed integers, returns a new array containing the square of each element in the same order. Assume the input satisfies that squaring any element does not overflow a 32-bit signed integer: for every valid index k, 0 <= input[k] * input[k] < i32::MAX. The function must handle empty arrays and must return an output array whose length equals the input length. For every index k in the valid range, the k-th element of the output equals input[k] * input[k].
fn square_nums(nums: &Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

function_signature:
spec fn is_even(n: u32) -> bool
fn is_product_even(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> (result: bool)

description:
Given a list of 32-bit unsigned integers, return the index of the first element that is odd. If the list contains no odd elements, return None. The input list may be empty.
Correctness requirements:
- If an index is returned, every element strictly before that index must be even, and the element at the returned index must be odd.
- If None is returned, then all elements in the list must be even.
fn find_first_odd(arr: &Vec<u32>) -> Option<usize>

Given a finite array of 32-bit signed integers and a target integer, determine whether the target occurs in the array. The array may be empty. Return true if and only if there exists an index i with 0 ≤ i < n such that the element at position i equals the target; otherwise, return false. The function must not modify the input array.
fn contains_k(arr: &Vec<i32>, k: i32) -> (result: bool)

Given two sequences of 32-bit integers, determine whether every element in the second sequence is strictly smaller than the corresponding element in the first sequence. Assume the two sequences have the same length. Return true if and only if, for all valid indices, the element in the first sequence at that index is greater than the element in the second sequence at the same index; otherwise return false. The specification requires that the input sequences have equal length and defines the result to be the universal condition over all indices within the length. An empty pair of sequences (both of length zero) should return true as the condition holds vacuously.
fn is_smaller(arr1: &Vec<i32>, arr2: &Vec<i32>) -> (result: bool)

You are given a non-empty list of integer sequences. Assume every sequence contains at least two elements. Return the first element of a sequence whose second element is minimal among the second elements of all sequences. If multiple sequences share the same minimal second element, returning the first element from any one of those sequences is acceptable.
fn min_second_value_first(arr: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (first_of_min_second: i32)

Given a non-empty vector of vectors of 32-bit integers, return the length of the shortest inner vector.

Assumptions:
- The outer vector contains at least one inner vector (i.e., its length is strictly positive).

Requirements for the returned value:
- It is a non-negative integer (a vector length).
- It is less than or equal to the length of every inner vector in the outer vector.
- There exists at least one inner vector whose length is exactly equal to the returned value.

Inner vectors may be empty; in that case, the result can be 0.
fn smallest_list_length(list: &Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> usize

Implement a procedure that initializes an unsigned 64-bit accumulator to 5 and performs two consecutive counted loops. Each loop iterates while the loop index is strictly less than 10 and increases the accumulator by 1 per iteration. Before each loop, introduce a ghost variable defined as the current accumulator minus the loop index. Throughout each loop, maintain the following invariants: the loop index never exceeds 10; the ghost variable is strictly less than 100; and the ghost variable is equal to the accumulator minus the loop index at every step. Prove termination of each loop using a strictly decreasing variant 10 minus the loop index. After completing both loops, the procedure must assert that the accumulator equals 25. All arithmetic is performed over 64-bit unsigned integers.
fn choose_odd()

Given a mutable array of 32-bit unsigned integers, rearrange its elements in-place so that the final array is sorted in nondecreasing order. There are no preconditions on the array length; the input may be empty. After the function returns, the following must hold:
- Sortedness: For all indices i and j with 0 <= i < j < n (where n is the final length of the array), the element at index i is less than or equal to the element at index j.
- Reordering witness: There exists a sequence of indices r of length n such that: (a) for every position k, 0 <= r[k] < n, (b) all indices in r are pairwise distinct (i.e., r is a bijection over {0, ..., n-1}), and (c) for every position k, the element at position k in the final array equals the element at position r[k] in the original (pre-state) array. Equivalently, the final array is a permutation of the original array.
Duplicates are allowed and must be preserved, and the function must terminate.
fn test1(nums: &mut Vec<u32>)

You are given a mutable array of unsigned 32-bit integers and a size parameter. Assume the size parameter equals the array’s initial length and is at most 0x7FFF_FFFF. Modify the array in place by capping every element greater than 2 to the value 2, without changing the array’s length. After this capping step, compute and return the sum of all elements in the array. The returned sum must be at most twice the size parameter (i.e., sum ≤ 2 × size).
pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<u32>, N: u32) -> (sum: u32)
function_signature: pub fn myfun(a: &mut Vec<u32>, N: u32) -> (sum: u32)

description:
Given an array of unsigned 64-bit integers, assume the array contains at least one odd element. Return the index of some odd element. The returned index must be strictly less than the array length. If multiple odd elements exist, returning any one of them is acceptable.
fn choose_odd(v: &Vec<u64>) -> usize

You are given two arrays of unsigned 64-bit integers and two arrays of booleans, all of the same length. Assume the input length is consistent across all four arrays. Assume that for every index, at least one of the two corresponding booleans is true. Also assume that every integer in both integer arrays is strictly less than 1000. Construct an output array (provided by the caller and allowed to be overwritten) whose length equals the common input length, such that for each position:
- If both booleans at that position are true, the output value is the integer average of the two integers at that position, defined as (first value + second value) divided by 2 using integer division.
- If only the first boolean is true, the output value is the corresponding integer from the first array.
- If only the second boolean is true, the output value is the corresponding integer from the second array.
The function must produce an output array satisfying these rules for every index.
fn conditional_average(vals_1: &Vec<u64>, vals_2: &Vec<u64>, conds_1: &Vec<bool>, conds_2: &Vec<bool>, avgs: &mut Vec<u64>)
function_signature: fn conditional_average(vals_1: &Vec<u64>, vals_2: &Vec<u64>, conds_1: &Vec<bool>, conds_2: &Vec<bool>, avgs: &mut Vec<u64>)

description:
You are given an array of 32-bit integers. Return a new array with all duplicate values removed. The output must satisfy the following properties: (1) the output array contains no duplicates; (2) the set of elements in the output is equal to the set of elements in the input. There are no preconditions; the input array may be empty and may contain any integer values, including negatives and boundary values.

In addition to the executable function, formalize the relationship between sequences and sets used in the specification and verification:
- Define a pure, recursive specification function that converts a finite sequence to a set by repeatedly dropping the last element of the sequence and inserting that element into the set. The recursion must be well-founded with a decreasing measure on the sequence length.
- Prove that, for any element, membership in the original sequence is logically equivalent to membership in the recursively constructed set.
- Prove that the recursively defined set is equal to the standard set comprehension of the sequence.
- Prove that pushing an element onto a sequence corresponds to inserting that element into the set of the sequence, i.e., the set of s.push(x) equals the set of s with x inserted.

The implementation of the array function must ensure the postconditions described above: the result has no duplicates, and the set of result elements equals the set of input elements.
spec fn seq_to_set_rec<A>(seq: Seq<A>) -> Set<A>
proof fn seq_to_set_rec_contains<A>(seq: Seq<A>)
proof fn seq_to_set_equal_rec<A>(seq: Seq<A>)
proof fn lemma_seq_push_to_set_insert<T>(s: Seq<T>, val: T)
fn remove_duplicates(nums: Vec<i32>) -> (res: Vec<i32>)
function_signature:
spec fn seq_to_set_rec<A>(seq: Seq<A>) -> Set<A>
proof fn seq_to_set_rec_contains<A>(seq: Seq<A>)
proof fn seq_to_set_equal_rec<A>(seq: Seq<A>)
proof fn lemma_seq_push_to_set_insert<T>(s: Seq<T>, val: T)
fn remove_duplicates(nums: Vec<i32>) -> (res: Vec<i32>)

description:
Given an array of 64-bit unsigned integers and a separate output array, write a function that populates the output array with all elements from the input that are divisible by 3, in the same order as they appear. Assume the output array is initially empty. The function must not modify the input array. After the function completes, the output array must be exactly the subset of the input consisting of values divisible by 3, preserving their original order, and must contain no other elements.
pub fn myfun4(x: &Vec<u64>, y: &mut Vec<u64>)

Given a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers, return the maximum value present in the vector.

Assumptions:
- The input vector has positive length (it is non-empty).

Requirements for the result:
- The returned value is greater than or equal to every element in the input vector.
- The returned value is equal to at least one element in the input vector (i.e., it is an element of the vector).

Notes:
- The vector may contain duplicate values.
- The vector may contain negative and positive values, including the minimum and maximum 32-bit integers.
- The input must not be modified.
fn find_max(nums: Vec<i32>) -> (ret:i32)

You are given a mutable array of 32-bit unsigned integers. Assume the array may be empty and that every element in the array is initially exactly 1. You are also given a 32-bit unsigned integer a satisfying 10 < a < 20, and a boolean flag that is equal to false.

Modify the array in place by adding a to each element. After the operation completes, the following must hold:
- For every valid index in the array, the element at that index equals 1 + a.
- The arithmetic property 20 < 2·a < 40 holds.
- The negation of the input boolean flag is true.

All operations must be valid for 32-bit unsigned arithmetic under the given assumptions. The function returns no value and performs the transformation in place on the input array.
pub fn havoc_inline_post(v: &mut Vec<u32>, a: u32, b: bool)
function_signature: pub fn havoc_inline_post(v: &mut Vec<u32>, a: u32, b: bool)

description:
Implement a function that searches a list of 32-bit integers for a given target value using a linear scan and returns the index of the first occurrence. The return type is a 32-bit signed integer. Assume the input list length is strictly less than 2,147,483,648. The function must satisfy the following properties:
- If the target is present, return a non-negative index strictly less than the list length where the element equals the target, and no earlier position contains the target (i.e., the returned index is the first occurrence).
- If the target is absent, return a negative value; in this case, all positions in the list must be different from the target.
- In all cases, the return value must be strictly less than the list length (which is vacuously true when returning a negative value).
fn linear_search(nums: Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> (ret: i32)
function_signature: fn linear_search(nums: Vec<i32>, target: i32) -> (ret: i32)

description:
Implement an in-place transformation on a mutable array of 32-bit signed integers. For every valid index in the array, increase the element at that index by exactly 4. The array length must remain unchanged. Assume the input satisfies the following safety condition: every element is less than or equal to 2,147,483,643 (0x7FFF_FFFB), so that adding 4 to any element does not overflow the 32-bit signed integer range. Formally, for all indices k in [0, n), the output element equals the original element plus 4, and the size n is preserved.
pub fn myfun2(x: &mut Vec<i32>)

Given a non-empty array of 32-bit signed integers, return the index of a maximum element. Assume the input size is at least 1. The returned index must be within the array bounds, and the value at that index must be greater than or equal to every element in the array. If multiple elements have the maximum value, returning the index of any one of them is acceptable.
pub fn myfun1(x: &Vec<i32>) -> (max_index: usize)

Given an array of 32-bit integers and a threshold value, return a new array that contains only the elements from the input array that are less than or equal to the threshold. Assume the input array contains pairwise distinct elements (no duplicates). The result must satisfy both of the following: (1) every element in the output is less than or equal to the threshold and originates from the input array, and (2) every element of the input array that is less than or equal to the threshold appears in the output (at least once). No additional elements may be introduced. The order of elements in the output is not specified by the problem statement.
pub fn remove_all_greater(v: Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> Vec<i32>
function_signature: pub fn remove_all_greater(v: Vec<i32>, e: i32) -> Vec<i32>

description:
Given a mutable array (vector) of 64-bit unsigned integers, reverse the order of its elements in place. The function should not allocate an additional array and must modify the input directly. The length of the array must remain unchanged. On return, for every index i in the range [0, n), where n is the original length of the array, the element at index i must be equal to the element at index n − 1 − i from the original array. The function must handle all valid vectors, including empty arrays and arrays with one element.
fn reverse(v: &mut Vec<u64>)

Implement a function that computes the arithmetic series of the first n natural numbers and returns the exact result. The input is a non-negative 64-bit unsigned integer. Assume the resulting arithmetic sum is strictly less than 10,000. Formally define a specification function over natural numbers that represents the arithmetic sum S(k) = 1 + 2 + ... + k via a recursive definition on k with well-founded termination. Prove that this specification function is monotonic: for any natural numbers i and j with i ≤ j, it holds that S(i) ≤ S(j). Implement the computation using a loop that iterates up to n while maintaining the following properties at each step: the loop index never exceeds n, the accumulated partial sum equals S(index), and the precondition bound S(n) < 10,000 remains valid. The loop must terminate with a strictly decreasing measure. At each iteration, justify that adding the next term keeps the partial sum bounded by S(n) by appealing to the monotonicity of the specification function. On completion, the function must return a 64-bit unsigned integer equal to S(n).
spec fn arith_sum_int(i: nat) -> nat
proof fn lemma_arith_sum_monotonic(i: nat, j: nat)
fn compute_arith_sum(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)

Implement a tail-recursive procedure that computes the triangular number of a given non-negative 32-bit unsigned integer. The triangular number of an integer n is defined as the sum of all integers from 1 to n, with triangle(0) = 0. Provide a specification-only function that defines this value inductively over the natural numbers. Additionally, supply a proof establishing that the triangular number function is monotonic: if i ≤ j then triangle(i) ≤ triangle(j). The main procedure takes three inputs: a target non-negative integer, a current index, and a mutable accumulator. Assume the current index is less than or equal to the target. Also assume the accumulator initially holds the triangular number of the current index. Assume the triangular number of the target fits in a 32-bit unsigned integer (strictly less than 2^32), guaranteeing that all intermediate additions are free of overflow. The procedure must update the accumulator to the triangular number of the target and terminate, with its recursion measure strictly decreasing by the difference between the target and the current index on each step.
spec fn triangle(n: nat) -> nat
proof fn triangle_is_monotonic(i: nat, j: nat)
fn tail_triangle(n: u32, idx: u32, sum: &mut u32)

You are given an array of 64-bit unsigned integers and a target index. Assume the following:
- The array length is positive.
- The target index is strictly between 0 and the array length (exclusive), i.e., it is nonzero and less than the length.
- The array satisfies a global property: for every valid position k in the range 0 <= k < length, the element at position k equals k + 2.

Return the element at the given index. The result must equal both the array’s element at that position and the arithmetic value index + 2.
fn get_element_check_property(arr: Vec<u64>, i: usize) -> (ret: u64)
function_signature: fn get_element_check_property(arr: Vec<u64>, i: usize) -> (ret: u64)

description:
Implement a function that, given a sorted array of 32-bit integers in non-decreasing order and a target integer, returns the smallest index at which the target could be inserted while preserving the array’s order. Assume the array may be empty. The returned index must be between 0 and the length of the array, inclusive. Additionally, every element strictly before the returned index must be less than the target, and every element at or after the returned index must be greater than or equal to the target.
fn binary_search(a: &Vec<i32>, key: i32) -> (n: usize)

Given a positive 8-bit unsigned integer, compute its prime factorization using trial division. Return a vector containing the prime factors of the input in nondecreasing order. The product of all returned factors must equal the input value. Each factor must be a prime number in the standard sense: a natural number p is prime if there is no integer i with 1 < i < p such that p % i == 0. For the input value 1, return an empty vector (since 1 has no prime factors). Assume the input satisfies 1 <= value <= 255.
pub fn factorize(n: u8) -> (factorization: Vec<u8>)

You are given three integers. Each integer is guaranteed to be within the inclusive range [0, 50]. In one operation, you may perform exactly one of the following actions:
- Increase any single one of the three integers by 2.
- Increase any two distinct integers by 1 each.
Your task is to compute the minimum number of operations required to make all three integers equal. Assume all inputs are non-negative and within the specified bounds. The result must be a non-negative integer, and if the three integers are already equal, the minimum number of operations is 0.
fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, c: i8) -> i8
function_signature: fn solve(a: i8, b: i8, c: i8) -> i8

description:
You are given a one-dimensional array of 8-bit signed integers and a target matrix shape specified by an array of two unsigned bytes [R, C]. Construct a matrix of dimensions R × C by broadcasting the one-dimensional array along the compatible dimension.

Assumptions:
- The one-dimensional array has positive length.
- The shape array has length exactly 2.
- Both shape entries R and C are positive.
- The length of the one-dimensional array equals either R or C (at least one dimension must match the array length).

Output requirements:
- Return a well-formed R × C matrix (each of the R rows contains exactly C elements).
- The total number of elements in the matrix equals R × C.
- Broadcasting rule:
  - If R equals the array length, treat the array as a column vector. For each valid row index i and column index j, the matrix entry at (i, j) must equal the i-th element of the array (each element replicated across its entire row).
  - Otherwise, C equals the array length, and the array is treated as a row vector. For each valid row index i and column index j, the matrix entry at (i, j) must equal the j-th element of the array (the entire array duplicated for every row).

The result must satisfy the above mapping for all valid indices 0 ≤ i < R and 0 ≤ j < C.
fn broadcast(a: Vec<i8>, shape: Vec<u8>) -> (ret: Matrix<i8>)
function_signature: fn broadcast(a: Vec<i8>, shape: Vec<u8>) -> (ret: Matrix<i8>)

description:
Given an integer n, return the first n Fibonacci numbers as a vector of 32-bit signed integers. The Fibonacci sequence is defined with zero-based indexing: F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and for all i ≥ 2, F(i) = F(i − 1) + F(i − 2). Assume the input size is at least 2. Additionally, assume the mathematical Fibonacci number at index n is strictly less than 2^31; this guarantees that all requested outputs (up to index n − 1) fit into a 32-bit signed integer. The output vector must have length exactly n, and for every index i with 2 ≤ i < n, the element at position i must equal the mathematical Fibonacci number F(i).
fn fibonacci(n: usize) -> (ret: Vec<i32>)

You are given two integer arrays and a non-negative integer N. Assume both arrays have length exactly N and N does not exceed 0x3FFF_FFFF. The second array must satisfy, for every valid index i (0 <= i < N), that its element is either i or i + 1. The task is to update the first array in-place so that, for each index i, its element becomes 1 if the corresponding element in the second array equals i, and becomes 2 if it equals i + 1. Return the sum of the values written into the first array. Under these assumptions, the result is guaranteed to lie within the inclusive range [N, 2*N].
pub fn simple_nested(a: &mut Vec<i32>, b: &Vec<i32>, N: i32) -> (sum: i32)
function_signature: pub fn simple_nested(a: &mut Vec<i32>, b: &Vec<i32>, N: i32) -> (sum: i32)
